   GOV.UK: search “inform and consult your employees”.

2. Employee Forums, Works Councils or Joint Consultative Committees  
   • A voluntary group that meets regularly with management.  
   • Gives employees a structured voice on day-to-day issues (working practices, policies, wellbeing).  
   • Purely advisory – does not give shares and does not alter company control.

3. Trade Union Recognition and Collective Bargaining  
   • A recognised union negotiates pay and conditions on behalf of members.  
   • Employees gain influence through negotiation and, in some cases, union-nominated observers on the board.  
   • Ownership stays with existing shareholders.

4. Employee-elected Director(s) on the Board  
   • The company changes its articles to allow one or more worker directors.  
   • Directors have the same legal duties as any other board member.  
   • Having a worker director does not itself transfer shares, unless paired with an ownership plan.

5. Tax-advantaged Employee Share Schemes  
   a) Share Incentive Plan (SIP) – employees can receive free shares, buy partnership shares, or get matching shares.  
   b) Save As You Earn (SAYE) – staff save monthly for 3 or 5 years, then use the savings to buy shares at a fixed price.  
   c) Company Share Option Plan (CSOP) – selected employees are granted options to buy shares at a later date.  
   • These schemes give individual employees an ownership stake.  
   • Shares are usually small percentages, so day-to-day control of the business normally stays with existing owners unless you issue a large number.

6. Enterprise Management Incentives (EMI)  
   • A flexible share-option scheme for smaller companies.  
   • You can target key staff and tailor vesting conditions.  
   • Options convert into shares, giving those employees a stake; others remain non-owners unless included.

7. Employee Ownership Trust (EOT)  
   • A majority of the company’s shares (51% or more) are sold to a trust that holds them on behalf of all employees.  
   • Employees do not hold shares individually; the trust owns them collectively.  
   • Staff elect or appoint trustees and usually have a formal council or forum to influence trustee decisions and business strategy.  
   • Once the trust owns more than 50%, employees collectively control the company.

8. Direct Employee Buy-in / Co-operative Structure  
   • Employees buy shares directly, or the company issues new shares that existing staff purchase.  
   • One-member-one-vote or similar rules can be adopted, giving employees control in proportion to, or independent of, shareholding.  
   • Requires careful drafting of the articles of association and possibly a shareholder agreement.

Key points to think about

• Influence without ownership – consultation bodies, unions and worker directors can give employees a significant say while you keep full equity.  
• Ownership without day-to-day control – share schemes allow staff to build wealth and feel invested, but unless they obtain 50%+ of voting shares, strategic control stays with existing owners.  
• Ownership with collective control – moving to an Employee Ownership Trust or co-operative model means employees (collectively) own and control the business; founders normally step back or accept minority rights.

Next steps

1) Decide whether you want to transfer equity, influence only, or both.  
2) Check the tax and legal requirements for share schemes or trusts (GOV.UK guidance: “Set up an employee share scheme” and “Employee ownership trusts”).  
3) Talk to employees to understand which model they value.  
4) Take professional advice on company law, tax and valuation before issuing shares or altering articles.  
5) Put any new arrangements in writing and register changes at Companies House if required.

More information  
GOV.UK employing people hub: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people  
Employee share schemes: https://www.gov.uk/tax-employee-share-schemes  
Employee ownership trusts: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/employee-ownership-trusts

prompt: What are the key steps to prepare my business to employ staff?
generated_response:
Below is a simple checklist for getting your business ready to take on its first employee. The steps are the same across the UK, including Wales.

1. Work out the role and budget  
   • Decide the duties, hours and pay.  
   • Make sure the pay meets at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage.  
   • Check you can afford additional costs such as National Insurance, pension contributions, holiday and sick pay.

2. Register as an employer with HMRC  
   • You must do this before the first payday (it can take up to 2 weeks).  
   • You’ll get a PAYE reference number for running payroll.

3. Take out employers’ liability insurance  
   • At least £5 million cover is required as soon as you employ anyone, even part-time staff.

4. Check the person’s right to work in the UK  
   • Verify original documents or use the online Home Office checking service.  
   • Keep a copy as proof.

5. Decide how you’ll run payroll  
   • Choose payroll software or appoint a payroll provider.  
   • Each pay period you’ll report deductions (tax, National Insurance, student loans) to HMRC under Real Time Information (RTI).

6. Set up a workplace pension scheme  
   • Most employees must be put into (and contributed to) a pension if they are aged 22–State Pension age and earn at least £10,000 a year.  
   • You’ll receive a staging/start date from The Pensions Regulator.

7. Prepare the written terms of employment  
   • Give every employee a ‘written statement of employment particulars’ on or before their first day.  
   • Include key details such as pay, hours, holiday entitlement and notice periods.

8. Create essential policies and keep records  
   • Disciplinary and grievance procedures (a legal requirement).  
   • Health and safety arrangements appropriate to your workplace.  
   • Keep records of pay, hours worked, holidays, sickness and any accidents for at least 3 years.

9. Consider extra checks if the role demands them  
   • DBS checks for work with children or vulnerable adults.  
   • Professional registration or qualification checks where relevant.

10. Onboard your employee  
   • Collect starter information (P45 or starter checklist).  
   • Enrol them in payroll and, if eligible, the pension scheme.  
   • Provide any training, equipment and a safe working environment.

Useful starting point  
GOV.UK: Employing people – overview and detailed guides  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

If you need tailored guidance, contact Business Wales or a qualified accountant/payroll provider.

prompt: Can I get legal advice if I'm facing an employment tribunal claim?
generated_response:
Yes. You have several ways to get legal advice or representation if an employment tribunal claim has been made against you.

1. Check any insurance policies  
   • Some household or packaged bank-account policies include “legal expenses” cover.  
   • Phone the insurer’s helpline and ask if employment tribunal defence is covered and how to make a claim.

2. Contact your trade union (if you are a member)  
   • Unions usually provide free legal advice and may appoint a solicitor to act for you.

3. Speak to Acas early conciliation  
   • Acas offers impartial advice to both employers and employees and will explain the tribunal process.  
   • Call 0300 123 1100 or see the Acas “Responding to a claim” guidance on GOV.UK.

4. Free advice services  
   • Citizens Advice Scotland – free, confidential guidance on your rights and options.  
   • A local law centre or university legal clinic (where available) can give preliminary advice or, in some cases, representation.

5. Consult a solicitor  
   • Look for one who specialises in employment law. The Law Society of Scotland’s “Find a Solicitor” tool can help.  
   • Ask for an initial fixed-fee meeting and a clear quote for any further work.

6. Legal aid  
   • Legal aid for employment tribunals is limited but may be available for discrimination cases. A solicitor can tell you if you qualify.

7. Represent yourself  
   • If you decide to act without a lawyer, use the official guidance on GOV.UK and Acas to prepare your response and documents.  
   • You can still get one-off advice or document checks from a solicitor or advice centre even if you handle the hearing yourself.

Key GOV.UK pages  
Responding to an employment tribunal claim (includes Acas early conciliation): https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

If you are unsure which option is best, contact Citizens Advice Scotland or Acas first for free, impartial guidance.

prompt: I need to stop employing people in my business. What steps do I need to take to inform HMRC and close my PAYE scheme?
generated_response:
You can close your PAYE scheme at the same time as you run your last payroll. Follow these steps.

1. Run your final payroll  
   • Pay each employee what you owe them and give them a P45.  
   • In your payroll software mark this as the ‘final payroll’ and enter the leaving date for each employee.

2. Send the final Full Payment Submission (FPS)  
   • Tick ‘Yes’ for ‘Final submission for the year’.  
   • If the software has a ‘scheme ceased’ or ‘ceased trading’ box, enter the date you stopped employing staff.

3. Send any final Employer Payment Summary (EPS)  
   • Only if you need to reclaim statutory payments or declare no payments in the period.  
   • Mark it as the final EPS and include the same ceased date.

4. Pay any PAYE, National Insurance and other deductions that are still due by the normal payment deadline (usually the 22nd of the next month if you pay electronically).

5. Keep your payroll records for at least 3 full tax years after the year you stopped employing people.

6. Wait for HMRC confirmation  
   • HMRC will automatically close the scheme once they process your final submissions and payments.  
   • Contact the employer helpline if you keep getting notices after 4 weeks.

If you decide to hire staff again later, you’ll need to open a new PAYE scheme or ask HMRC to reactivate the old one.

More guidance is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I'm a reservist in Scotland and my employer has asked about my training schedule. What should I tell them about the time off for training and how does it affect my job?
generated_response:
Reservist training – what your employer needs to know

1. Typical commitment  
   • 1 evening drill night a week (about 2–3 hours).  
   • 1 or 2 training weekends a month.  
   • An annual camp of around 14 consecutive days once a year.  
   Units can vary, so give your employer your exact dates as soon as your training diary is issued (usually 6–12 months ahead).

2. Time-off rules  
   • There is no statutory right to paid time off for reservist training.  
   • You can ask for:  
     – paid special leave (if it’s part of your employer’s policy),  
     – unpaid leave, or  
     – to use your normal annual leave.  
   • Most employers decide case-by-case; the Ministry of Defence encourages them to be flexible and gives guidance.

3. Protecting your job  
   • It is against the law to dismiss or treat you unfavourably because you are a reservist.  
   • If you are mobilised for active service, you have separate legal rights to be reinstated afterwards under the Reserve Forces (Safeguard of Employment) Act 1985.  
   • For routine training, the usual workplace policies apply, but you should not be penalised for asking for reasonable time off.

4. Good practice when talking to your employer  
   1) Give the full training calendar and flag any dates that affect term time early.  
   2) Explain whether you are requesting paid special leave, unpaid leave or using annual leave.  
   3) Offer to minimise disruption (for example, swapping duties or arranging cover).  
   4) Keep the conversation open—update them if dates change.

If your employer wants official guidance, signpost them to:  
Employing a reservist (GOV.UK) – https://www.gov.uk/employee-reservist

Still unsure? Check your unit’s employer support officer or the Defence Relationship Management helpline for tailored advice.

prompt: I need to check a job applicant's right to work in the UK. Do I need to use their share code, or can I check their original documents instead?
generated_response:
You have two ways to carry out a legal ‘right to work’ check. Which one you must use depends on the type of immigration status the applicant holds.

1. Ask the applicant how their immigration status is held.  
   • If it is kept only in a Home Office online record (for example: an eVisa, settled or pre-settled status, a biometric residence permit/card issued after 6 April 2022, a Frontier Worker permit), you must use the online service with their share code. Original documents are not valid for these digital statuses.  
   • If they have an acceptable physical document (for example: a British or Irish passport, a UK birth certificate plus evidence of National Insurance number, or an older biometric residence permit/card), you can do a manual check instead. A share code is optional in these cases.

2. How to do an online check  
   a. Ask the applicant for their share code and date of birth.  
   b. Go to ‘View a job applicant’s right to work details’ on GOV.UK and enter the details.  
   c. Print or save the profile page showing their photo and permission to work, and note the date you viewed it.

3. How to do a manual (original-document) check  
   a. See Lists A and B in the Home Office guidance for the exact documents you can accept.  
   b. Check the document in the applicant’s presence. Make sure the photo matches them and the document is genuine.  
   c. Take a clear copy (or scan) of every page you need, record the date you made the check, and keep the copy securely for the length of employment plus two years.

4. If the applicant has an outstanding Home Office application or appeal and cannot provide either route, use the Employers Checking Service and keep the Positive Verification Notice you receive.

Doing the correct check and keeping the evidence gives you a statutory excuse against any civil penalty.

Further guidance: GOV.UK – ‘Check a job applicant’s right to work’ (https://www.gov.uk/check-job-applicant-right-to-work).

prompt: I'm a student in Scotland and I want to understand my contract and working hours. How do I find information about my rights and responsibilities regarding my working hours and contract terms?
generated_response:
You can check your rights and duties in three main places.

1. Read your own contract or written ‘statement of employment particulars’. Employers must give this in writing. It should set out:
   • job title and duties   
   • pay and how often you’re paid  
   • normal working hours, breaks and holiday  
   • notice periods and any probation

2. Use the GOV.UK guidance  
   • Employment contracts: explains what must be in a contract, changes to terms and zero-hours arrangements.  
   • Working hours: covers the 48-hour average limit, opting out, overtime, rest breaks and paid holiday.  
   • Holiday entitlement calculator: helps you check the days you should get.  

   Start at: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people and follow the links to ‘Contracts’ and ‘Working hours’.

3. Get one-to-one advice if anything is unclear  
   • Call the Acas helpline on 0300 123 1100 (free, confidential).  
   • Speak to your students’ union, Citizens Advice Scotland or an employment adviser at your college/university.

Keep copies of any letters or emails you send or receive about changes to your hours or terms. If your employer is not following the rules, Acas can tell you the next steps, including informal resolution or raising a formal grievance.

prompt: What are the conditions for applying for an injunction to stop industrial action that affects my business?
generated_response:
You can only ask a court to stop (or ‘interdict’) planned or ongoing industrial action if you can show that the action is unlawful.  In practice, the court will grant an injunction/interdict only when all the conditions below are met.

1. The industrial action is not protected by law  
   Examples include:  
   • no lawful ballot or an invalid ballot (wrong electorate, poor turnout calculation, inaccurate ballot paper wording).  
   • the union failed to give you at least 14 days’ written notice of the dates and types of action (7 days if you both agree).  
   • secondary or sympathy action – workers striking in support of a dispute that is not with their own employer.  
   • action over a political issue rather than a genuine trade dispute.  

2. You act promptly  
   Apply as soon as you become aware of the defect.  Delay can make it harder to get urgent relief.

3. You have evidence  
   Collect copies of the ballot paper, notices sent to you, any communications to members, and details of the harm the action is causing or is likely to cause.

4. The balance of convenience favours an injunction  
   The court will weigh the damage to your business if the action continues against the restriction on the union and workers if it is stopped.

5. You give a legal undertaking  
   If the court later decides the injunction should not have been granted, you must be able to pay damages the union or workers suffer because of it.

How to apply

1. Send a letter before action to the union setting out why you believe the action is unlawful and asking them to withdraw it immediately.  
2. If they refuse or do not reply, instruct a solicitor experienced in employment or commercial litigation.  
3. In Scotland, apply to the Court of Session (or the sheriff court in some cases) for an interdict; elsewhere in the UK you apply to the High Court for an injunction.  
4. Prepare the claim form, witness statement and draft order.  These must show the unlawful elements and the urgency.  
5. The court will usually list an urgent hearing (sometimes the same or next day).  You must serve the papers on the union and attend the hearing with evidence.  
6. If the order is granted, serve it on the union as directed.  Breaching the order can lead to contempt of court proceedings against the union.

If you are unsure whether the legal tests are met, get specialist legal advice before applying.  Incorrect applications can be costly.

Further information  
GOV.UK – Employing people (general guidance): https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I work in a retail store and am an agency worker. Can I take time off for training?
generated_response:
Agency workers do not have the statutory right to ask for unpaid ‘time to train’ in the way that permanent employees can. The legal right under the Time to Train rules (Employment Rights Act 1996, sections 63D-63R) only applies to employees in organisations with 250 or more staff and specifically excludes agency workers.

What you can do instead:

1. Check your contract or assignment terms. Some agencies or hirers include paid or unpaid training opportunities in their agreements.  
2. Ask your agency consultant if the hirer will release you for training, and whether the hours will be paid. They can agree, but they are not legally obliged to.  
3. If the training is essential for the role (for example, health and safety or legally required product training), the hirer or agency should arrange and pay for it during working time.  
4. If it is optional development, you may need to take annual leave, unpaid leave or do the course outside working hours.  
5. Consider other funded routes such as Skills Bootcamps or adult learning courses that run outside your shifts.

If you are unsure what your contract allows, ask the agency for a written explanation. You can also call Acas (0300 123 1100) for free, confidential advice on agency worker rights.

prompt: Can I employ a 12-year-old for a summer job in my shop, such as helping with customer service or stocking shelves?
I recently left my job and am trying to find a new one. Can I ask my previous employer for a reference, and what should I do if they refuse?
generated_response:
You can always ask a previous employer for a reference, but in most jobs they do not have to give one. Here is what to do:

1. Make a polite written request to the HR department or your former line manager. Say what role you are applying for and when the reference is needed.

2. If they refuse:
   • Ask whether they can at least confirm your job title and the dates you worked there (a ‘basic’ or ‘employment-only’ reference).  
   • Approach another person who supervised you—such as a senior colleague or store manager—if company policy allows personal references.  
   • Offer alternative referees to the new employer (for example, another past employer, a client, or a professional acquaintance).

3. If you think the refusal is unfairly damaging your job search:
   • Check the employer’s written reference policy (often in the staff handbook).  
   • Raise the issue informally first, then make a formal complaint (grievance) if you left very recently and are still within the company’s grievance-raising period.  
   • For regulated roles (financial services, childcare, certain care roles), the employer may be obliged to provide a reference—mention this if it applies to you.

4. If you receive a reference you believe is misleading or incorrect, you can:
   • Ask the employer to amend it.  
   • Provide evidence (such as appraisals or performance records).  
   • Consider legal advice if the reference is factually wrong and has cost you a job offer (this could be defamation or negligent misstatement).

5. Keep copies of all correspondence and any written policies in case you need them later.

Further guidance on employment rights is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I heard that a union can be derecognised if the number of workers in the bargaining unit drops below 50%. How do I know if this applies to my workplace and what should I do next?
generated_response:
The 50% figure is part of the statutory rules on union recognition and derecognition in the UK. Here’s how to work out whether it affects your workplace and what you can do.

1. Check how the union was recognised  
   • Ask your union rep or HR whether the agreement is statutory (decided by the Central Arbitration Committee, the CAC) or voluntary (agreed directly with the employer).  
   • The 50% membership rule only applies to statutory recognition.

2. Identify the “bargaining unit”  
   • The bargaining unit is the specific group of workers covered by the recognition agreement (for example, all shop-floor staff in one store).  
   • Make sure you know whether you are in that group.

3. Find out current union membership levels  
   • The union can usually tell you how many members it has in the bargaining unit.  
   • If fewer than half the workers in the unit are union members, the employer (or at least 10 % of the workers) can apply to the CAC to derecognise the union once the agreement has been in place for at least three years.

4. Understand the derecognition process  
   • The CAC will first check whether membership is below 50 %.  
   • If it is, the CAC normally orders a secret ballot.  
   • For derecognition to succeed, both of these must be true:  
     – A majority of those who vote say “yes” to derecognition, and  
     – At least 40 % of everyone in the bargaining unit votes “yes”.  
   • If those thresholds are not met, the union stays recognised.

5. Decide what you want to do  
   • If you support the union, talk to colleagues about joining—it is the simplest way to keep membership above 50 %.  
   • If you are unsure, ask the union or ACAS for impartial guidance on what recognition means for pay and conditions.  
   • If you favour derecognition, gather facts first and consider free advice from ACAS before taking any formal step.

6. Get advice early  
   • ACAS helpline (0300 123 1100) can explain the rules in plain language.  
   • Full statutory guidance is on GOV.UK under “Trade union recognition and derecognition”: https://www.gov.uk/trade-union-recognition

If anything is unclear—especially the size of the bargaining unit or current membership—ask your union representative or HR in writing. That written record will help if the issue ever goes to the CAC.

prompt: I'm looking after my child and want to take some time off work to focus on their needs. How do I apply for unpaid parental leave and what rights do I have while I'm away?
generated_response:
Unpaid parental leave lets you take time off work to care for your child without losing your job.

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • You must be an employee (not self-employed or a worker on a short contract).  
   • You need at least 1 year’s continuous service with your employer.  
   • The child must be under 18 on the date the leave starts.  
   • You can take up to 18 weeks of unpaid parental leave for each child in total, but no more than 4 weeks of that in any one leave year.

2. Work out how much leave you want to take  
   • Leave is normally taken in whole weeks (for example, 1, 2, 3 or 4 weeks at a time).  
   • If your child gets Disability Living Allowance or Personal Independence Payment, or the employer agrees, you can take it in single days.

3. Give your employer notice in writing  
   • Tell them at least 21 days before you want the leave to start.  
   • Include the dates you want to begin and end the leave and state it is “statutory unpaid parental leave”.  
   • Keep a copy of the request.

4. Wait for your employer’s response  
   • They can postpone the leave for up to 6 months if the business would be seriously disrupted, but they must:  
     – discuss it with you, and  
     – confirm the new dates in writing within 7 days of your request.  
   • They cannot refuse the leave altogether if you meet the rules.

Your rights while you’re away  
• Job security – you are entitled to return to the same job (or a suitable alternative on no worse terms if the leave is more than 4 weeks).  
• Pay and conditions – length of service, pension rights and statutory holiday continue to build up.  
• Protection from unfair treatment – you must not be dismissed or treated badly for taking or asking for parental leave.  
• Pay – the leave is unpaid unless your contract gives extra pay.

If you need more than 4 weeks at once, talk to your employer: some agree to longer blocks or a mix of other leave (annual leave, parental bereavement leave, flexible working). If you are unsure about your employer’s policy, ask HR for the written parental-leave policy.

Further information: GOV.UK guidance on unpaid parental leave – go to gov.uk and search “parental leave” or start at https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people.

prompt: I was dismissed from my job without proper notice. How can I find out if I am owed any compensation or if I can challenge the decision?
generated_response:
Being dismissed without proper notice may give you several potential claims, but you need to check exactly what happened and act quickly.

1. Confirm your employment status and service length  
   • You must be an “employee” (not self-employed or agency) to claim unfair dismissal or notice pay.  
   • For ordinary unfair dismissal you usually need at least 2 years’ continuous service, unless the reason is “automatically unfair” (for example pregnancy, whistleblowing, trade-union activity or asserting a statutory right).  

2. Work out the notice pay you should have had  
   • Statutory minimum notice is 1 week if you’ve worked 1 month–2 years, then 1 extra week for each full year up to 12 weeks.  
   • Your contract may give longer notice.  
   • If you were told to leave immediately, you should normally receive pay in lieu of that notice.  

3. Check other payments owed  
   • Outstanding wages, overtime or commission already earned.  
   • Accrued but untaken holiday.  
   • Statutory redundancy pay (only if you were dismissed for redundancy and have 2 years’ service).  

4. Ask your employer for a written breakdown  
   • Put your request in writing (email is fine).  
   • State that you believe you were not given proper notice and ask for:  
     – the reason for dismissal  
     – the notice period applied  
     – any payments they intend to make.  

5. Try to resolve it informally or through your employer’s grievance procedure  
   • Keep copies of all correspondence.  
   • If you reach agreement, get it confirmed in writing and check the payment date.  

6. If that fails, start Acas Early Conciliation quickly  
   • You must begin Early Conciliation within 3 months less 1 day of the effective dismissal date to keep your right to a tribunal claim.  
   • It’s free and compulsory before you can lodge a claim.  
   • Acas will try to broker a settlement; if none is reached, they give you a certificate so you can proceed.  
   Link: https://www.gov.uk/acas-early-conciliation  

7. Employment Tribunal claim (if necessary)  
   • Submit the claim online using the certificate number.  
   • Possible awards include:  
     – basic award (similar to redundancy pay)  
     – compensatory award for lost earnings and benefits  
     – unpaid notice, holiday or wages.  

8. Get advice if unsure  
   • Employment law is detailed and deadlines are strict.  
   • Consider speaking to:  
     – Acas helpline (0300 123 1100)  
     – a trade union (if you are a member)  
     – a local advice centre or solicitor specialising in employment law.  

If you are close to the 3-month deadline, start Acas Early Conciliation straight away to protect your position, even if you are still gathering evidence.

prompt: I need to check an employee's immigration status because they don't have their documents. What steps should I follow to use the Employer Checking Service and avoid fines?
generated_response:
If a worker cannot show you acceptable right-to-work documents, you must use the Home Office Employer Checking Service (ECS). Doing this correctly gives you a “statutory excuse”, which protects you from civil penalties if it later turns out the person is working illegally.

Follow these steps:

1. Confirm you can use the ECS  
   • Use it only when the worker cannot show documents because, for example, they have an outstanding visa application or appeal, their documents are with the Home Office, or they have digital status but cannot provide a share code.  

2. Collect the information you will need  
   • Worker’s full name, date of birth and nationality.  
   • Any Home Office reference number they have (for example, a visa, case-work or ARC reference).  
   • Your business details and the job start date.

3. Make the online check  
   • Go to the Employer Checking Service request form on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/employee-immigration-employment-status  
   • Fill in the form with the details above and submit.  
   • You will get a confirmation page and reference number – keep this for your records.

4. Wait for the Home Office reply  
   • You should receive an email reply within 5 working days.  
   • If the result is a Positive Verification Notice (PVN), keep it. It protects you from fines for 6 months from the notice date.  
   • If you get a Negative Verification Notice or no reply after 5 working days, contact the ECS helpline or delay employing the person until their status is proven.

5. Keep records and diarise a follow-up check  
   • Keep copies of the PVN and the reference number securely for at least 2 years after employment ends.  
   • If the PVN is time-limited (usually 6 months), repeat the ECS check before it expires.

6. Check existing staff in the same way  
   • If a current employee’s permission to work is time-limited and they cannot show their updated status, submit a fresh ECS request before the old PVN runs out.

Extra help  
• Employer Checking Service helpline: 0300 790 6268 (Mon–Fri, 9 am–4:45 pm).  
• Penalties for employing illegal workers can be up to £20,000 per worker, so keep clear records of every check.

If you are unsure at any stage, call the helpline or read the “Employing people: right to work checks” guidance on GOV.UK.

prompt: I need to know what steps I should take if my partner's baby is in neonatal care and I want to take leave and get pay. How do I apply for this and what do I need to do to ensure I get the right amount of leave and pay?
generated_response:
If your partner’s baby has to stay in neonatal care, you can use existing family-leave rights straight away. A new statutory “Neonatal Leave and Pay” scheme has been passed but is not expected to start until at least April 2025, so for now you need to rely on paternity leave and any other leave your employer offers.

1. Tell your employer or HR team as soon as you can  
   • Explain that the baby is in neonatal care and give the expected dates you want to be off.  
   • Ask whether your workplace has its own neonatal, compassionate or emergency leave policy (many schools and local authorities do).  

2. Statutory Paternity Leave (SPL)  
   • You can take 1 or 2 weeks if you have worked for the same employer for at least 26 weeks by the 15th week before the baby was due.  
   • The leave must be taken in one block and normally within 56 days of the birth, but you can delay it if the baby is still in hospital—up to 8 weeks after the baby comes home.  
   • Give at least 28 days’ notice using form SC3 (your employer may have an internal form).  

3. Statutory Paternity Pay  
   • Paid at £172.48 a week (2024-25 rate) or 90 % of average weekly earnings if lower.  
   • You qualify if you earn at least £123 a week on average before tax.  
   • Your employer pays it through payroll; provide any proof they ask for (for example, the baby’s MATB1 or a hospital letter).  

4. Shared Parental Leave and Pay (optional)  
   • If your partner chooses to end her maternity leave early, you can share up to 50 weeks of leave and up to 37 weeks of pay between you.  
   • Both parents complete a “curtailment” notice and SPL forms; give at least 8 weeks’ notice.  
   • This can give you more time off while the baby is in hospital.  

5. Unpaid time off for dependants or parental leave  
   • You are entitled to reasonable unpaid time off for an emergency involving a dependant.  
   • You can also take up to 18 weeks of unpaid parental leave per child (maximum 4 weeks a year, taken in whole weeks) if you have a year’s service.  

6. Annual leave or sick leave  
   • You can ask to use paid annual leave instead of, or to top up, statutory leave.  
   • If stress or exhaustion is affecting you, you can speak to your GP about sick leave—but sick pay rules will apply.  

7. Keep records  
   • Save copies of all forms, emails and any hospital letters confirming the baby’s stay.  
   • Check each payslip to make sure the correct Statutory Paternity Pay or Shared Parental Pay has been applied.  

8. If there is a problem  
   • Raise it first with your HR or payroll team.  
   • If unresolved, you can contact Acas for free workplace advice on 0300 123 1100.  

More detailed guidance is on GOV.UK under Employing People (family-friendly leave and pay):  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: What should I do if I receive a claim against me and conciliation hasn't worked?
generated_response:
If Acas early conciliation has ended without a settlement and you have now been sent an employment tribunal claim (form ET1), act quickly. You normally have 28 days from the date on the tribunal’s notice to respond.

1. Read the ET1 form and the tribunal cover letter straight away. Note the “response required by” date. Missing it can lead to a default judgment against you.  
2. Gather any documents that relate to the issues in the claim (contracts, emails, wage slips, policies, meeting notes). Keep them in date order and back them up.  
3. Complete the response form (ET3).  
   • You can do this online or download the form from GOV.UK.  
   • Explain which parts of the claim you agree with, dispute, or do not understand, and give a clear outline of your version of events.  
   • Attach a schedule of documents if you already have one.  
4. Send the ET3 to the tribunal office before the deadline. Keep proof of submission (email receipt or recorded-delivery slip).  
5. Keep talking to Acas if you are open to settling. Conciliation formally ends when the tribunal proceedings begin, but Acas can still help both sides reach a settlement (a ‘COT3’) at any time before or during the hearing.  
6. Start preparing your case:  
   • List the key facts you will rely on.  
   • Identify any witnesses and ask them for statements.  
   • Put together a chronological “bundle” of the documents both sides will need.  
7. Watch for tribunal directions. The judge may order you to exchange witness statements, a joint document bundle or a schedule of loss by set dates. Missing these can harm your case.  
8. Consider getting advice. Employment law can be complex, so you may want to speak to:  
   • a solicitor or regulated adviser  
   • your trade organisation or HR consultancy (if you have one)  
   • Citizens Advice or a law centre if you need free or lower-cost help  
9. Attend any preliminary hearing (often by phone or video) and the final hearing, or arrange for someone to represent you. Bring all required papers and witnesses.

If you are unsure about any step, check the detailed guidance on GOV.UK: Responding to an employment tribunal claim (search “ET3 response GOV.UK”) or contact the tribunal office named in your paperwork.

prompt: I've tried talking to my manager about a problem at work, but nothing has been resolved. How do I formally raise a grievance and what happens next?
generated_response:
You have the right to raise a formal grievance if talking informally has not fixed the problem.

1. Check your employer’s grievance policy  
   • It should tell you who to write to, any time limits and how the process works.  
   • If you cannot find it, ask HR or look in your staff handbook or intranet.

2. Put your grievance in writing  
   • Clearly head it “Formal Grievance”.  
   • Set out the facts in date order, explain how it affects you and say what outcome you want (for example an apology, training, or a change in working practices).  
   • Send it to the person named in the policy (often HR or a senior manager). Keep a copy.

3. Your employer should invite you to a meeting  
   • You have the right to be accompanied by a colleague or trade-union representative.  
   • Take notes and any evidence (emails, rotas, witness names).

4. Employer investigates and gives a written decision  
   • They should explain what they found and any action they will take.  
   • There is no set legal deadline, but they must act reasonably and without undue delay.

5. If you disagree, you can appeal  
   • Write to say why you think the decision is wrong and what you want changed.  
   • The appeal should be heard by someone not previously involved, where possible.

6. Still not resolved?  
   • Try mediation or ask your union for further help.  
   • Before making an employment tribunal claim you must contact Acas for ‘early conciliation’. This is free and compulsory.

Useful link  
GOV.UK guidance on workplace grievances and your rights: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

If you are unsure about any step, speak to your union (if you have one) or call the Acas helpline on 0300 123 1100 for free advice.

prompt: I'm a retired person in Wales and I want to know how to report my employer for not paying me the correct National Minimum Wage. What steps should I take?
generated_response:
You can complain in confidence to HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) if an employer has paid you less than the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage.

1. Check the current rates and what you should have been paid. You can use the minimum wage calculator on GOV.UK or compare your hourly rate with the legal rate for the dates you worked.

2. Gather evidence such as:
   • recent payslips  
   • your contract or written statement of employment  
   • any time-keeping records you hold (for example, rota, diary, clock-in records)  

3. Decide whether to raise the issue with the employer first. You do not have to, but if you feel comfortable it can sometimes resolve things quickly.

4. Make your complaint to HMRC using the online Pay and Work Rights complaint form or by phoning the Acas helpline on 0300 123 1100 (they take the details for HMRC).  
   • You can remain anonymous, but giving contact details helps HMRC investigate and keep you informed.  
   • Say that the issue is underpayment of the National Minimum Wage/National Living Wage and give the dates, hours worked and what you were paid.

5. HMRC will review the information, contact the employer if they open an investigation, and can require them to:  
   • pay any arrears owed to you (going back up to six years)  
   • pay a financial penalty to the government  

6. If you still have unpaid wages after HMRC’s process, you can also bring a claim to an Employment Tribunal, but you must start this within 3 months minus 1 day of the last underpayment.

7. For free, confidential advice at any stage, contact:  
   • Acas (0300 123 1100)  
   • Citizens Advice Cymru  

Further guidance and links to the complaint form are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I'm managing an employee in Scotland who has taken multiple periods of sickness. How do I know if these periods are considered linked and if I need to calculate SSP manually?
generated_response:
A “linked” sickness period is important because it affects when Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) starts and whether you need to work it out yourself.

1. Work out if each absence is a Period of Incapacity for Work (PIW).  
   • A PIW is 4 or more calendar days in a row when the employee is sick – weekends and bank holidays count.  
   • Shorter absences (1–3 days) do not count.

2. Check the 8-week (56-day) rule.  
   • Two PIWs are linked if there are 8 weeks or less between the last sick day of one PIW and the first sick day of the next PIW.  
   • Keep linking each new PIW that meets this rule. After a continuous 3-year run of linked PIWs, the chain ends and a new one starts.

3. What linking means for SSP.  
   • The 3 waiting days (unpaid “qualifying days”) are only served once at the start of the first PIW in a linked chain.  
   • From then on, SSP is due from the first qualifying day of each later PIW in the chain.  
   • Earnings and length-of-service tests are applied only at the start of the first PIW.

4. Do you need to calculate SSP manually?  
   Most payroll software can handle linked PIWs if you enter the correct start and end dates for each spell. You usually only need to do a manual calculation if:  
   • your software cannot link the periods, or  
   • the linked absences run past the 3-year limit (you would then treat the next PIW as a fresh claim with new waiting days).  

5. How to calculate SSP yourself (if required).  
   a) Work out the employee’s average weekly earnings over the 8 weeks before the first PIW in the chain – they must earn at least £123 a week (2023/24 rate).  
   b) Identify the qualifying days (the days they normally work).  
   c) SSP is paid at £109.40 a week (2023/24 rate), pro-rated for qualifying days.  
   d) Start paying from the 4th qualifying day of the first PIW, or from day 1 of later linked PIWs.  
   e) Keep paying up to a maximum of 28 paid weeks in the linked chain.

If you are unsure whether your software is treating the periods as linked, check the absence report it produces or contact the software provider. For detailed rules see the Employer’s guide to Statutory Sick Pay on GOV.UK:

https://www.gov.uk/employers-sick-pay

prompt: I'm a teacher and I want to work part-time. How do I legally request a change to my working hours and what should I expect from my employer?
generated_response:
You have a legal right to make a “statutory flexible working request” if you have worked for the school (or academy trust) for at least 26 weeks. A request to go part-time counts as flexible working.

How to make the request  
1. Read the school’s flexible-working or staffing policy so you know who to write to and any internal deadlines.  
2. Write a dated letter or email that clearly says it is a “statutory flexible working request”. Include:  
   • the change you want (for example, three days a week, or shorter days)  
   • the date you would like it to start (normally the next academic year or term)  
   • how you think the change might affect the school and how any impact could be managed (for example, timetable adjustments, job-share, use of cover supervisors).  
3. Send the request to the headteacher or HR lead and keep a copy.

What the employer must do  
• Meet with you (unless they agree the request straight away).  
• Consider the request reasonably and give you a written decision within three months, unless you both agree more time.  
• Approve the request, offer an alternative you can accept, or refuse it for one (or more) of the eight business reasons set out in the law (for example, inability to reorganise work or detrimental effect on pupils).  
• Offer an appeal process if they refuse.

If the request is approved  
• You’ll get a written change to your contract showing your new hours, pay (pro-rata), pension contributions and any change to duties such as meeting times.  
• Agree when the new pattern starts and whether there will be a review period.

If the request is refused  
• You can appeal internally, pointing out any factual errors or solutions not considered.  
• If you still think the decision was not handled correctly, you can raise a grievance or take the case to Acas early conciliation and, if needed, an employment tribunal. The tribunal can look at whether the school followed the correct procedure and used valid reasons, rather than whether part-time work is desirable.

Extra tips  
• Submit the request well before timetabling for the next academic year to give the school time to plan cover.  
• Speak informally to your head or department lead first—mutual planning often helps.  
• Joining a job-share with another teacher can make approval easier.  
• Your union (if you are a member) can review your draft request or attend meetings with you.

Official guidance  
Full rules and template letters are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/flexible-working

prompt: How do I calculate my holiday entitlement if I work part-time and irregular hours?
generated_response:
In the UK you are legally entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday a year. How you work out those 5.6 weeks depends on your pattern of work.

1. If you work the same part-time hours every week  
   • Count how many days (or hours) you normally work in a week.  
   • Multiply that by 5.6.  
   Example: you work 3 days a week. 3 × 5.6 = 16.8 days’ paid holiday a year.

2. If your hours or days change from week to week (irregular or casual work)  
   • Holiday usually builds up (accrues) as you work.  
   • A simple way many employers use is: 12.07% of the hours you actually work.  
     – 12.07% comes from 5.6 weeks’ holiday ÷ 46.4 working weeks.  
   • Add up the hours you have worked in the period you want to check (for example, a month).  
   • Multiply the total hours by 0.1207 (12.07%).  
   Example: you worked 60 hours this month. 60 × 0.1207 ≈ 7.24 hours of paid holiday earned.

3. Taking the holiday  
   • You request time off in the normal way your employer sets (for example a holiday form or rota request).  
   • When you take leave, you are paid for the number of hours or days you would have worked.  
   • For irregular hours, your holiday pay is based on your average weekly pay over the last 52 paid weeks.

4. If you start or leave part-way through the year  
   • Holiday accrues from your first day of work at roughly 12.07% of the hours you do.  
   • You are entitled to be paid for any untaken holiday when you leave.

If your contract gives more than the legal minimum, use the higher figure in the same way.

Still unsure?  
• Ask your employer to confirm how they calculate holiday for part-time or casual staff (they must keep a record).  
• See the holiday entitlement guidance on GOV.UK for full details: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people  
• You can also get free advice from Acas on 0300 123 1100.

prompt: I'm thinking about buying a small business, but I'm worried about the employees. How can I know if their jobs will stay the same if the business changes hands?
generated_response:
In most business sales the UK rules called TUPE (Transfer of Undertakings - Protection of Employment) apply. If they do, the employees move to you on the same contracts and service dates they had with the previous owner.

1. Check whether TUPE is likely to apply  
   • It covers sales of a business (or part of one) that continues to trade in a similar way.  
   • It does not usually apply to a share purchase (because the employer stays the same) or to one-off purchases of assets only.

2. Ask the current owner, in writing, for the “employee liability information”  
   They must give you this at least 28 days before the hand-over. It includes each employee’s name, age, terms and conditions, length of service, any disciplinary or grievance cases and any outstanding claims.

3. Inform and consult staff (or their elected representatives)  
   You must tell them:  
   • that the transfer is happening, when and why  
   • whether you plan any changes (known as “measures”) after the transfer  
   • how the transfer affects their jobs, pay, holidays, pension and any collective agreements

4. Understand what you can—and cannot—change  
   • Contracts, pay, hours, holiday entitlement and continuous service move over intact.  
   • You cannot dismiss staff or change terms just because of the transfer; doing so is automatically unfair.  
   • Changes are only allowed for a genuine economic, technical or organisational reason that requires alterations in the workforce, and you must follow a fair process.

5. Plan early if restructuring might be needed  
   If you foresee redundancies or other changes, take legal advice first and carry out separate consultation after the transfer, showing the valid business reason.

6. Keep written records  
   Keep copies of the liability information, consultation notes and any agreements reached with staff or their representatives.

If you are unsure whether TUPE applies or how to handle consultation, get specialist legal or HR advice before the sale completes.

Further guidance: GOV.UK – Employing people (includes the TUPE section)  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I'm a nurse looking to hire a part-time caregiver for elderly patients. How do I ensure that the caregiver is being paid correctly according to the National Minimum Wage for different types of work?
generated_response:
To stay within the National Minimum Wage rules you need to:

1. Check the current rates  
   • Rates change every April. Look up the latest National Minimum Wage (NMW) and National Living Wage (NLW) figures on GOV.UK before you agree pay.

2. Work out which type of work the caregiver will do  
   The rules split work into four categories. Most home-care jobs fall into one of these:  
   • Time work – paid by the hour worked (the usual case).  
   • Salaried hours work – paid an annual salary for a set number of hours.  
   • Output work – paid by the piece (rare in care).  
   • Unmeasured work – hours vary and are not timed (you agree a “realistic average” of hours in writing).  
   Decide the category first; it affects how you calculate the wage.

3. Record every hour that counts for NMW  
   For a caregiver this normally includes:  
   • The time they are actually caring, even if the patient is asleep.  
   • Travel time between patients or between your home and the patient’s home.  
   • Mandatory training, meetings or supervision sessions.  
   • Waiting time on site.  
   Keep written or digital records—they are your evidence if HMRC checks you.

4. Do the hourly pay check  
   a. Add up the total pay before tax and other deductions for the pay period.  
   b. Add up all NMW-eligible hours for the same period.  
   c. Divide pay by hours.  
   d. Make sure the result is at least the correct NMW/NLW rate for the worker’s age.  
   If it is lower, raise the pay or reduce deductions that count (for example, charging for uniforms).

5. Provide an itemised payslip every payday  
   This shows gross pay, deductions and net pay. It is a legal requirement for workers classed as employees or workers.

6. Re-check rates every April and whenever the caregiver has a birthday that moves them into a higher wage band.

7. Use the GOV.UK calculator or get advice if unsure  
   The National Minimum Wage calculator on GOV.UK lets you test your figures. You can also ring the Acas helpline on 0300 123 1100 for free advice.

Key link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

Following these steps and keeping clear records will help you meet your legal duty and avoid penalties.

prompt: I'm a teacher in Scotland and I need to know what steps I should take to prepare for Making Tax Digital for Income Tax as a sole trader.
generated_response:
Making Tax Digital (MTD) for Income Tax is being phased in from April 2026. Sole traders with total business and property income above £50,000 will have to follow the rules first, then those above £30,000 from April 2027. Here’s how to get ready:

1. Check when you’ll be affected  
   • Add up your gross self-employed and property income for a tax year (before expenses).  
   • If it will be £50,001 or more in 2024-25, you’ll need to join MTD from 6 April 2026; £30,001–£50,000 means 6 April 2027.  
   • If your total stays under £30,000, you won’t be mandated yet, but you can join voluntarily.

2. Review how you keep records now  
   • Under MTD you must keep digital records of all income and expenses.  
   • Spreadsheets are allowed only if linked to “bridging” software that can send data to HMRC.

3. Choose compatible software early  
   • Look for “MTD for Income Tax-compatible” bookkeeping software.  
   • Most packages let you photograph receipts, tag expenses, and submit updates directly to HMRC.  
   • If you already use software, confirm with the provider that it will support MTD ITSA and what it will cost.

4. Set up a separate digital business bank account if practical  
   • A dedicated account makes it easier for software to pull in transactions automatically and reduces errors.

5. Practise quarterly bookkeeping  
   • From your start date you’ll send a summary to HMRC every 3 months, so get into the habit now.  
   • Reconcile bank transactions, label income (for example, tuition fees, resources sold) and allowable expenses (travel, consumables, professional fees) at least once a month.

6. Keep your existing Self Assessment routine for now  
   • You still file the annual Self Assessment return until you are fully on MTD.  
   • Once on MTD you’ll send:  
     a) the four quarterly updates,  
     b) an End-of-Period Statement (to confirm figures and claim allowances),  
     c) a Final Declaration to confirm all income (including PAYE salary if you have one).

7. Sign up for MTD when HMRC opens the service to you  
   • Use your Government Gateway user ID that you already use for Self Assessment.  
   • HMRC will publish the sign-up link and instructions well before April 2026.

8. Allow time to learn the software  
   • Book a demo or watch tutorials.  
   • If you have an accountant, check they can access your software and the MTD agent services account.

9. Keep backup copies  
   • Store digital records in the cloud and keep a secure backup, as you must retain them for 5 years after the 31 January submission deadline.

10. Stay informed  
   • Timetables can change, so check GOV.UK for updates: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax  

If you’re unsure which software to buy or whether your income falls into the first phase, speak to an accountant or contact HMRC’s Self Assessment helpline.

prompt: I'm a nurse in Wales and I'm considering selling shares in a company that I've owned for several years. What are the specific requirements I need to meet to qualify for Business Asset Disposal Relief, especially regarding the company's activities and my shareholding?
generated_response:
To qualify for Business Asset Disposal Relief (BADR) when you sell your shares, you must meet the conditions below on every day in the 2 years up to (and including) the date you sell them.

1. Type of company  
   • The company must be a trading company or the holding company of a trading group.  
   • “Trading” means it carries on substantive trading activities and any non-trading activities (for example, investment of surplus cash or property letting) are not substantial. HMRC’s rule of thumb is that non-trading activities should take up no more than about 20% of the company’s turnover, assets, expenses and management time.

2. Your role  
   • You must be an employee or office-holder (director or company secretary) of that company or of another company in the same trading group.

3. Size of shareholding – the 5% tests  
   You must hold, directly:  
   • at least 5% of the company’s ordinary share capital, and  
   • at least 5% of the voting rights.  

   In addition, one of these must also be true:  
   • You are entitled to at least 5% of the company’s distributable profits and 5% of assets on a winding-up, or  
   • You would receive at least 5% of the proceeds if the whole company were sold (the “proceeds” test).  

   If your shares come from an approved EMI option granted at least 2 years before you sell, the 5% tests do not apply—but the company still has to be a trading company and you must still be an employee.

4. Ownership period  
   • You must have met all the conditions above for a continuous period of 24 months before the sale date.  
   • Time you spent holding qualifying EMI options can count towards the 24 months, as long as the options were granted at least 24 months before the disposal.

5. Lifetime gains limit and tax rate  
   • You can claim BADR on up to £1 million of qualifying gains over your lifetime.  
   • Gains that qualify are taxed at 10% instead of the normal Capital Gains Tax rates.

How to claim  
1) Work out your gain on the share sale.  
2) Complete the Business Asset Disposal Relief section of your Self Assessment tax return for the year you sell the shares (or amend the return later).  
3) Submit the claim by the first anniversary of 31 January following the tax year of disposal (for example, by 31 January 2026 for a disposal in 2023-24).

If you are unsure whether the company’s non-trading activities are “substantial”, or if recent share issues have diluted your holding below 5%, get professional tax advice before you sell.

Find full guidance at: https://www.gov.uk/business-asset-disposal-relief

prompt: I'm running a small business and want to claim tax relief on some equipment I bought. How do I calculate the writing down allowances for these items?
generated_response:
Writing-down allowances (WDAs) let you claim tax relief on the cost that is still left after any first-year or Annual Investment Allowance (AIA) claims.

Follow these steps each year:

1. Put each item in the right capital allowance pool  
   • Main rate pool (18% WDA): most plant and machinery, computers, office equipment.  
   • Special rate pool (6% WDA): integral features of a building (e.g. lifts, air-con), long-life assets, cars with CO₂ emissions over 50 g/km.  
   • Single-asset pool: if you lease the item or it’s used partly for non-business work.

2. Work out the pool’s opening balance  
   • Start with last year’s closing balance (or £0 if it’s your first year).  
   • Add the cost of new items bought this year that you are NOT claiming under AIA or a first-year allowance.  
   • Subtract the sale price or insurance proceeds for any items you’ve disposed of.

3. Apply the WDA rate  
   • Multiply the remaining balance by 18% for the main pool or 6% for the special rate pool.  
   • The result is the amount you can deduct from your taxable profits for the year.

4. Carry the balance forward  
   • Subtract the allowance you’ve just claimed from the pool balance.  
   • The figure left becomes next year’s opening balance.

Worked example (main rate pool)  
Opening balance: £5,000  
Add new equipment (not covered by AIA): +£3,000  
Less sale proceeds: –£500  
Pool before WDA: £7,500  
WDA at 18%: £1,350 (claim this in your tax return)  
Closing balance to carry forward: £6,150

Key points  
• You can usually claim AIA (up to £1 million a year) first, which gives 100% relief straight away. Only use WDAs on any cost left over.  
• Keep invoices, sale receipts and your pool calculations with your records – HMRC can ask for them.  
• If your accounting period is shorter or longer than 12 months, pro-rate the WDA (e.g. 9-month period → 9/12 of the yearly allowance).  
• Cars have separate rates: 18% if CO₂ ≤ 50 g/km, 6% if over.

If you are unsure which pool an item belongs to, check the detailed HMRC guidance or speak to an accountant.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: What are the consequences if I fail to register with CIS before taking on a subcontractor?
generated_response:
Under the Construction Industry Scheme (CIS) you must register as a contractor before you pay a subcontractor. If you do not, HMRC can apply several sanctions.

1. Penalties for late CIS returns  
   • From the month you should have started filing, HMRC will expect a monthly return.  
   • Automatic late-filing penalties start at £100 for just 1 day late, rise to £200 after 2 months, then £300 (or 5 % of the CIS tax due, if higher) at 6 months, and the same again at 12 months. Interest is added on any tax that should have been paid.

2. Liability for unpaid deductions  
   • You are still required to deduct CIS tax from each payment to the subcontractor (usually 20 % once verified, 30 % if not).  
   • If you have not deducted or paid this over to HMRC, they can charge you the full amount that should have been withheld, plus interest and penalties of up to 100 % of that tax.

3. Higher deduction rate for the subcontractor  
   • Until you are registered and have verified the subcontractor, you must deduct 30 %. If you fail to do this, HMRC can pursue you for the shortfall.

4. Impact on future compliance status  
   • Late or non-registration can count against you if you later apply for gross-payment status (where no deductions are made from your own income).

5. Possible compliance checks  
   • Persistent non-compliance can trigger an HMRC investigation, leading to further assessments or enforcement action.

Safest next step  
Register as a CIS contractor straight away and submit any outstanding monthly returns. If you have already made payments, contact the CIS helpline to disclose the position and arrange to pay any tax and penalties due. Acting promptly usually limits extra charges.

Further guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I'm a nurse and I've been asked to help manage a trust for a patient. What steps should I take to ensure I understand the tax implications and responsibilities of being a trustee?
generated_response:
When you agree to act as a trustee you take on legal and tax duties for the trust as well as a duty of care to the patient (the beneficiary).  

Follow these steps to make sure you understand what HMRC expects and what records you must keep.

1. Get the trust deed  
   • Ask for a copy of the trust deed and any later amendments.  
   • Read it so you know the type of trust (for example bare, interest-in-possession, discretionary) and the powers you have.

2. Check if the trust must be registered  
   • Most UK trusts now have to be listed on HMRC’s Trust Registration Service (TRS), even if they do not owe tax yet.  
   • If it is not registered, register it online or ask a solicitor or accountant to do this. You will receive a Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) if HMRC expects a tax return.

3. Set up good record-keeping  
   • Open a separate bank account in the name of the trust if one is not already in place.  
   • Keep all bank statements, invoices, dividend vouchers and property records.  
   • Record any money paid to or for the beneficiary.

4. Understand the taxes that may apply  
   • Income Tax – due on interest, dividends, rent or other income the trust receives.  
   • Capital Gains Tax – due on any chargeable gain when the trust sells or transfers assets.  
   • Inheritance Tax – 10-year anniversary charges and exit charges can apply to some trusts.  
   • You must give each beneficiary an R185 form showing the income and tax deducted that relates to them.

5. File annual returns if required  
   • If HMRC issues a Trust and Estate Tax Return (SA900), complete it by 31 January following the tax year.  
   • Pay any tax due by the same deadline.  
   • Even if HMRC does not send a return, you must tell them within 6 months of the end of the tax year if the trust owes more than £100 in Income Tax or any Capital Gains Tax.

6. Review investments and act in the beneficiary’s best interests  
   • Follow the “prudent person” rule—invest wisely and avoid unnecessary risk.  
   • Take professional investment advice if the trust deed or size of the fund makes that sensible.

7. Keep other trustees (if any) and the beneficiary informed  
   • Prepare simple annual accounts.  
   • Share key decisions and minutes so everyone can see you are managing the trust properly.

8. Get professional help when unsure  
   • Trustees are personally liable for mistakes. If the trust is large or complex, budget for an accountant or solicitor to handle the tax returns and give legal advice.  
   • Free guidance is on GOV.UK under “Trusts and taxes” and “Register a trust”. Start at: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

By following these steps—and seeking professional advice if anything is unclear—you will meet your duties and avoid unexpected tax problems for yourself or the patient.

prompt: I need to pay Machine Games Duty, but I'm not sure how to calculate the amount I owe. What steps should I take to ensure I file the correct return and make the payment on time?
generated_response:
Machine Games Duty (MGD) is based on each machine’s “net takings” for the quarter and the rate that applies to the type of machine. Follow these steps:

1. Check you are registered  
   • You must be registered for MGD before you can file a return or pay. If you have not registered, do that first through your business tax account on GOV.​UK (look under the “Tax” section).  
   • HMRC will confirm your MGD registration number and the filing quarters that apply to you.

2. Keep a clear record for each machine during the quarter  
   • Total stakes taken (money or tokens put in).  
   • Total cash prizes paid out.  
   • Any non-cash prizes must be valued at cost to you.  
   Good records are essential if HMRC ever asks for evidence.

3. Work out net takings per machine  
   Net takings = stakes taken minus prizes paid out.  
   Ignore any VAT that may be included in the stake.

4. Identify the correct MGD rate  
   HMRC sets three rates:  
   • 5% (lower rate) – very low-stake, low-prize machines.  
   • 20% (standard rate) – most gaming machines.  
   • 25% (higher rate) – higher-stake or higher-prize machines.  
   Use your machine’s stake and prize limits to decide which rate applies. If you are unsure, ask your supplier or check the guidance on GOV.​UK under “Machine Games Duty”.

5. Calculate the duty  
   For each machine: net takings × applicable rate = duty due.  
   Add up the duty for all machines on the premises to get the amount for the quarter.

6. Complete the quarterly return (form MGD1)  
   • Log in to your business tax account and choose “Machine Games Duty – submit return”.  
   • Enter the net takings and duty for each rate band.  
   • Submit the return within 30 days of the quarter end date shown on your registration (for example, 30 April for the quarter ending 31 March).

7. Pay on time  
   • Payment is due on the same 30-day deadline.  
   • Faster Payments or CHAPS reach HMRC the same day; Bacs and Direct Debit take 3 working days.  
   • Use your 14-character MGD reference so HMRC can match the payment.

8. Keep copies  
   Save a PDF of the return and keep your working papers for at least four years in case HMRC checks them.

If anything is unclear—especially which rate your machines fall into—telephone the MGD helpline or ask a professional adviser before filing. It is better to confirm now than amend a return later.

You can start from the tax section on GOV.​UK (https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax) and follow the link for “Machine Games Duty” to reach the detailed guidance, the online return and payment instructions.

prompt: I heard about the 'Breathing Space' scheme. Can I still proceed with a bankruptcy petition if the debtor is using this scheme?
generated_response:
In most cases, no – you must pause a bankruptcy petition while the debtor is in a Breathing Space moratorium.

What the rules say  
1) When a standard Breathing Space (60-day) or a Mental Health Crisis Breathing Space is in force, creditors must not take “any step to enforce” a qualifying debt.  
2) Presenting, advertising or continuing with a bankruptcy petition counts as enforcement action, so it has to stop.  
3) If the petition was already issued before the Breathing Space started, the court will automatically stay the case until the moratorium ends. You cannot take further steps (for example, serving or advertising the petition) during that time.  
4) Interest, fees and charges on the debt must also be frozen for the duration of the moratorium.  
5) When the Breathing Space ends, you are free to start or resume bankruptcy action—unless the debtor has entered another formal insolvency process (for example an Individual Voluntary Arrangement), in which different rules apply.  
6) Exceptional circumstances: if you believe serious harm will be caused by waiting, you can apply to the court for permission to proceed, but permission is rarely granted and you would need strong evidence.

Safest next steps  
• Make sure you have received the official Breathing Space notification (usually by email or post). Check it lists your debt.  
• Update your records so all recovery action is suspended until the end-date shown in the notice.  
• Diarise the end-date; you will get a further notice if the Breathing Space is cancelled early or (in a mental-health case) extended.  
• If you think the debt was wrongly included, raise it with the debt advice provider named on the notice.

Read more  
Breathing Space: guidance for creditors – GOV.UK  
https://www.gov.uk/breathing-space

prompt: I'm a teacher in Scotland and I need to apply for a National Insurance number. What do I need to do and how long will it take?
generated_response:
You normally get a National Insurance (NI) number automatically just before you turn 16.  
If you never received one, or you moved to the UK later, you can apply online.

1. Check you qualify  
   • You must live in the UK and have the right to work or study here.  
   • You need a passport, biometric residence permit or national identity card you can photograph.

2. Apply online  
   • Go to GOV.UK and search “apply for a National Insurance number” or use: https://www.gov.uk/apply-national-insurance-number  
   • Fill in the short form with your personal details and upload clear photos of:  
     – your identity document (inside pages if it is a passport)  
     – your face (a selfie)  
   • Submit the form. It is free – there is no fast-track or paid service.

3. Wait for contact from DWP  
   • Most people hear back within a few days by email or text.  
   • If your photos are not clear or more evidence is needed, you might be asked to:  
     – upload extra images, or  
     – attend a short identity appointment or post original documents.

4. Receive your NI number letter  
   • When your identity is confirmed, DWP send a confirmation letter in the post.  
   • Standard times: around 4 to 6 weeks.  
   • It can take up to 16 weeks if demand is high or extra checks are needed.

5. Give the number to your employer and keep the letter safe  
   • You can start or continue working while you wait—tell the school you have applied and give them the number when it arrives.  
   • HMRC will update your tax and pension records automatically once the number is used.

If you are unsure whether you already have an NI number, look on:  
• old payslips or P60s  
• letters about tax, benefits or student loans  
• a personal tax account on GOV.UK

Still can’t find it, or need help with the online form?  
Phone the National Insurance helpline on 0300 200 3500 (Mon–Fri, office hours).

prompt: I work as a nurse and need to understand if I have to register with HMRC for anti-money laundering regulations. What are the requirements and steps to register?
generated_response:
You only need to register with HMRC for anti-money laundering (AML) supervision if the service you provide is one of the activities covered by the Money Laundering Regulations 2017.  

1. Check whether your work is a “regulated activity”.  
   HMRC supervises businesses that:  
   • act as money service businesses (currency exchange, money transmission, cheque cashing)  
   • provide accountancy or bookkeeping services  
   • offer trust or company formation services  
   • trade in high-value goods for cash (£10,000 or more)  
   • act as estate or letting agents, art market participants or bill payment service providers  

   Nursing, medical and other healthcare services are not on this list. If you only practise as a nurse (whether employed or self-employed) you do not need AML registration.

2. Situations where you might need to register  
   You would only have to register if you run a side business that does fall under the regulated activities above—for example, a bookkeeping service you run in your spare time.

3. If you do need to register  
   a. Set up or sign in to a Government Gateway account.  
   b. Go to ‘Register for Money Laundering Supervision’ and add the service.  
   c. Complete the online application, pay the registration fee and any fit-and-proper checks fee.  
   d. Keep records and renew annually.

Further details and the registration portal are on GOV.UK at:  
https://www.gov.uk/guidance/money-laundering-regulations-register-with-hmrc

If you are unsure whether an extra activity you offer is caught by the regulations, check the guidance above or contact HMRC’s AML helpline before applying.

prompt: I'm the executor of my uncle's will and need to submit Inheritance Tax. What form do I use to report the value of his property and land?
generated_response:
Use schedule IHT405 – ‘Houses, land, buildings and interests in land’.  

How it fits in:

1. Complete the main return form IHT400 for the estate as a whole.  
2. Attach schedule IHT405 to give the detailed valuation of any UK property or land the deceased owned (or a share of).  
3. Send both forms, any other relevant schedules and the payment (or payment reference if paying electronically) to HMRC’s Inheritance Tax Office.

If you’re not sure of the open-market value, HMRC expects you to get a professional valuation from a surveyor or estate agent and keep the written evidence.

Guidance and blank forms are on GOV.UK – search “IHT400 forms and schedules” or go straight to:
https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

If the estate is an “excepted estate” (for most deaths on or after 1 January 2022 that are below the nil-rate band or pass to a spouse/civil partner or charity) you normally do not file IHT400/IHT405 at all. In that situation you just report the figures to the Scottish Courts & Tribunals Service when applying for Confirmation.  

If you are in any doubt, check the HMRC Inheritance Tax helpline or speak to a probate professional before submitting the forms.

prompt: I'm a nurse and I've received a Simple Assessment letter. How do I pay the tax I owe and what happens if I don't pay on time?
generated_response:
A Simple Assessment (sometimes shown as a “NoA” – Notice of Assessment) means HMRC has worked out the tax you owe and given you a deadline to pay.  

Paying it is straightforward.

How to pay

1. Find your payment reference  
   • It’s on the letter – usually 14- to 17-digits starting with ‘XA’.  
   • You must quote this exactly so the money reaches the right account.

2. Choose a payment method  
   • Online or telephone banking (Faster Payments, CHAPS or Bacs)  
     – Sort code 08-32-10, account number 12001039, account name ‘HMRC Cumbernauld’.  
   • Debit or corporate credit card: use the “Pay your tax bill” service on GOV.UK.  
   • In person at your bank or building society (cash or cheque) – take the payslip.  
   • Cheque by post to HMRC – allow at least 3 working days and enclose the payslip.  

   Full details and the online card-payment link are at:  
   https://www.gov.uk/pay-tax

3. Pay by the deadline on your letter  
   • It is normally 31 January after the tax year, or 3 months from the date of the letter – whichever is later.  
   • Keep the payment confirmation or bank receipt.

If you cannot pay in full

Contact HMRC before the deadline on 0300 200 3823 (Time to Pay). In most cases they will let you set up an instalment plan if you give details of your income and outgoings.

What happens if you pay late

• Interest: charged daily from the day after the deadline until the tax is cleared.  
• Penalties on the unpaid amount:  
  – 30 days late: 5 %  
  – 6 months late: a further 5 %  
  – 12 months late: another 5 %  
• HMRC can also pass the debt to collection agencies or take it directly from wages or your bank account.

Think the figure is wrong?

You have 60 days from the date of the Simple Assessment to query or appeal – follow the instructions on the back of the letter or ring HMRC using the number above.

Best next step: pay (or arrange a plan) before the stated deadline to avoid interest and penalties.

prompt: I recently moved house and need to update my address with HMRC. How do I do this, and what do I need to prepare?
generated_response:
You can usually update your address in a few minutes online.

1. Go to ‘Tell HMRC about a change of address’ on GOV.UK  
   https://www.gov.uk/tell-hmrc-change-of-details

2. Select ‘Start now’ and sign in with your Government Gateway user ID and password.  
   • If you have never used the service, choose ‘Create sign-in details’ and follow the on-screen steps.

3. After signing in, pick ‘Personal details’ (or the equivalent link in your Personal Tax Account) and enter:  
   • your new address and postcode  
   • the date you moved

4. Check the summary page and submit. HMRC will update:  
   • Income Tax and National Insurance records  
   • Self Assessment (if you’re registered)  
   • State Pension and PAYE details (if relevant)

5. Extra steps if you claim other HMRC benefits:  
   • Tax Credits – phone the Tax Credits Helpline (0345 300 3900) or use the tax-credits online service to report the change within 1 month.  
   • Child Benefit – you can update the address in the same online account, or call 0300 200 3100 if you prefer.

What to have ready  
• Government Gateway user ID and password  
• National Insurance number (shown on payslips, P60 or your NI card)
