• Full new address and move date  
• Any HMRC reference numbers (for example your Unique Taxpayer Reference if you do Self Assessment)

If you cannot use the internet, phone HMRC on 0300 200 3300 (Income Tax) with the details above.

Keep a note of the confirmation screen or email for your records, and tell your pension provider, bank and employer separately—HMRC does not pass the new address to them.

prompt: I'm running a limited company in Wales and need to file my accounts and tax return. What steps should I follow to ensure everything is done correctly and on time?
generated_response:
Running a UK limited company means you have two separate filings every year:

• Statutory accounts to Companies House  
• A Company Tax Return (CT600) to HMRC, together with any Corporation Tax payment

Follow these steps to stay on track.

1. Know your deadlines  
   a) File statutory accounts: within 9 months of your company’s financial year-end (for first accounts the deadline can be shorter).  
   b) Pay Corporation Tax: 9 months and 1 day after the end of the accounting period.  
   c) File the Company Tax Return: within 12 months of the same accounting period. Missing any of these triggers automatic penalties.

2. Keep proper records all year  
   • Sales invoices, purchase bills, bank statements and payroll records.  
   • Maintain a fixed-asset register and mileage logs if relevant.  
   • Keep records for at least 6 years in case HMRC asks to see them.

3. Prepare statutory (GAAP-compliant) accounts  
   • Balance sheet, profit and loss, notes and a director’s report.  
   • Use accounting software or an accountant to ensure the format meets Companies House requirements.  
   • Get the accounts approved and signed by a director before filing.

4. File the accounts with Companies House  
   • Online filing is quickest and cheaper than paper.  
   • You’ll receive an acknowledgement when Companies House accepts them. Keep this for your records.

5. Work out your Corporation Tax  
   • Adjust the profit in your accounts for tax purposes (for example, disallowable expenses, capital allowances).  
   • Calculate any R&D relief or other allowances your company can claim.

6. Submit the Company Tax Return (CT600) to HMRC  
   • Must be filed online using iXBRL-tagged accounts and computations.  
   • Most accounting software produces the correct format, or your accountant can do it.  
   • Keep the HMRC submission receipt as proof of filing.

7. Pay any Corporation Tax due  
   • Pay electronically to clear in HMRC’s account by the deadline.  
   • Set a calendar reminder a week early; bank processing times can vary.

8. Review and repeat  
   • At your year-end, confirm your next set of statutory and tax deadlines.  
   • Update your bookkeeping regularly to avoid a last-minute rush.

9. If something goes wrong  
   • File as soon as possible—penalties increase the longer you are late.  
   • Contact Companies House or HMRC if you cannot pay on time; they may agree a payment plan.

Further guidance, forms and online filing services are on GOV.UK:
https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

If you are unsure at any stage, consider using a qualified accountant or contacting HMRC’s Corporation Tax helpline.

prompt: I'm a nurse and I want to know how to estimate my Income Tax for the current year. I earn a salary and have a pension contribution. How do I use the service to calculate my tax and National Insurance payments?
generated_response:
You can use HMRC’s free ‘Check your Income Tax for the current year’ service to see an up-to-date estimate of both Income Tax and employee National Insurance (NI) on your salary.

1. Get the details you will need  
   • Your gross annual salary (the amount before tax).  
   • How much you pay into your NHS or workplace pension each year.  
   • Your National Insurance number.

2. Sign in to the service  
   • Go to GOV.UK and search ‘check income tax current year’ or use: https://www.gov.uk/check-income-tax-current-year  
   • Select ‘Start now’ and sign in with your Government Gateway user ID and password.  
     – If you do not have an account, choose ‘Create sign in details’ and follow the on-screen steps (you will need an email address and proof of identity such as a passport or driving licence).

3. Add or check your PAYE (salary) details  
   • The service should already show your employer if they report PAYE in real time.  
   • Confirm the figures for salary and pension contributions shown.  
   • If anything is missing, use the ‘Add or correct employment details’ link.

4. View your tax estimate  
   • The main screen shows:  
     – Your tax code.  
     – The amount of Income Tax HMRC expects to collect this tax year.  
     – An estimate of your NI contributions.  
   • You can expand ‘how your tax is worked out’ to see the steps, including pension deductions and the Personal Allowance (£12,570 for most people).

5. Check the figures make sense  
   • Make sure your pension contributions have been taken off your taxable pay.  
   • If the salary or pension amounts are wrong, update them or phone HMRC to correct them; the estimate will refresh once HMRC processes the change.

6. Save or print the estimate  
   • Use the ‘print/save this page’ option if you need a copy for your records.

If you prefer using a smartphone or tablet, the HMRC app offers the same estimate once you sign in.

Need help or can’t get online?  
• Call the HMRC PAYE helpline on 0300 200 3300 (Monday–Friday). Have your National Insurance number and latest payslip to hand.  
• A helpline adviser can tell you your current tax code and give a verbal estimate, and can post a summary if needed.

Further guidance: the general tax section is at https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

Following these steps will give you the most accurate, up-to-date figure HMRC holds for your Income Tax and National Insurance for the current tax year.

prompt: I inherited a pension pot from my late partner and want to know if I need to pay tax on the money I receive. What steps should I take to understand my tax obligations and how to report them to HMRC?
generated_response:
Whether you pay tax on an inherited pension depends on how and when you take the money.  

1. Ask the pension provider for the key facts  
   • The age your partner was when they died (before or after 75).  
   • The type of pension (defined-contribution pot, annuity, or defined-benefit scheme).  
   • How the benefits will be paid to you (lump sum, flexi-access drawdown, regular income, or annuity).  
   • Whether any tax will be taken off before you are paid.  
   Get this in writing—HMRC may ask for it later.

2. Work out if the payments are taxable  
   • Death before 75 and the payment is made within 2 years: normally tax-free.  
   • Death at 75 or over, or payment made after 2 years: anything you draw is treated as your income and taxed at your marginal Income Tax rate.  
   • A dependant’s or nominee’s annuity is always taxable as income, whatever the age at death.  

   The official rules are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/tax-on-inherited-private-pensions

3. Check how tax will be collected  
   • Lump sum: the provider may deduct tax through PAYE using an emergency code; HMRC will later issue a refund or ask for extra if the code was wrong.  
   • Drawdown or inherited annuity: tax is taken each time you’re paid, again through PAYE.  

4. Make sure HMRC has the right tax code  
   • When the first payment is made, HMRC should receive the details automatically from the provider and adjust your tax code.  
   • If you think too much or too little tax is being taken, phone HMRC’s Income Tax helpline (0300 200 3300) and quote the PAYE reference from your payslip or P60.  

5. Decide if you need Self Assessment  
   • You only need to file a tax return if your total untaxed income (including the inherited pension) is more than £2,500 a year, or if HMRC tells you to.  
   • If required, register by 5 October following the tax year in which you first receive the income: https://www.gov.uk/register-for-self-assessment  
   • Enter the pension payments in the ‘Other pensions and retirement annuities’ section of the return.  

6. Keep records  
   • Payment statements or payslips from the provider.  
   • Letters showing the date of death, payment dates and amounts.  
   • Any P60 or P45 they issue at the end of the tax year.  
   Keep these for at least six years in case HMRC asks for evidence.

If anything is unclear after speaking to the provider, call HMRC or a regulated financial adviser before you withdraw money—once taken, tax mistakes can be harder to fix.

prompt: What should I do if I'm facing a court claim for debts and I'm not sure whether to go bankrupt or seek other options?
generated_response:
Court action is urgent, so deal with the claim first and then weigh up your longer-term debt options.

1. Read the claim form straight away. Note the “response pack” date.  
   • If the debt is correct and you can pay it, you can admit the claim and offer instalments.  
   • If you need time to decide, send the Acknowledgement of Service within 14 days to get a total of 28 days to respond.  
   • If you dispute all or part of the debt, you must file a defence within the 28-day limit.

2. Gather the facts.  
   • Check that the amount, creditor details and any interest or fees are right.  
   • Find evidence of payments, agreements or disputes.  
   • If anything looks wrong, ask the claimant for written proof (“particulars of claim”, credit agreement, default notice).

3. Work out what you can afford.  
   • List your income and essential spending to see what is left for debt payments.  
   • This budget will be needed for any court offer, payment plan or insolvency application.

4. Get free, impartial debt advice before choosing a solution.  
   • MoneyHelper, Citizens Advice and StepChange all offer confidential help.  
   • An adviser can look at your full situation and explain the pros and cons of each option.

5. Compare the main options.  
   a) Payment plan or debt management plan – informal, keeps you out of court but relies on creditors agreeing.  
   b) Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) – a formal 5- or 6-year agreement; you make a single monthly payment and any unpaid balance is written off at the end if you keep to the terms.  
   c) Debt Relief Order (DRO) – for debts under £30,000, low assets and low spare income; cheaper and less severe than bankruptcy.  
   d) Bankruptcy – usually lasts 1 year but can affect your home, assets, job and credit record for 6 years; fees total £680.

   GOV.UK guidance:
   • Options for paying off your debts: https://www.gov.uk/options-for-paying-off-your-debts  
   • Bankruptcy: https://www.gov.uk/bankruptcy  

6. Decide how to handle the court claim.  
   • If you choose bankruptcy, you can still make a reasonable monthly offer on the claim form until the order is approved.  
   • If you prefer a payment plan, propose affordable instalments on the admission form (N9A).  
   • If you file for a DRO or IVA, tell the creditor; they may pause action while it is assessed.

7. Keep to any deadline or agreement you make. Missing a court deadline can lead to a default judgment, extra costs and enforcement (bailiffs, attachment of earnings).

If you are unsure after speaking to an adviser, act to protect your position: return the Acknowledgement of Service, supply any defence or offer on time, and keep the court informed while you explore your chosen debt remedy.

prompt: I'm a retired person who has been paying the reduced rate of National Insurance. How do I stop paying the reduced rate and start paying the standard rate?
generated_response:
First check whether you still need to pay National Insurance at all.

• If you have reached State Pension age (currently 66), you no longer pay National Insurance on employment or self-employment earnings. Give proof of your date of birth to your employer or HMRC and your contributions should stop completely.

If you are below State Pension age but have been paying the married woman’s or widow’s reduced rate and now want to pay the standard rate, do the following:

1. Get form CF9 – ‘Revocation of Married Woman’s Reduced Rate Election’.  
   You can download it or ask HMRC to post a copy (search “CF9 revocation form” on GOV.UK or call the National Insurance helpline).

2. Fill in the form, sign and date it.  
   The form asks for your National Insurance number, the date you want the change to start, and details of your current employer if you have one.

3. Send the completed form to HMRC at the address shown on the form.

4. Tell your employer’s payroll team you have cancelled your reduced-rate election.  
   They will change your NI category letter from ‘B’ (reduced rate) to ‘A’ (standard) from the first payday after HMRC confirms the change.

5. Check your next payslip.  
   It should show the standard employee NI rate and category letter ‘A’. Keep the payslip and HMRC confirmation for your records.

Self-employed?  
Tick the box on the CF9 form saying you are self-employed. HMRC will start charging Class 2 and Class 4 NI from the date you choose (usually the start of the next tax year).

Need help or unsure which form to use?  
Contact the National Insurance helpline on 0300 200 3500 or use the webchat on GOV.UK.

Relevant guidance:  
https://www.gov.uk/national-insurance-married-women-and-widows

prompt: I recently moved house and need to update my address with HMRC. How do I do this and what do I need to prepare beforehand?
generated_response:
You can tell HMRC about a new address online in your personal tax account. It’s quick if you already have a Government Gateway sign-in.

What to have ready  
• Your Government Gateway user ID and password (or be ready to create one).  
• Your National Insurance number.  
• If you complete Self Assessment: your 10-digit Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR).  
• If you claim Child Benefit or Tax Credits: your customer reference numbers (shown on recent letters).  
• The full new address and the date you moved.

How to update the address  
1. Go to ‘Tell HMRC your change of address’ on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/tell-hmrc-change-of-details  
2. Sign in to your personal tax account with your Government Gateway details.  
3. Select ‘Personal details’ then ‘Address’, enter the new address and the date you moved, and submit.  
4. Check for on-screen confirmation or an email to the address linked to your account.

What happens next  
• HMRC updates your PAYE record, Self Assessment, National Insurance and State Pension details automatically.  
• If you claim Child Benefit or Tax Credits, the change usually flows through, but it’s worth checking those sections of your account a few days later.  
• Your employer or pension provider will see the new address on their next payroll download, so you don’t normally need to tell them separately.

If you cannot use the online service  
Call the relevant HMRC helpline (PAYE: 0300 200 3300; Self Assessment: 0300 200 3310; Tax Credits: 0345 300 3900) and give the same details over the phone.

Keep a note of the date you reported the change in case you need to show you told HMRC promptly.

prompt: I'm running a limited company and received a notice to deliver a Company Tax Return. What should I include in my tax return and when do I need to submit it?
generated_response:
When you get a ‘notice to deliver a Company Tax Return’, you must file a full Corporation Tax return package, even if the company made no profit.

What to include  
1. CT600 form – the main Company Tax Return.  
   • company name, registration number and period covered  
   • taxable profit or loss for the period  
   • Corporation Tax calculation, showing reliefs and allowances claimed (for example AIA, R&D, losses brought forward)  
2. Statutory accounts for the same period (‘company accounts’) – profit and loss, balance sheet, directors’ report and any notes.  
3. Tax computations – a detailed breakdown of how you moved from the accounts figure to the taxable profit or loss.  
4. Any supplementary CT600 schedules you need (for example loans to participators, charities, tonnage tax, ring-fence activities).  
5. iXBRL tagging – accounts and computations must be tagged before you upload them.  
File the return online through HMRC’s Corporation Tax service or compatible commercial software.

Filing deadline  
The deadline is the later of:  
• 12 months after the end of the accounting period the return covers, or  
• 3 months after the date on HMRC’s notice (if the notice arrived less than 9 months after the period ended).

Example:  
Accounting period ends 31 March 2024.  
– Standard deadline: 31 March 2025.  
If HMRC issues the notice on 1 February 2025, you still have until 1 May 2025 (three months) because that is later than 31 March 2025.

Do not confuse this with the Corporation Tax payment deadline, which is 9 months + 1 day after the period end (in the example above, 1 January 2025).

Penalties for late filing start at £100 and increase quickly, so file on time even if you have no tax to pay.

Full guidance and online filing links:  
https://www.gov.uk/company-tax-returns

prompt: My relative has been placed in care and needs help with their tax credits. What steps should I take to manage their claim effectively?
generated_response:
Helping someone else with their tax-credit claim usually involves two things: (a) making sure HMRC is told about the change in their circumstances and (b) getting authority so you can speak to HMRC and handle the claim for them.

1) Collect the key details  
   • their full name, address (or care-home address), National Insurance number and tax-credit reference.  
   • the exact date they moved into care and whether it is expected to be temporary or permanent.

2) Tell HMRC about the move straight away (you must report most changes within 1 month).  
   • Phone the Tax Credits Helpline: 0345 300 3900 (Mon–Fri).  
   • Explain the care situation and give the date it started. HMRC will confirm if the award can continue (for example, payments can sometimes carry on for up to 52 weeks in residential care) or if anything needs to change.

3) Get authority to deal with the claim on their behalf.  
   • If your relative can still manage their affairs, ask them to complete form TC689 (‘authorising a helper’) naming you. HMRC will then let you discuss and update the claim.  
   • If they cannot manage their affairs, apply to become their ‘appointee’ with HMRC or use an existing lasting power of attorney that covers finances. HMRC will guide you through what evidence they need.

4) Check the rest of the claim details are up to date.  
   • Household income for the current tax year.  
   • Any other recent changes (for example working hours, disability benefits, childcare costs).  
   • Make notes of everything you report and keep any letters from HMRC.

5) Keep making the annual renewal on time.  
   • Each spring HMRC sends a renewal pack. Complete it—even if nothing has changed—or the payments will stop and any overpayment will have to be repaid.

6) Ask about future changes.  
   • Tax credits are being replaced by Universal Credit. If the care placement becomes permanent or other circumstances change, HMRC will tell you if and when a new Universal Credit claim is needed.

7) Get independent advice if you are unsure.  
   • Organisations such as Citizens Advice or Age UK can check the award, make sure no other benefits are missed and help with any overpayment issues.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

If anything is unclear, phone the Tax Credits Helpline first—they can confirm the correct form and next steps for your situation.

prompt: I'm a student in Wales and I want to know what information I need to provide to use the Self Assessment tax calculator for estimating my tax bill. What details should I enter?
generated_response:
The Self Assessment tax calculator on GOV.UK works best if you have the figures you’ll eventually put on your return. Before you start, collect the information below and enter the amounts for the tax year you’re checking (6 April to 5 April).

1. Personal details  
   • Your National Insurance number  
   • Your date of birth (to check age–related allowances)  
   • Where you live (England, Wales or Scotland – so the calculator uses the right income-tax rates)

2. Income that will appear on your return  
   • Employment income: total pay and tax already deducted under PAYE (from your P60 or last payslip)  
   • Self-employment profits after allowable business expenses, if any  
   • Bank or building-society interest received (gross amount)  
   • Dividends from UK or overseas companies  
   • Rental income after allowable expenses  
   • Any pension income or state benefits that are taxable  
   • Other taxable income – for example trust income, casual earnings or overseas income

3. Tax already paid or deducted  
   • PAYE tax (from your P60/P45)  
   • Tax deducted at source on interest, dividends or CIS payments  
   • Payments on account you have already made for the year

4. Deductions, allowances and reliefs  
   • Gift Aid donations (gross amount)  
   • Personal pension contributions (gross amount)  
   • Trading or property allowances you intend to claim  
   • Blind Person’s Allowance or Marriage Allowance you’re entitled to  
   • Professional fees or subscriptions you pay personally  
   • Charitable gifts of shares, land or property

5. Student loan details (if relevant)  
   • The plan type (Plan 1, Plan 2, Plan 4 or postgraduate loan)  
   • Any loan repayments already taken through PAYE

6. Capital gains (only if you need a full picture)  
   • Total gains and losses after deducting any reliefs

Enter the figures exactly as they appear on your documents. The calculator will then show:

• how much Income Tax and (if applicable) National Insurance you’re likely to owe  
• the effect of Welsh income-tax rates  
• any balance still to pay or potential refund

The result is an estimate. Keep the figures you used so you can copy them into your online Self Assessment return later.

For the calculator and related guidance, start at GOV.UK/browse/tax.

prompt: I need to submit my Self Assessment tax return, but I'm not sure if I should do it online or print it out. What's the best way to go about it?
generated_response:
Most people find the online Self Assessment service quicker and easier than sending a paper (printed) return. Here is how the two options compare and what to do next.

1. Deadlines  
   • Paper SA100: must reach HMRC by 31 October after the end of the tax year (or 3 months from the date HMRC told you to file, if that was later).  
   • Online return: deadline is 31 January. You get an extra three months, which helps if you are waiting for figures such as bank interest or dividends.

2. Convenience and accuracy  
   • Online: the form adapts to the answers you give, does the calculations for you, checks for common errors and gives you an instant estimate of tax due or any refund.  
   • Paper: you fill in every section that might apply and do your own calculations, or wait for HMRC to do them after they receive it.

3. Amending a return  
   • Online: you can log in and correct mistakes up to 12 months after 31 January.  
   • Paper: you must resend a corrected paper return, which takes longer.

4. Confirmation and records  
   • Online: you receive on-screen confirmation straight away and can download a PDF copy at any time.  
   • Paper: get proof of posting and keep a copy yourself in case it is lost in the post.

5. Getting started online  
   a. Create or sign in to a Government Gateway account on GOV.UK.  
   b. Add the Self Assessment service (you will need your Unique Taxpayer Reference and National Insurance number).  
   c. HMRC sends an activation code by post; enter it within 28 days to finish setting up.  
   d. Once activated, complete and submit the return online, then pay any tax due by 31 January.

6. If you still prefer paper  
   • Download the SA100 form or phone HMRC to ask for one to be posted to you.  
   • Use black ink, write clearly, and post it well before 31 October using recorded delivery if possible.  
   • Keep a copy for your records.

7. Need help?  
   • Use HMRC’s online help sheets, webchat or the Self Assessment helpline.  
   • If you are unsure which sections apply, consider getting advice from a professional accountant or a free advice service.

GOV.UK link: https://www.gov.uk/self-assessment-tax-returns

In short, filing online gives you a longer deadline, built-in checks and instant confirmation, so it is usually the better option unless you have no internet access or strongly prefer paper.

prompt: I heard there are council garages available, but I'm not sure how to apply. Can you explain the process?
generated_response:
Most councils let you rent a lock-up garage, but each area runs its own waiting list and rules. The basic steps are the same across Wales.

1) Find the right department  
   • Go to your local council’s website (or call the housing team).  
   • Look for pages called “Rent a council garage” or “Lock-up garages”.

2) Check who can apply  
   • Most councils accept applications from both council tenants and private residents who live in the borough.  
   • Some give priority to Blue Badge holders or people living on the estate where the garage is located.

3) Join the waiting list  
   • Fill in the online or paper form with your name, address, contact details and (sometimes) the make and registration of your vehicle.  
   • List the garage sites you’re interested in.  
   • Submit proof of address and photo ID if asked.  
   • The council will confirm your place on the list and an approximate waiting time.

4) Receive an offer  
   • When a garage becomes free, the council will contact the next person on the list.  
   • You’ll normally have a few days to accept, sign the licence agreement and pay the first month’s rent.

5) Sign the licence and pay the fees  
   • Read the terms: what you can store, notice period (usually one week), and rules on repairs or sub-letting (not allowed).  
   • Pay the rent—often monthly by Direct Debit—plus any refundable key deposit.

6) Keep your licence valid  
   • Make sure the vehicle you store is taxed and insured.  
   • Tell the council if you move home or no longer need the garage; give the required notice in writing.

If you can’t find the garage application page, use the “Housing and local services” section on GOV.UK (https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services) and follow the link to your local council.

Still unsure? Call your council’s housing or neighbourhood services team and ask for “garage lettings” – they can send you the correct form or put you on the waiting list over the phone.

prompt: If I am a landlord with multiple flats rented out on assured shorthold tenancies, how many votes do I get in an RTM company and what happens if the RTM process doesn't result in the building being managed by them?
generated_response:
Voting rights in a Right to Manage (RTM) company  

• You get one vote for every flat for which you are a qualifying leaseholder.  
  – If you own three long-lease flats (even if they are all let out on assured shorthold tenancies), you have three separate votes.  
  – Sub-letting the flats does not remove your voting rights, provided you still hold the long leases yourself.  

What if the RTM process does not complete?  

The building is not handed over if:  
• the RTM company withdraws its claim,  
• the claim is ruled invalid or refused by the tribunal, or  
• the company fails to meet the legal timetable up to the “acquisition date”.

If that happens:  
1. Day-to-day management stays with the freeholder or their managing agent exactly as before.  
2. The RTM company must pay the landlord’s reasonable costs of dealing with the failed claim (for example, legal or valuation fees).  
3. Leaseholders must wait 12 months before serving a fresh RTM claim for the same building.  
4. In the meantime, they can consider other routes, such as applying for the tribunal to appoint a manager or starting collective enfranchisement to buy the freehold instead.

You can read more about leaseholder rights and building management on GOV.UK (Housing and local services section): https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: My leaseholder association is not recognized by the landlord. How can I resolve this issue through the tribunal?
generated_response:
You can ask the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) to give your association formal “recognised tenants’ association” (RTA) status when the landlord will not grant it. Once you have the certificate, the landlord must consult the association on service charges and other leasehold matters.

1. Check you meet the basic rules  
   • At least 50% of the qualifying leaseholders in the block or estate must be paid-up members.  
   • The association must have a written constitution, elected officers and its own bank account.  
   • It must represent only long-lease residential tenants (normally leases over 21 years).  

2. Collect the evidence  
   • A copy of your constitution.  
   • A membership list showing flat numbers, full names and contact details (mark anyone who does not want details shared).  
   • Proof that each member owns a qualifying lease (for example, a copy of the lease front page or Land Registry title).  
   • Minutes of the meeting where officers were elected.  
   • Any correspondence with the landlord refusing recognition.

3. Complete the tribunal application form  
   • Use the “Recognition of a tenants’ association (RTA)” form on GOV.UK or request it from your regional tribunal office.  
   • Attach the evidence, sign it and keep copies for everyone involved.  
   • There is currently no application fee for this type of case.

4. Send the application  
   • Post or email it to the relevant First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) regional office (addresses are on the form).  
   • At the same time, send a copy to the landlord or managing agent so they have the chance to comment.

5. Tribunal process  
   • The tribunal will review the papers and may ask for more information or arrange a short hearing (often held online or by phone).  
   • The landlord can object, but must explain why the legal tests are not met.  
   • If everything is in order, the tribunal issues a certificate of recognition, usually for 4–5 years (renewable).

6. After you are recognised  
   • Give the certificate to the landlord and managing agent.  
   • The landlord must then consult the association on service-charge budgets, major works and insurance.  
   • Keep membership above 50% and hold regular, minuted meetings so renewal is straightforward.

If you need practical help with the form or gathering documents, a local leasehold advice centre or citizens advice service can guide you.  

GOV.UK links  
General leasehold and tribunal information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: Is there any support available for people who have missed mortgage payments?
generated_response:
Help is available, but act quickly so the arrears do not grow.

1) Speak to your lender straight away  
   • Tell them you have missed payments and ask for their “tailored support”.  
   • Most lenders can offer options such as reduced payments, switching to interest-only for a time, extending the mortgage term or adding the missed amount to the end of the loan.  
   • Agree a plan in writing and keep to it.

2) Work out what you can afford  
   • List your essential spending (housing costs, energy, food, travel).  
   • Use this to show the lender a realistic figure you can pay each month.

3) Get free, independent debt advice  
   • Contact Citizens Advice Scotland on 0800 028 1456 or MoneyHelper on 0800 138 7777.  
   • An adviser can talk to the lender for you, check benefits and help with a budget.

4) Check if you can get Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI)  
   • SMI is a government loan that pays towards the interest on your mortgage if you receive certain benefits (for example Universal Credit, Employment and Support Allowance or Pension Credit).  
   • Read the rules and how to claim on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/support-for-mortgage-interest

5) If you live in Scotland and repossession is a risk, look at the Home Owners’ Support Fund  
   • Mortgage to Rent: a social landlord buys your home and you become a tenant.  
   • Mortgage to Shared Equity: the Scottish Government takes a stake in your home to clear or reduce the mortgage.  
   • An advice agency can help you apply.

6) Consider extra breathing space  
   • In Scotland you can apply for a short-term moratorium on creditor action, or set up a Debt Arrangement Scheme that freezes interest while you repay what you owe.  
   • A debt adviser can submit the forms.

7) Keep talking to the lender  
   • Tell them at once if your situation changes.  
   • Open all letters—ignoring them makes court action more likely.

More housing and money guidance is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

If you are unsure which option fits your circumstances, speak to a qualified debt adviser before you decide.

prompt: Are there any other schemes similar to Rent to Buy in the UK besides England?
generated_response:
Yes. Housing support is devolved, so each nation has its own version of “rent first, buy later” or part-buy schemes.

1. Wales  
   • Rent to Own – Wales: you rent a new-build home at market rent for 5 years. Up to 25 % of the rent you have paid is turned into a deposit when you take out a mortgage at the end of the term.  
   • Shared Ownership – Wales and Homebuy – Wales: you buy a share (usually 25 %–75 %) and pay reduced rent on the rest, with the option to buy more later.

2. Scotland  
   • LIFT (Low-cost Initiative for First Time Buyers) – Open Market and New Supply Shared Equity schemes let you buy most of a home with a smaller deposit while the Scottish Government takes an equity share.  
   • Mid-Market Rent-to-Buy: some councils and housing associations offer 5 to 10-year rent periods at below-market rent and give a discount or deposit contribution if you buy. Availability is local, not nationwide.

3. Northern Ireland  
   • Rent to Own (run by Co-Ownership): you rent a new home for 3 years. Part of the rent (plus any increase in the property’s value) is turned into a deposit if you buy at the end of the term.  
   • Co-Ownership Shared Ownership: buy 50 %–90 % of a home and pay rent on the rest, with the option to “staircase” up to full ownership.

Next steps  
• Schemes open and close, and some have waiting lists. Check the housing pages of the relevant national government or the local housing association running the scheme.  
• Compare the monthly costs, deposit rules and length of the rent period so you pick the scheme that best matches your budget and timescale.

You can start from the general housing section on GOV.UK and follow the links for Wales, Scotland or Northern Ireland: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I submitted a listed building consent application, but the decision came late. What should I do now?
generated_response:
A late decision does not automatically cancel the consent process, but you still have options.

1. Check the decision notice  
   • Read it carefully, including any conditions or reasons for refusal.  
   • Note the date on the notice—this starts the clock for any appeal.

2. Decide whether you are happy with the outcome  
   • If consent was granted and the conditions are acceptable, you can proceed as set out in the notice.  
   • If it was refused, or the conditions are too restrictive, you can appeal.

3. Appealing the decision (Scotland)  
   • You normally have 3 months from the date of the decision notice to appeal to the Scottish Ministers through the Directorate for Planning and Environmental Appeals (DPEA).  
   • You cannot now appeal on “non-determination” because a decision has been issued, even though it was late.  
   • Gather all documents: your original application, the late decision notice, supporting plans, and any correspondence, then submit them with your appeal form.

4. If you are not appealing but are unhappy about the delay  
   • Raise a complaint with the planning department first.  
   • If still dissatisfied, you can escalate through the council’s complaints procedure and, ultimately, to the Scottish Public Services Ombudsman.

5. Keep records  
   • Save all emails, letters and the date you received the decision—these are useful for appeals or complaints.

Further information  
GOV.UK overview of housing and planning services: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services  
For appeals, see the “listed building consent appeals” guidance on the Scottish Government or DPEA website.

If you are unsure about any step, consider getting professional planning advice before the appeal deadline passes.

prompt: I'm trying to understand if I can get help with my mortgage interest payments. What are the conditions for receiving Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI)?
generated_response:
Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI) is a loan from the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) that helps pay only the interest on your mortgage or certain home-improvement loans. You repay it (with interest) when you sell or transfer your home.  

To qualify, all of the following must apply.

1. You receive a qualifying benefit  
   • Universal Credit (UC) – you must have been getting UC for at least 3 continuous months.  
   • Income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance, Income Support or income-related Employment and Support Allowance – you normally wait 39 weeks (about 9 months) from the date your claim started.  
   • Pension Credit – SMI can start straight away.  

2. You own the home you live in (or have a shared-ownership mortgage) and the loan is secured against that home.

3. The loan the DWP will cover is within the limit  
   • Up to £200,000 of outstanding mortgage or eligible home-improvement loan.  
   • If you get Pension Credit, the limit is £100,000.

4. You agree to the loan conditions  
   • SMI is paid straight to your lender.  
   • A second-charge is placed on your property.  
   • You repay the SMI loan (plus interest) when you sell or transfer your home, or earlier if you choose.

5. Your mortgage is not already in a “mortgage holiday” or similar payment-break arrangement when SMI is due to start.

Other points to note

• SMI helps with interest only; you still have to pay anything towards the capital, insurance or arrears yourself.  
• Being in paid work does not automatically stop SMI, but the amount you get under Universal Credit can change as your earnings change.  
• You can stop taking SMI at any time by telling the DWP.

If you meet the conditions, contact the office that pays your qualifying benefit and ask for an SMI loan application.

Full guidance: GOV.UK – Support for Mortgage Interest  
https://www.gov.uk/support-for-mortgage-interest

prompt: I'm a landlord in England and my tenant has an excluded tenancy. What do I need to do if they refuse to leave after giving them reasonable notice?
generated_response:
When a tenant has an ‘excluded tenancy’ (for example, a lodger who shares your home) you do not need a court order to end the tenancy.  You must still give reasonable notice and carry out the eviction peace­fully.

What to do if they will not leave:

1. Check the notice you gave  
   • It should be in writing.  
   • The length must be ‘reasonable’ – usually the same as their rental period (for example, one week’s notice if they pay weekly, one month if they pay monthly).  

2. Keep evidence of the notice  
   • Date-stamped copy of the letter, email or text.  
   • Proof it was delivered or handed to them.  

3. Wait until the notice period has ended  
   • Do not try to remove them before the final day.  

4. Evict peace­fully  
   • You may change the locks once the notice period has expired and the occupier is out of the property (for example while they are at work).  
   • Do not use or threaten force, and do not remove them physically. Using force can amount to illegal eviction.  

5. Deal with their belongings  
   • If anything is left behind, store it safely and give them a reasonable time to collect it.  
   • Put your request to collect in writing and keep a record.  

6. Refund any deposit (minus agreed deductions)  
   • Return it promptly and in writing explain any deductions for damage or unpaid rent.  

7. If the occupier refuses to leave or you feel at risk  
   • Contact the police if there is a breach of the peace or threats of violence.  
   • Consider speaking to a solicitor or your local council’s tenancy relations officer for advice.  

Key guidance  
GOV.UK – Evicting excluded occupiers (lodgers and people living in your home):  
https://www.gov.uk/private-renting-evictions/evicting-excluded-occupiers

prompt: I'm selling a home that I rent out to tenants. What do I need to do about the tenants?
generated_response:
In Scotland you have two basic options when you decide to sell a rented property:

Option A – Sell with the tenants still living there  
The tenancy carries on exactly as it is; the buyer becomes the new landlord.
1. Tell the tenants in writing that you are putting the property on the market and that their tenancy rights stay the same.  
2. Make sure your sales particulars say the property is being sold “with tenants in situ” and give the buyer copies of:
   • the written tenancy agreement  
   • safety certificates (gas, electrical, fire alarms)  
   • the current rent statement and any arrears record  
   • deposit protection details (which approved scheme, amount, date protected)  
3. On completion, transfer the protected deposit to the buyer’s nominated scheme account within 30 working days and give the tenants the updated deposit certificate.  
4. Give the buyer your landlord registration number and any repairing-standard compliance paperwork.  

Option B – Sell the property vacant  
You can only end a Private Residential Tenancy (PRT) by using the correct eviction ground and notice.
1. Serve a written Notice to Leave on each tenant, citing Ground 1 (you intend to sell).  
   • 28 days’ notice if the tenant has lived there 6 months or less.  
   • 84 days’ notice if they have lived there more than 6 months.  
2. Include evidence of your intention to sell (for example, a letter from an estate agent or a Home Report).  
3. Keep paying for all landlord safety checks and repairs until the tenancy legally ends.  
4. If the tenants do not move out after the notice period, you must apply to the First-tier Tribunal (Housing and Property Chamber) for an eviction order—you cannot remove them yourself.  
5. Once the tenants leave, return their deposit within 10 working days of agreeing any deductions and then complete the sale in the usual way.  

Until the sale completes you remain responsible for:
• registering as a landlord with the local council  
• protecting the deposit in an approved scheme  
• gas, electrical and fire-alarm safety compliance  
• repairs that fall under the Repairing Standard  

If you are unsure about the notice periods, evidence needed or tribunal process, get advice from a qualified letting agent, a solicitor or a free service such as Shelter Scotland’s helpline.

For general guidance on housing and landlord duties visit: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I'm considering renting a property in England, but I heard about a scheme called Rent to Buy. Is this scheme still available, and how does it work?
generated_response:
Yes. Rent to Buy is still running in England, but only on new-build homes that a housing association or other registered provider has chosen to let under the scheme. There is no central application form – you apply directly to the landlord that advertises a Rent to Buy home.

How it works  
1. You rent the home at about 80% of the local market rent (sometimes called “intermediate rent”).  
2. The tenancy usually lasts up to 5 years. The lower rent is meant to help you save a deposit.  
3. At any point after the first 6 months (and usually by year 5), you can:  
   • buy the home outright, or  
   • buy a share through Shared Ownership and buy more shares later.  
4. If you decide not to buy by the end of the agreed period, the landlord can offer you another option (for example, extend the rent period or move you to a market-rent tenancy).

Basic eligibility  
• You must be a first-time buyer (or returning buyer who no longer owns a home).  
• Household income must normally be £80,000 a year or less outside London (£90,000 or less in London).  
• You need to show you can afford the rent and are aiming to save for a deposit.

Finding a Rent to Buy home  
1. Check housing association websites, local estate agents and property search sites for “Rent to Buy” or “Intermediate Rent” listings in the area you want.  
2. When you see a property, contact the landlord and complete their application form (proof of income, ID and credit checks are standard).  
3. If accepted, you sign an assured shorthold tenancy agreement at the reduced rent.

Other options to consider  
• Shared Ownership – buy a share straight away and pay rent on the rest.  
• First Homes – new-build properties sold at a discount to first-time buyers.  
• Help to Buy: Mortgage Guarantee – can help with a 5% deposit.

Where to read more  
GOV.UK overview of affordable home-ownership schemes (including Rent to Buy):  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

If you need help finding local providers, ask your council’s housing team or check Homes England’s “find a home” search tools on GOV.UK.

prompt: I live in a council home in Wales and I'm considering buying it. Can I apply for the Right to Buy scheme with my spouse, who also lives with me but isn't part of the same tenancy?
generated_response:
You cannot use Right to Buy in Wales any more. 

• The Welsh Government ended the Right to Buy and Right to Acquire for council and housing-association homes on 26 January 2019.  
• Because the scheme has closed, neither you nor your spouse can apply, even if you are the tenant and even if you applied together.

What you can do instead

1. Speak to your landlord (the council or housing association) to ask:
   • whether they plan to sell any homes on the open market, or  
   • whether they run other low-cost home-ownership schemes.

2. Look at Welsh Government schemes such as:
   • Help to Buy – Wales (for new-build homes)  
   • Shared Ownership – Wales.

3. If you still want to buy a property, get independent financial and legal advice before making any commitment.

More housing guidance is on GOV.UK at https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services, but full details of Welsh schemes are on the Welsh Government’s housing pages.

prompt: How do I find out if my local council provides pest control services in Wales?
generated_response:
Most councils list their pest control services online or will tell you by phone.

1) Go to https://www.gov.uk/find-local-council and enter your postcode.  
2) Follow the link to your council’s website.  
3) Look for a section called ‘Pest control’, ‘Environmental health’ or ‘Public protection’. It should say whether the council treats pests, which pests are covered (for example rats, mice, wasps), and the charges.  
4) If you cannot find the information online, phone the council’s environmental health or customer services number (shown on the same page) and ask whether they offer pest control and how to book.

If your council does not provide the service, they can usually give you details of local professional pest controllers.

prompt: I'm a landlord in Wales and I received a civil penalty notice for not checking my tenant's right to rent. How can I challenge this decision?
generated_response:
A civil penalty for a “right to rent” breach can be challenged, but you must act quickly.

1. Check the basics  
   • Right to Rent only applies to property let in England. If your rental is in Wales, the Home Office should not have issued a penalty – that is usually enough to overturn it.  
   • Note the “date of service” on the notice. You normally have 28 days from that date to object.

2. Gather evidence  
   • Proof the property is in Wales (land registry title, council tax bill, tenancy agreement showing the address).  
   • If the property is in England, collect the checks you did carry out, any correspondence with the tenant, and copies of their documents.

3. Send a written objection to the Home Office Civil Penalty Compliance Team  
   • State that you are objecting under section 23(2) of the Immigration Act 2014.  
   • Explain why the penalty is wrong (for example, the property is in Wales, or you carried out the checks).  
   • Include copies of your evidence.  
   • Keep a copy and send the objection by recorded delivery or another tracked method.

4. Await the Home Office response  
   The Home Office can:  
   • cancel the penalty,  
   • reduce the amount, or  
   • confirm it.  
   You will get a written decision.

5. If the penalty is upheld, you can appeal to a county court  
   • You must do this within 28 days of the Home Office decision letter.  
   • Complete form N161 (“Appellant’s notice”) and lodge it with the county court named in the decision letter, paying the court fee (you can apply for help with fees if needed).  
   • The court will set a hearing date where you can present your case.

6. Pay or arrange instalments if you decide not to appeal  
   If you choose not to appeal, contact the Home Office to agree a payment plan before the due date to avoid enforcement action.

Need more help?  
• Read the Home Office guidance on “Objections and Appeals against Civil Penalties” (search on GOV.UK).  
• If you are unsure, get specialist advice from a solicitor experienced in immigration compliance or landlord advice services such as the NRLA.

Relevant GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I want to know how I can get a more precise record of my property boundary in Wales. What options are available to me if I need to resolve a dispute with my neighbor?
generated_response:
You can usually sort a boundary problem without going to court, but you will need better evidence than the small-scale plan that comes with most title deeds.  
Here are the main options open to you in Wales.

1. Order the Land Registry documents  
   • Go to GOV.UK and search “property boundaries”.  
   • Use HM Land Registry’s online service to download an official copy of your title register and title plan (£3 each).  
   • Do the same for your neighbour’s title.  
   • These plans follow the “general boundaries” rule, so the red line is only a guide – it is not exact to the centimetre.

2. Check any older deeds or plans you already hold  
   Deeds prepared before Land Registry first registered the property sometimes contain large-scale, hand-drawn plans or written descriptions that can narrow down the line.

3. Commission a chartered land surveyor  
   • A surveyor can carry out a measured survey, mark the line on the ground and prepare a detailed scaled plan.  
   • Choose someone who is a member of RICS or another recognised professional body.  
   • Share the survey with your neighbour to see if you can reach agreement.

4. Make a boundary agreement with your neighbour  
   • If you both accept the line, sign a simple boundary agreement or deed.  
   • Send it to HM Land Registry with form AP1 and the fee (currently £40) so it is noted on both titles.  
   • This does not change ownership – it just records what you both agree the boundary is.

5. Apply for a “determined boundary” if you want Land Registry to show the exact line  
   • Complete form DB, attach the surveyor’s plan and submit it with form AP1 and the fee (£90).  
   • Land Registry will tell your neighbour and give them a chance to object.  
   • If everyone agrees, the plan is added to both titles and becomes conclusive.  
   • If there is an objection, the matter can be referred to the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) for a decision.

6. Try mediation before going to a tribunal or court  
   • Your local authority may offer free or low-cost neighbour mediation.  
   • RICS also runs an independent boundary dispute mediation service.  
   • Mediation is quicker and cheaper than formal proceedings and can help preserve good neighbour relations.

7. Court or tribunal as a last resort  
   If mediation fails and the dispute affects your property rights or value, you can apply to the First-tier Tribunal or the county court for a ruling. Get independent legal advice first, as costs can be high.

Key GOV.UK link  
Your property boundaries – https://www.gov.uk/your-property-boundaries

If you need more help understanding the forms or the cost, contact HM Land Registry’s customer support or speak to a solicitor who specialises in property law.

prompt: Can I use the Rent to Buy scheme in England if I live in London?
generated_response:
Rent to Buy is only funded by Homes England for homes outside Greater London, so you cannot use that particular scheme if you live in London.

What you can do instead  
1. Look at London Living Rent – a Mayor of London scheme that lets you rent at a discount and build up savings to buy later.  
2. Check Shared Ownership homes in London, which let you buy a share and pay rent on the rest.  
3. Visit the GLA’s Homes for Londoners search tool or speak to local housing associations to see what’s available in your borough.

General housing help is listed at: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I'm renting a mobile home on a park and I received a written agreement. How long do I have to review it before signing and what should I check for?
generated_response:
Time to review

• In most cases you must be given at least 28 days to look over the written statement (agreement) before you are asked to sign it or pay anything other than a small deposit.  
• You can choose to shorten the 28-day period, but you must do that in writing. Do not feel pressured to waive the time if you are not ready.

Key points to check

1. Names, address and pitch details  
   - Make sure the site owner’s details, the pitch number and the park address are correct.

2. Pitch fee and other charges  
   - Initial pitch fee amount.  
   - How and when the fee can be increased (the review process and notice period).  
   - Any separate charges for utilities, services, council tax or maintenance.

3. Length and security of the agreement  
   - Most park-home agreements in England are open-ended and give you security of tenure under the Mobile Homes Act 1983.  
   - Check that the agreement states your right to keep the home on the pitch unless a tribunal or court orders otherwise.

4. Site rules and your obligations  
   - Quiet hours, parking, pets, age-restrictions and any rules you must follow.  
   - Penalties for breaking rules.

5. The site owner’s obligations  
   - Standards for maintaining roads, lighting, water, drainage, common areas and safety checks (e.g. electrical, gas, fire points).

6. Utilities and meters  
   - Whether utilities are separately metered to your pitch and the tariff you will be charged.  
   - Your right to see meter readings and how often you will be billed.

7. Selling, gifting or leaving the home  
   - Your right to sell the home on the pitch and what commission (if any) the site owner can claim.  
   - Procedure if you want to assign the agreement or leave permanently.

8. Termination and eviction  
   - Grounds on which the site owner could end the agreement.  
   - Notice periods and the role of the First-tier Tribunal in any dispute.

9. Dispute resolution  
   - Any stated process for complaints and escalation to the tribunal or court.

10. Guarantees, inventories and deposits  
   - Record any inventory or condition schedule of the home and pitch.  
   - Make sure any deposit amount and terms for its return are written in.

If anything is unclear

• Ask the site owner for clarification in writing.  
• Take independent advice before you sign – for example from Citizens Advice or the Leasehold Advisory Service (LEASE) Park Homes team.  
• Do not sign or waive the 28-day period until you are satisfied.

Further information: GOV.UK guide ‘Park homes: your rights and responsibilities’ (https://www.gov.uk/park-mobile-homes).

prompt: I need to find a service to dispose of old batteries and chemicals safely. How do I locate the correct local service for this in England?
generated_response:
Most household batteries and chemicals count as hazardous waste, so your local council arranges safe disposal.  

1. Go to GOV.UK → Housing and local services (https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services) and choose ‘Find your local council’.  
2. Enter your postcode. This takes you to your council’s website.  
3. Look for pages titled ‘Household waste recycling centre’, ‘hazardous waste’, or ‘battery recycling’. They usually show:  
   • supermarket or library drop-off points for ordinary household batteries  
   • which recycling centres take car batteries, paint, solvents, garden chemicals, cleaning fluids, etc.  
   • whether you need to book a slot or request a special collection.  
4. Follow the instructions given (for example, keeping chemicals in their original, sealed containers and transporting them upright).  
5. If you can’t find the information or have large or unusual quantities, phone the council’s waste service and ask for the hazardous waste desk. They can confirm the nearest site or arrange a collection.  

For business or school waste you must use a licensed hazardous-waste contractor instead of the household service.

prompt: Can anyone other than the person who made the application appeal a minor commercial development decision?
generated_response:
No. In England only the person (or company) that made the planning application can lodge an appeal with the Planning Inspectorate against a council’s decision on a minor commercial development.

If you are a neighbour, local business or other third-party:

• You can send comments (supporting or objecting) while the application is being considered.  
• Once the council has decided, you cannot appeal simply because you disagree with the outcome.  
• Your options after the decision are limited to:  
  – asking the council to take enforcement action if you believe the development is not being built as approved; or  
  – seeking legal advice about a judicial review in the High Court if you think the council’s decision was unlawful (this must be done quickly and can be costly).

For the official rules, see “Appeal a planning decision” on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/appeal-planning-decision

prompt: How can I find out if I am eligible for legal aid during an eviction process?
generated_response:
You can check your legal aid position in a few minutes.

1. Use the online legal aid checker  
   Go to GOV.UK and search ‘check legal aid’ (or go straight to https://www.gov.uk/check-legal-aid).  
   • Choose ‘housing’ and then ‘eviction or repossession’.  
   • Answer the questions on where you live, what help you need and your money (income, benefits, savings).  
   • The checker will tell you straight away if you’re likely to qualify.

2. Gather the financial evidence you will need  
   • Last 3 months’ payslips or benefit letters.  
   • Recent bank statements for all accounts.  
   • Proof of rent or mortgage, childcare costs and any maintenance you pay.  
   • National Insurance number and photo ID.

3. Contact a legal aid housing adviser or solicitor  
   • Use the ‘Find a legal aid adviser’ tool that appears at the end of the checker, or call firms directly and ask if they have a housing legal aid contract.  
   • In Wales you can also phone Civil Legal Advice on 0345 345 4 345 (textphone 18001 0345 609 6677). They will do a means test and, if you’re eligible, refer you to a specialist.  
   • If court papers have already been issued, ask for emergency legal aid so advice can start straight away.

4. If you have a court hearing today  
   • Go to the court and ask for the Housing Possession Court Duty Scheme. A legal aid solicitor will be on duty and can advise and represent you for free, whatever your income.  
   • Take all letters, court papers and evidence of your finances with you.

Key points to remember  

• Eviction (possession) cases usually meet the ‘merits’ test for legal aid automatically—only the financial test matters.  
• You will not have to pay anything up front if you qualify, but you may have to repay some or all costs from any money you win or keep.  
• If the online checker is unclear, call Civil Legal Advice or a local legal aid solicitor and ask them to run a full assessment.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/check-legal-aid

prompt: I'm a landlord in Scotland and I received a civil penalty notice for not checking my tenant's right to rent. How can I object to the penalty?
generated_response:
Right to Rent checks only apply to property let in England. Landlords in Scotland do not have a legal duty to carry them out, so you should not normally receive a civil penalty for a Scottish tenancy.

What to do:

1. Check the notice is genuine  
   • The Home Office is the only body that can issue a Right to Rent civil penalty.  
   • If the notice came from anyone else, treat it as a potential scam and report it to Action Fraud (0300 123 2040).

2. If the notice is from the Home Office, write back within 28 days of the date on the penalty notice:  
   • State that the property is in Scotland and therefore outside the scope of the Right to Rent scheme.  
   • Include the tenancy address, the date you received the notice and any supporting documents (for example, the tenancy agreement).  
   • Send your objection to the address or email given in the notice. You can also use the Home Office “Objection to penalty notice” form:  
     https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/objection-to-penalty-notice-immigration

3. Keep copies of everything you send and any delivery receipts.

4. Wait for the Home Office decision.  
   • They must confirm, reduce or cancel the penalty.  
   • If they confirm the penalty and you still disagree, you have a further 28 days to appeal to the First-tier Tribunal (Immigration and Asylum Chamber). There is no fee for this appeal.

If at any stage you are unsure whether the notice is genuine or need help preparing your objection, contact the Home Office Civil Penalty Compliance Team using the details on the notice, or speak to a qualified housing or immigration adviser.

prompt: I'm 17 and have been homeless for a few weeks. How does Children's Services help me find a place to live and what options are available if returning home isn't safe?
generated_response:
If you are 17, the law treats you as a “child in need” if you have nowhere safe to live. Your local council’s Children’s Services (sometimes called Social Services) holds the main duty to make sure you have suitable accommodation and support.

What Children’s Services must do  
1. Assess your situation straight away. Ring the council’s Social Services duty team (there is usually a 24-hour number on the council’s website) or visit in person and say you are 17 and homeless.  
2. Carry out a ‘child in need’ assessment. They look at why you are homeless, any risks (for example, violence or exploitation) and what help you need.  
3. Offer emergency accommodation the same day if you have nowhere safe that night. This might be:  
   • a foster placement or supported lodgings  
   • a place in a supported hostel or foyer for young people  
   • in rare cases, a B&B for a short time while something better is found  
4. Decide whether to accommodate you under section 76 of the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014 (similar to section 20 in England). If you agree, you become a “looked-after child”. That gives you:  
   • a safe place to live until at least age 18  
   • a personal adviser and a pathway plan for education, training and health  
   • ongoing “care leaver” support up to age 21 (or 25 if you stay in education or training).  
5. If you do not want to be looked after, Children’s Services must still help you secure suitable housing and support you to claim benefits such as Universal Credit.

If going home is unsafe  
• Children’s Services will look at mediation only if it is safe and you agree.  
• They must not press you to return where there is violence, abuse or exploitation.  
• Tell them clearly why it is unsafe so this is recorded in your assessment.

Other help you can ask for  
• A social worker to attend any housing appointments with you.  
• Referral to a specialist youth homelessness or domestic abuse service.  
• Help with travel, food and essential items until benefits start.  
• Support to stay in education or training (travel costs, equipment, bursaries).

What to do now  
1. Contact your local council’s Social Services duty (or out-of-hours) team today and ask for an urgent homelessness assessment.  
2. Keep notes of who you speak to, times, and what was agreed.  
3. If you are turned away, ask for the decision in writing and contact Shelter Cymru’s free helpline on 08000 495 495 for immediate advice.  
4. In an emergency, call 999 or present at any police station for help getting through to Social Services.

Useful link  
GOV.UK Housing and local services hub: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

If you feel uncertain or are not getting the help you need, contact Shelter Cymru or speak to a trusted adult (teacher, GP, youth worker) who can escalate your case with the council.

prompt: If I get a Migration Notice, do I need to do anything else besides switching to Universal Credit?
generated_response:
A Migration Notice means your current benefit is ending and you must claim Universal Credit (UC) by the deadline in the letter. The main action is to make that claim, but there are a few extra points to check so you keep getting paid on time.

1. Read the notice carefully and note the ‘deadline day’ (usually 3 months from the date on the letter). Missing it means your existing benefit will stop and you could lose transitional protection.

2. Make your Universal Credit claim before the deadline.  
   • Apply online at GOV.UK or call the UC Migration Notice helpline if you cannot apply online or need an extension.  
   • If you live with a partner, you must make a joint claim even if only one of you received the letter.

3. Complete everything the claim asks for:  
   • Prove your identity (online or at the Jobcentre).  
   • Add details of rent, childcare costs, savings and earnings so UC can calculate your payment.  
   • Book and attend the first interview where you accept your ‘claimant commitment’.

4. Keep reporting any change in circumstances (work hours, income, moving home, relationship changes) straight to Universal Credit from now on. You no longer report these to the legacy benefit office.

5. Check your first UC statement. If you claimed before the deadline and your old benefit was higher, it should include a ‘transitional protection’ top-up so you’re not worse off at the point of transfer. Contact UC promptly if it looks wrong.

6. Your previous benefit(s) will stop automatically once the UC claim is in place—you do not need to cancel them yourself. Keep any letters you receive, in case there are queries or overpayment checks later.

If you can’t claim by the deadline, call the number on the Migration Notice straight away—they can give you up to one extra month in some situations.

For the claim form and more detail, go to: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: My child is doing a Foundation Apprenticeship in Scotland. Do I still get Child Benefit?
generated_response:
Yes. A Foundation Apprenticeship in Scotland counts as ‘approved education’ for Child Benefit purposes, so you can normally keep receiving Child Benefit while your child is on the course, as long as they:

• are under 20, and  
• are not being paid a wage for the apprenticeship (Foundation Apprenticeships are unpaid).

What you need to do:

1. If your child has just turned 16, HMRC will have sent you a form or an online link asking about their education or training. Tick or select the option for ‘Foundation Apprenticeship (Scotland)’ and return it.  
2. If you have already replied, you do not need to do anything else—payments will carry on until the course ends or your child turns 20, whichever is sooner.  
3. Tell the Child Benefit Office straight away if your child stops the apprenticeship, starts full-time paid work, claims certain benefits themselves, or turns 20. This prevents overpayments you might have to repay later.

If you are unsure, contact the Child Benefit helpline (the number is on any recent award letter) or see the Child Benefit section of GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I recently started a new relationship and moved in with my partner. Do I need to report this change to HMRC and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
It depends on whether you get any HMRC-run benefits or allowances.

1. Tax Credits (Working Tax Credit or Child Tax Credit)  
   • Yes – you must tell HMRC within 1 month when you start living with a partner.  
   • Call the Tax Credits helpline on 0345 300 3900 or use the online “manage your tax credits” service.  
   • Your single claim will end and you’ll need to make a new joint claim. HMRC will confirm what evidence they need (for example, income details for you both).

2. Child Benefit  
   • Moving in with a partner does not usually change who gets Child Benefit, but you must update:  
     – your new address (if you moved)  
     – any change to the bank account that receives the payments.  
   • Update details through your online Personal Tax Account or call the Child Benefit helpline on 0300 200 3100.

3. Marriage Allowance  
   • If either of you already claims Marriage Allowance, check that the new living arrangements haven’t changed who is the lower earner or whether you still want to share the allowance. You can change or cancel it in your Personal Tax Account.

4. PAYE tax code  
   • If you now share a company car, medical insurance or similar benefit provided through your partner’s employer, tell HMRC so your tax codes stay correct.

5. Universal Credit or other DWP benefits  
   • These are run by the Department for Work and Pensions, not HMRC, but you must also report a new partner to them if you claim UC, ESA, PIP, etc. Use your online account or call the relevant helpline.

If you are unsure which benefits you receive, check your Personal Tax Account on GOV.UK or ring the helplines above. Update HMRC as soon as possible to avoid overpayments or penalties.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I'm a nurse and I'm temporarily unable to work because of a serious illness. How do I find out what benefits I could be entitled to and how much I might get?
generated_response:
If illness stops you working, there are a few benefits you can check. The easiest way to see what you qualify for (and roughly how much) is to use an online benefits calculator, then follow up with a phone call or local advice service if you prefer speaking to someone.

1. Gather basic details before you start  
   • recent payslips or proof of other income  
   • savings amount (if any)  
   • rent or mortgage costs  
   • details of the illness and how long you expect to be off work

2. Use a benefits calculator  
   • Turn2us: 0808 802 2000 (they can do the calculation over the phone if online is difficult)  
   • entitledto: www.entitledto.co.uk  
   These tools ask the questions once and list everything you could claim, with estimated amounts.

3. Check the main benefits for people off sick  
   • Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) – paid by employers for up to 28 weeks if you earn at least £123 a week before tax. Current rate is £116.75 a week.  
   • New-style Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) – claim if SSP has ended or you do not qualify for it. Based on your National Insurance record and can be paid even if you have savings.  
   • Universal Credit – helps with living costs or rent if household income is now low. (You can get this alongside new-style ESA.)  
   • Personal Independence Payment (PIP) – under State Pension age and have day-to-day difficulties caused by the illness.  
   • Attendance Allowance – if you are already over State Pension age and need help with personal care or supervision.  
   • Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit – for illnesses or injuries caused by work (for example, some infectious diseases caught while nursing).  
   • Council Tax Reduction – most councils give a discount if your income has dropped.

4. How to apply  
   • SSP – ask your employer’s payroll or HR team. Provide a fit note if they need one.  
   • ESA – phone 0800 055 6688 or download the ESA1 form from GOV.UK.  
   • Universal Credit – apply online, or call the UC helpline if you need help.  
   • PIP – start a claim by calling 0800 917 2222.  
   • Attendance Allowance – download the form or ring 0800 731 0122 for a paper copy.  
   • Industrial Injuries – claim form BI100 from GOV.UK or the Barnsley IIDB Centre (0800 121 8379).  
   • Council Tax Reduction – contact your local council.

5. Get free help with the forms  
   • Citizens Advice Cymru: 0800 702 2020  
   • Macmillan Welfare Rights (if the illness is cancer-related): 0808 808 0000  
   • Your hospital’s welfare‐rights or occupational health team (common in NHS trusts).

6. Check other support  
   • NHS Pension Scheme may offer ill-health retirement or temporary injury allowance—speak to your NHS pensions officer.  
   • Some unions (e.g. RCN) provide hardship grants.

Key link  
GOV.UK benefits hub: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

If you are unsure which benefit applies to your exact age or circumstances, start with the benefits calculator or ring Citizens Advice—they will talk you through the next steps.

prompt: I'm trying to apply for free school meals for my child, but I'm not sure which benefits I qualify for. How do I check if I'm eligible and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
Free school meals are arranged through your local council. The council checks which benefits you get and whether your income is low enough.

Benefits that usually qualify
• Universal Credit – if your household’s ‘take-home’ income is £7,400 a year or less (after tax and not counting benefits)  
• Income Support  
• Income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance (JSA)  
• Income-related Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)  
• Child Tax Credit (but not Working Tax Credit) and your annual income is below £16,190  
• Working Tax Credit ‘run-on’ (the 4 weeks after you stop qualifying for Working Tax Credit)  
• Guarantee element of Pension Credit  
• Support under Part 6 of the Immigration and Asylum Act 1999

If you get Working Tax Credit (apart from the 4-week run-on) or your income is above the limits, you will not normally qualify.

What to do

1. Find your council’s application page  
   Go to https://www.gov.uk/apply-free-school-meals and enter your postcode. It will take you to the right council webpage or give you a phone number.

2. Check the list of qualifying benefits shown on the council site and make sure you receive one of them.  
   • Have recent benefit letters, a Universal Credit statement, or a tax-credit award notice to hand.

3. Complete the online form (or paper form if offered).  
   • You will need:  
     – your National Insurance number (or NASS number if you have one)  
     – your child’s name, date of birth and school  
     – details of the benefit you receive

4. Submit the form. Most councils verify your benefit status automatically with the Department for Work and Pensions or HMRC, so you rarely need to send proof unless they ask.

5. Wait for confirmation. The council or the school will tell you if your child has been approved and the start date. Meals are usually free from the date the claim is approved (they are not backdated).

If you are unsure which benefit you receive or your income limit, call the free school meals team at your council. They can check instantly once they have your National Insurance number.

Still uncertain?
Bring your latest benefit award notice to the school office or contact a local advice centre; they can check the figures and help fill in the form.

prompt: If I'm over State Pension age and living with my partner who hasn't reached State Pension age, can I still claim Housing Benefit?
generated_response:
It depends on whether you and your partner are already getting Housing Benefit.

1. New claims  
   • Since 15 May 2019, couples where one person is over State Pension age and the other is below it (known as a ‘mixed-age couple’) can no longer make a new claim for Housing Benefit.  
   • Instead, you normally need to claim Universal Credit for help with rent. The claim must be made jointly, and both of you are treated as working-age for benefit purposes.

2. Existing claims  
   • If you were already getting Housing Benefit as a couple before 15 May 2019, you can usually keep receiving it as long as you remain eligible and there’s no break in your claim.  
   • A break of more than one week, or moving to a different local authority area, can end the protection and you’d have to claim Universal Credit instead.

3. Alternative help with housing costs  
   • If you own your home and need help with mortgage interest, you may be able to apply for Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI) instead.  
   • If you (not your partner) are eligible for Pension Credit and the claim started before 15 May 2019, you may still have Housing Benefit linked to that award.

Next steps  
• If you need to make a new claim, start a Universal Credit application as soon as possible.  
• If you’re unsure whether your existing Housing Benefit is protected, ask your local council’s benefits team.  
• For detailed guidance, see GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

If your situation is complex or you think you should still be entitled, get free advice from Citizens Advice or another independent benefits adviser.

prompt: I'm the widow of someone who worked for the National Coal Board. How do I apply for the National Concessionary Fuel Scheme and get the cash allowance for fuel?
generated_response:
You apply through the National Concessionary Fuel Scheme’s administrator, the Concessionary Fuel Office (CFO).

1. Check you have the key details:
   • Your late partner’s full name, National Insurance number and, if you know it, their mineworker or pension reference  
   • Your marriage or civil-partnership certificate and your partner’s death certificate  
   • Your own National Insurance number and bank account details (the cash allowance is paid straight into your account)

2. Ask the CFO for an application pack:
   • Phone 0345 759 0529 (Monday–Friday, 8.30 am–5 pm)  
   • Email fuel@coalpensionservices.co.uk  
   • Or write to:  
     Concessionary Fuel Office  
     Coal Pension Services Ltd  
     17 Union Street  
     Sheffield S1 2FH  

3. Fill in the form they send you and return it with photocopies of the documents they ask for. Keep copies for your records.
