4. The CFO will confirm whether you qualify and, if you do, set up the cash allowance. Payments are usually made every three months and are taxable, so they will send you a statement for your records.

If you are not sure whether your late partner had an entitlement, still contact the CFO – they can check the National Coal Board pension records for you.

For the official guidance, search GOV.UK for “National Concessionary Fuel Scheme”.

prompt: What are the options for getting help and advice if I'm struggling with my housing benefit decision?
generated_response:
If you think the Housing Benefit decision is wrong or you do not understand it, there are free places you can turn to for help.

1. Ask your council to explain the decision  
   • Phone or visit the Housing Benefit team shown on your decision letter.  
   • You can ask for a written statement of reasons and, if you still disagree, ask for a “mandatory reconsideration”.  
   • There is usually a 1-month time limit, so do this as soon as you can.

2. Citizens Advice  
   • They give free, confidential advice on benefits and appeals.  
   • Find your nearest office or start an online chat by entering your postcode at citizensadvice.org.uk.

3. Shelter  
   • Shelter’s housing advisers can help with benefit problems that affect your rent.  
   • Call their free helpline on 0808 800 4444 or use webchat at shelter.org.uk.

4. Local welfare rights or law centre  
   • Many councils fund welfare-rights units or partner law centres that can check your entitlement and help with appeals.  
   • Ask the council, your school or youth worker if there is one in your area.

5. Benefits calculators online  
   • A benefits calculator can show if you are getting the right amount.  
   • Try the Turn2us, Policy in Practice or entitledto calculators (all free).

6. Appeal to a tribunal (if the council keeps the decision)  
   • If the mandatory reconsideration is not in your favour, you can appeal to an independent tribunal within 1 month of the new decision.  
   • An adviser can fill in the SSCS1 appeal form and, if needed, come with you.

Extra support if you are under 18  
• You can ask a trusted adult—parent, carer, teacher or social worker—to speak to the council or an advice charity on your behalf.  
• If you are at school or college, a student support officer may also be able to help you contact an adviser.

Full guidance and links to Housing Benefit, reconsiderations and appeals are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I live in Wales and am expecting my first child. Can I still get the Sure Start Maternity Grant, or do I have to apply for something else?
generated_response:
Yes – the Sure Start Maternity Grant is still available in Wales. It’s a one-off payment of £500 for your first child (or for twins/triplets if you already have children).

Who can get it  
You normally qualify if both apply:  
• this is the first child you will be responsible for (or you already have children but are expecting multiples), and  
• you or your partner already get one of these benefits: Universal Credit, Income Support, income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance, income-related Employment and Support Allowance, Pension Credit, Child Tax Credit (at a rate higher than the family element) or Working Tax Credit that includes a disability or severe-disability element.

When to claim  
From 11 weeks before the baby is due until 6 months after the birth.

How to claim  
1. Download form SF100 (Sure Start) or ask Jobcentre Plus for a paper copy.  
   GOV.UK link: https://www.gov.uk/sure-start-maternity-grant  
2. Fill in the form. A midwife, doctor or health visitor must sign Part 13 to confirm your pregnancy or the baby’s birth.  
3. Post the form to “Freepost DWP SSMG” (the address is on the form) or hand it in at your local Jobcentre Plus.  
4. You’ll get a decision letter; if approved the money is paid straight into your bank account.

If you’re refused and think that’s wrong, you can ask for a mandatory reconsideration within one month.

Need help?  
• Jobcentre Plus: 0800 169 0140 (Welsh language 0800 169 0240, textphone 0800 169 0286).  
• Citizens Advice can help you check benefit entitlement and fill in the form.

There is no separate Welsh scheme—you use the standard Sure Start Maternity Grant process.

prompt: I'm unsure whether I should appeal or complain about my tax credit decision. What's the difference?
generated_response:
Appeal or complain – what each one is for

1. Appeal  
Use an appeal when you believe the decision itself is wrong – for example the award is too low, you have been told you must repay an overpayment, or your claim has been stopped.

• First ask HMRC to look at the decision again (this is called a ‘mandatory reconsideration’). Do this within 30 days of the decision letter.  
• If you still disagree after the reconsideration, you can appeal to an independent tribunal. The tribunal will look at the facts and the law and can change the decision.

2. Complaint  
Make a complaint if you are unhappy with the way HMRC handled your case rather than the decision. Typical reasons include long delays, staff being unhelpful, losing documents, or giving unclear information.

• Complaints go through HMRC’s two-stage complaints process.  
• A complaint cannot change the award figure, but HMRC can apologise, speed things up, or in some cases offer compensation for poor service.

Can I do both?  
Yes. You can appeal to challenge the decision and also complain about the service if you need to.

Next steps

• Check the deadline on your decision letter and ask for a mandatory reconsideration straight away if you think the decision is wrong.  
• If you want to complain, write or phone HMRC’s Tax Credits Complaints team and explain what went wrong and how you’d like it put right.  
• Keep copies of letters, forms and notes of phone calls.

Full guidance and contact details are on GOV.UK – search “appeal a tax credits decision” or start at: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I'm expecting a baby and need to know what financial support I can claim. How do I check if I'm eligible for Maternity or Paternity Leave or Pay, or Maternity Allowance?
generated_response:
Here is the quickest way to check which payments or leave you (or your partner) might qualify for and what to do next.

1. Work out who is claiming what  
   • The pregnant person can claim either Statutory Maternity Pay (SMP) or Maternity Allowance.  
   • Their partner (including fathers and same-sex partners) can claim Statutory Paternity Leave and Pay.  
   • You may both be able to use Shared Parental Leave and Pay once the baby is born.

2. Check Statutory Maternity Pay (SMP)  
   The pregnant person is usually eligible if:  
   • they are an employee (not self-employed)  
   • they have worked for the same employer for at least 26 weeks by the end of the 15th week before the due date  
   • their average gross pay is at least £123 a week (2023/24 rate)  
   What to do: ask the employer’s HR or payroll team for form SMP1 or confirmation of entitlement. Full rules are on GOV.UK – search “Statutory Maternity Pay and Leave”.

3. Check Maternity Allowance (if SMP is refused)  
   Maternity Allowance is paid by Jobcentre Plus, usually when the pregnant person is self-employed or has not been with the same employer long enough for SMP.  
   • They must have worked (employed or self-employed) for at least 26 weeks in the 66 weeks before the due date and earned at least £30 a week in 13 of those weeks.  
   • Claim on form MA1 (download or order from GOV.UK).  
   GOV.UK page: gov.uk/maternity-allowance

4. Check Statutory Paternity Leave and Pay (for partners)  
   You can usually get one or two weeks’ paid leave if:  
   • you are employed and will be responsible for the child’s upbringing  
   • you have worked for your employer for at least 26 weeks by the 15th week before the due date  
   • your average gross pay is at least £123 a week.  
   Give your employer form SC3 at least 15 weeks before the due date.  
   GOV.UK page: gov.uk/paternity-pay-leave

5. Consider Shared Parental Leave (SPL)  
   After the first 2 weeks of maternity leave, up to 50 weeks of leave and 37 weeks of pay can be shared between the parents if both meet the work-history tests.  
   GOV.UK page: gov.uk/shared-parental-leave-and-pay

6. Use the online eligibility checker  
   GOV.UK has a simple checker for maternity, paternity and shared leave/pay: gov.uk/pay-leave-for-parents. It takes about five minutes and tells you exactly what you can claim.

7. Apply on time  
   • SMP: employer pays it with wages once they have the MAT B1 certificate (issued by the midwife or GP at 20+ weeks).  
   • Maternity Allowance: claim up to 11 weeks before the due date; payments go direct to the claimant’s bank.  
   • Paternity Pay: employer pays it with wages once they have form SC3.  

8. Other help once the baby arrives  
   • Child Benefit – claim within 3 months to avoid losing payments.  
   • If you’re on a low income, check Universal Credit, Healthy Start vouchers and Sure Start Maternity Grant (Scotland: Best Start Grants).

If any of the tests above are borderline, the safest next step is to use the GOV.UK checker or speak to your employer’s HR/payroll team. For Maternity Allowance questions call Jobcentre Plus on 0800 055 6688.

prompt: If I'm self-employed and report zero income, will I still get Universal Credit?
generated_response:
Yes, you can still claim Universal Credit (UC) if you are self-employed and your earnings are £0, but what you receive depends on whether the Minimum Income Floor (MIF) applies to you.

1. Monthly reporting  
   • You must report your self-employed income and expenses every month, even if the figure is £0.  
   • UC is then recalculated for that assessment period.

2. Minimum Income Floor (MIF)  
   • After you have been in “gainful self-employment” for more than 12 months, DWP usually applies the MIF.  
   • The MIF is an assumed income roughly equal to 35 hours a week at the National Living Wage (lower hours if you have caring responsibilities or health issues agreed with your work coach).  
   • If your actual earnings are below the MIF—or £0—the MIF amount is still used in the calculation, so your UC award may be much lower or even reduced to £0.

3. When the MIF does not apply  
   • Start-up period: You get one 12-month “start-up period” when you first enter self-employment while on UC. During this time your actual earnings (including £0) are used and you can keep business profits to reinvest.  
   • Not gainfully self-employed: If DWP decide your work is not “gainful” (for example, it is irregular or very low paid), the MIF is removed and you are treated like any other claimant looking for work.  
   • Exceptional circumstances: You can ask for a temporary MIF suspension if you have a significant drop in trade for reasons beyond your control (for example, illness). Your work coach decides this case-by-case.

4. What to do if your UC is reduced  
   • Ask your work coach to review whether the MIF should apply.  
   • Provide evidence of your business accounts, why income is currently £0, and any steps you are taking to increase earnings.  
   • If you think the decision is wrong, you can request a Mandatory Reconsideration.

5. Other help  
   • You may qualify for New Style Jobseeker’s Allowance or other support if you are looking for additional employment.  
   • Free, confidential advice is available from Citizens Advice or a welfare rights adviser.

Check the full rules on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: Where can I apply for Housing Benefit in Wales?
generated_response:
Apply through your local council.

1. Go to the Housing Benefit section of your council’s website (search “Housing Benefit” plus your council’s name).  
2. Use the online claim form or download a paper form if offered.  
3. If you need help or cannot apply online, call or visit the council’s benefits team—contact details are on the same web page.  
4. Send any evidence the council asks for (for example, proof of rent, income and savings) within the deadline they give you.

If you are making a brand-new claim for help with rent and you are not yet getting Housing Benefit, you’ll normally need to apply for Universal Credit instead. Check the guidance or speak to the council if you’re unsure.

General benefits information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: What happens if I move house while receiving Universal Credit?
generated_response:
Tell Universal Credit about your move as soon as you know the date you will leave the old address. Moving is a “change of circumstances” and can affect how much you’re paid, especially the housing element.

What you need to do

1. Sign in to your Universal Credit online account (or phone the helpline if you do not use the online service).
2. Select “Report a change of circumstances”, then “Where you live and what it costs”.
3. Enter:
   • the date you will leave your current home  
   • the new address and postcode  
   • whether you rent, own or live with someone else  
   • your new rent or mortgage costs and service charges  
   • the date you start being liable for those costs
4. Upload evidence when asked—usually a tenancy agreement, rent statement, or mortgage letter. Take clear photos or scans.
5. Keep paying your current rent until your tenancy ends. Universal Credit can cover housing costs for one home only, except in a few limited overlap situations (for example, if you have to pay rent on both homes for up to one month while you move).
6. Check your next Universal Credit statement to see the new housing element. If the amount looks wrong, send a message in your journal straight away.

Things to watch

• Local Housing Allowance (private renters) or social-rent rules may give you a different maximum amount at the new address, even within the same town.  
• If you move to a different local council area you’ll need to claim Council Tax Reduction from the new council separately—Universal Credit does not do this for you.  
• Moving in with a partner or anyone else who shares bills counts as another change; their income and savings may alter your award.  
• Missed reporting deadlines can lead to overpayments (which you must repay) or a gap in your housing costs, so tell DWP before or on the day you move if possible.

Where to find more information

Universal Credit: report changes of circumstances  
https://www.gov.uk/universal-credit/changes-of-circumstances

prompt: I live in Wales and I'm worried about my Council Tax bill. How can I check if I'm paying the correct amount and if I'm eligible for any reductions?
generated_response:
Here’s what to do.

1. Check your property’s Council Tax band  
   • Go to the Valuation Office Agency (VOA) search page on GOV.UK and enter your postcode.  
   • Make sure the band on screen matches the band shown on your latest bill.  
   • If you think the band is wrong, ask the VOA for a review (called making a “proposal”). They will tell you how to do this.

2. Find out what that band should cost in your area  
   • Look on your local council’s website or ring the Council Tax team and ask for this year’s charges for each band.  
   • Compare the figure for your band with the amount on your bill. Small differences can be due to community or police precepts, but large gaps are worth querying with the council.

3. Check if you qualify for standard discounts  
   You may get 25% off if you are the only adult living in the home (“single person discount”).  
   Other discounts apply if:  
   • all residents are students  
   • someone is classed as severely mentally impaired  
   • you are a live-in carer (not the partner or child of the person you care for)  
   • the property is empty or unfurnished for a set period.

4. Look at disability–related reductions  
   • If someone in the household needs an extra room, bathroom or space for a wheelchair because of a disability, you can ask for the “disabled band reduction”.  
   • This usually moves the bill down by one band.

5. Check the Council Tax Reduction (CTR) scheme for low-income households  
   • Wales runs a national CTR scheme. If you get Pension Credit, Universal Credit or have a low income or savings under £16,000 (unless you get Pension Credit Guarantee Credit), you may get help.  
   • The council will work out any reduction after you fill in a short form or apply online.

6. Ask about discretionary help  
   • If you are in financial hardship but do not meet the main rules, the council can consider a discretionary discount. This is decided case-by-case.

7. Apply or query by phone or post if you’re not confident online  
   • Explain which discount or reduction you think applies.  
   • Keep a note of the date, who you spoke to and any reference numbers.

8. Keep paying what you can while things are being checked  
   • This avoids arrears building up. If your bill is later reduced, the council will adjust what you owe.

Useful link  
Council Tax Reduction and other help: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

If you are unsure which form to use or need help filling it in, ring your council’s Council Tax team and ask them to post a paper form or talk you through it.

prompt: I'm a teacher who was injured during a training exercise while in the reserves. How do I claim compensation for my injury?
generated_response:
You claim through the Armed Forces Compensation Scheme (AFCS). The scheme covers regulars and reservists who are injured or become ill as a result of service, including training.

What you need  
1. Your service number, unit and dates of service.  
2. Details of what happened – date, place, nature of the exercise.  
3. Medical evidence (e.g. hospital report, GP notes, X-rays).  
4. Employment or wage details if the injury has affected your earnings.

Time limit  
You normally must claim within 7 years of the injury or of first noticing the condition.

How to claim  
1. Download claim form AFCS001 from GOV.UK (search ‘Armed Forces Compensation Scheme claim form’).  
2. Read the guidance notes, then complete the form – give as much detail as you can about the injury and its impact.  
3. Attach copies of your medical evidence.  
4. Send the form and evidence to:  
   Veterans UK  
   Norcross, Blackpool FY5 3WP  
   (The address is on the form. You can also email a scanned copy to dbs-afcs@mod.gov.uk.)  
5. Veterans UK will acknowledge your claim and may ask for extra information or arrange a medical assessment.  
6. They aim to decide simple cases within 6 months. You will get a letter telling you whether an award is made, what level, and your right to appeal.

If your injury worsens later, you can apply for a review.

Other help while you wait  
• Statutory Sick Pay or occupational sick pay from your teaching job.  
• Armed Forces charity support (e.g. SSAFA, Royal British Legion) for practical or financial help.

If you need advice on filling in the form or appealing a decision, contact the free Veterans UK helpline on 0808 1914 2 18 or speak to a welfare officer through your reserve unit.

prompt: I'm a nurse and I'm worried about my financial situation. Where can I get help?
generated_response:
You’ve got several places to turn for confidential, free help.

1. Check what state help you could claim  
   • Use a benefits calculator on GOV.UK to see if you can get Universal Credit, Council Tax Reduction, or help with childcare: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits  
   • In Wales you may also qualify for the Discretionary Assistance Fund if you face an emergency.

2. Look at money-guidance services  
   • MoneyHelper gives impartial budgeting tools and can point you to free debt advice: https://www.moneyhelper.org.uk  
   • Citizens Advice Cymru can check benefits, negotiate with creditors and explain your rights: call 0800 702 2020 or use their online chat.

3. Get profession-specific support  
   • Cavell Nurses’ Trust offers grants for registered nurses facing hardship: https://www.cavellnursestrust.org  
   • If you’re an RCN member, the RCN Welfare Service gives money advice and one-off grants: 0345 772 6100.  
   • NHS organisations in Wales run Employee Assistance Programmes that include financial counselling—ask your HR or intranet.

4. Speak to a free debt-advice charity early if you’re falling behind on bills  
   • StepChange: 0800 138 1111  
   • National Debtline: 0808 808 4000

5. Talk to lenders and utility suppliers once you have advice. Most will let you set up an affordable payment plan if you explain you’re getting debt-advice support.

If you’re unsure where to start, begin with MoneyHelper or Citizens Advice Cymru—they’ll outline all your options and refer you to specialist nursing grants if needed.

prompt: I think my tax credit decision is wrong, but I'm not sure how to proceed. What should I do?
generated_response:
If you think HMRC have got your tax credit decision wrong you can ask them to look at it again and, if needed, appeal.

1. Check the decision letter (award notice). Note the date on it – you normally have 30 days from that date to challenge the decision.

2. Gather any evidence that supports what you believe is wrong (for example payslips, childcare receipts, letters that show a change of circumstances).

3. Ask for a ‘mandatory reconsideration’.  
   • Phone the Tax Credit Helpline on 0345 300 3900 (or 0300 200 1900 from a mobile).  
   • Tell them you want a mandatory reconsideration and explain why the decision is wrong.  
   • They may ask you to fill in form TC846 or to write in. If so, send the form or letter within the 30-day time limit and keep a copy.

4. HMRC will send you a ‘Mandatory Reconsideration Notice’ telling you whether they have changed the decision.

5. If you still disagree, you can appeal to an independent tribunal.  
   • Complete form SSCS5 within 30 days of the date on the reconsideration notice.  
   • Attach the notice and any extra evidence.  
   • The tribunal is run by HM Courts and Tribunals Service, not HMRC.

6. Keep paying back any overpayment HMRC has already asked for, unless they tell you otherwise. Not paying could lead to extra charges.

7. If the deadline has passed or you find the process difficult, call the helpline and explain – HMRC can sometimes accept late requests if you have a good reason.

Free help is available from Citizens Advice or a local welfare rights service.

Full guidance and the forms are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/appeal-tax-credit-decision

prompt: My business has debts and I want to close it. What are my options for dealing with the debts before closing?
generated_response:
The steps depend on how your business is set up.  

1. Check your business structure  
   • Sole trader: you and the business are the same legal person, so the debts are your personal responsibility.  
   • Partnership: partners share liability (unless it is a limited liability partnership).  
   • Limited company: the company is a separate legal entity. Directors normally are not personally liable unless they have given personal guarantees or traded while insolvent.

2. List every debt  
   Write down each creditor, the amount owed and any security (for example, a loan secured on equipment). This helps you see which options fit.

3. Decide whether the business can repay anything  
   • If you can clear the debts in full, pay them before you close.  
   • If not, move to a formal insolvency option.

4. Options for sole traders or partners  
   a) Informal repayment plan – contact each creditor and agree reduced payments until cleared.  
   b) Debt Management Plan – arranged through a debt-advice provider.  
   c) Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) – a legally binding agreement to pay what you can afford over up to 5 years; the rest is written off.  
   d) Bankruptcy – your assets are sold to repay creditors and most remaining debts are written off.  

   Further details: search “IVA” or “bankruptcy” on GOV.UK.

5. Options for limited companies  
   a) Company Voluntary Arrangement (CVA) – the company pays an agreed amount to creditors over time and can keep trading if viable.  
   b) Creditors’ Voluntary Liquidation (CVL) – directors choose to liquidate; an insolvency practitioner (IP) sells company assets and distributes the proceeds. Any unpaid debt is written off when the company is dissolved.  
   c) Compulsory liquidation – creditors ask the court to wind up the company if you do not act first. Usually more costly and offers less control.  

   See GOV.UK: “Liquidate your company”.

6. Pick and appoint a licensed insolvency practitioner (IP) if you need a CVA, CVL or bankruptcy  
   • An IP will explain costs, duties and the paperwork.  
   • You can find one via your professional body or by searching “find an insolvency practitioner GOV.UK”.

7. Deal with employees, assets and tax  
   • Tell staff and follow redundancy rules.  
   • Sell any remaining stock or equipment (the IP does this in a liquidation).  
   • Submit final VAT, PAYE and Corporation Tax (if a company) returns and pay what you can.

8. Keep records  
   Keep financial records for at least six years in case HMRC or creditors need to see them.

If you are unsure which route suits you, contact a free, regulated debt-advice service or speak to an IP before you start.

prompt: I'm part of a community group in Wales and we want to start an unincorporated association. Do we need to register it, and what happens if we make a profit?
generated_response:
You can set up an unincorporated association in Wales without any formal registration.

What you need to do  
1. Agree a written constitution. This says what the group is for, how you admit members, elect officers, handle money and wind the group up.  
2. Keep minutes of meetings and basic annual accounts.  
3. Open a bank account in the association’s name (most banks will ask to see the constitution and a list of officers).  

When you might have to register  
• Charity status: if your aims are wholly charitable and your annual income will be over £5,000, you must register with the Charity Commission for England and Wales.  
• Tax: HMRC can ask you to file a Corporation Tax return if the association makes a taxable profit. You do not need to register in advance, but you must tell HMRC if you have profits that could be taxed.

What happens if you make a profit  
• Surplus kept within the group – for example to fund future activities – is still counted as ‘profits’ for tax purposes unless the association is a registered charity or qualifies for mutual-trading relief.  
• HMRC may charge Corporation Tax on those profits. You will need to complete a Company Tax Return and pay any tax due, usually nine months after the end of your accounting period.  
• If you distribute profits to members, those payments are taxable on the association and may also be taxable income for the members.  

Key risks to note  
• Officers and members have unlimited personal liability for the association’s debts, because an unincorporated association has no separate legal personality.  
• Some funders and suppliers may insist that you incorporate (for example as a charitable incorporated organisation or company limited by guarantee) to limit liability and make contracts easier.

Where to get more detail  
GOV.UK – Guidance on running clubs and voluntary associations and paying Corporation Tax if you make a profit:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

If you are unsure whether you will owe Corporation Tax or should register as a charity, speak to HMRC or seek independent legal advice.

prompt: I'm a tenant in Scotland and I suspect there's asbestos in the building I rent. Who is responsible for managing it?
generated_response:
In most cases the duty to manage asbestos rests with your landlord, not the tenant.

1. Non-domestic parts of the building (for example shared corridors, stairwells, boiler rooms, roofs):  
   • The “duty-holder” under the Control of Asbestos Regulations 2012 is usually the landlord or whoever is responsible for maintenance and repair.  
   • They must find out if asbestos is present, keep a register, label it where necessary and make sure it is safe (or arrange removal if it cannot be kept in good condition).

2. Inside your private flat or house:  
   • The landlord is still responsible for the fabric of the building – walls, ceilings, pipe lagging, floor tiles, etc.  
   • If you suspect asbestos, tell the landlord or letting agent in writing and ask for an asbestos survey or written evidence that the material is safe.

What to do next
1. Write to your landlord (keep a copy) describing what you have found and where.  
2. Ask for a copy of the current asbestos management plan or survey for the building.  
3. If they do not respond within a reasonable time, contact your local council’s environmental health team. They can require the landlord to investigate and make the area safe.  
4. If you think the asbestos is damaged and releasing dust, avoid the area, ventilate the room as little as possible and call the council or the Health and Safety Executive (HSE) for advice on urgent enforcement.

Useful links  
GOV.UK – Duty to manage asbestos (HSE): https://www.hse.gov.uk/asbestos/duty.htm

If you are unsure or feel the material is immediately dangerous, treat it as an emergency and contact the council or HSE without delay.

prompt: How can I find out if a company has been wound up or is in the process of being liquidated?
generated_response:
You can check a company’s status for free online or by phone.  

1) Go to Companies House “Find and update company information” (search “Companies House service” in your browser).  
   • Type the company name or number and open its record.  
   • Look under “Filing history” and “Status”.  
   • If the company is in liquidation, administration or has a winding-up order, this will be shown and any documents filed by the liquidator can be opened and read.

2) Check insolvency notices in The Gazette (the UK’s official public record).  
   • Visit thegazette.co.uk and choose “Insolvency”.  
   • Search the company name.  
   • You will see any winding-up petitions, liquidation appointments or final dissolution notices.

3) If you prefer to speak to someone, call:  
   • Companies House contact centre: 0303 123 4500 (Monday to Friday).  
   • Insolvency Service helpline: 0300 678 0015 (select the option for “company liquidation”).  
   Give them the company name and, if possible, its registration number.

4) If you are dealing with the company directly, ask for the name and contact details of the appointed liquidator. A licensed insolvency practitioner must handle the process and can confirm the position.

Further business guidance is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

If any information is unclear or you cannot get online, phone Companies House for help—they can read the public record to you.

prompt: I recently sold some custom-made curtains online and a customer is returning them saying they are not as described. What should I do to handle this situation according to the law?
generated_response:
Under UK consumer law, bespoke or “made-to-order” items do not give the buyer a right to change their mind, but they must still:

• match the description you gave  
• be of satisfactory quality  
• be fit for their normal purpose (or any purpose you agreed to)

If the customer says the curtains are “not as described”, the relevant rules are in the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and the Consumer Contracts Regulations.

What to do

1. Ask the customer for clear details (e.g. photos, measurements) so you can see how the curtains differ from your listing or agreed specification. Keep all messages and images as evidence.

2. Decide quickly whether you accept the item is not as described.  
   • If you agree: the buyer can reject the goods and claim a full refund.  
   • If you disagree: explain why in writing and try to resolve it; if the dispute continues you may need Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) or, ultimately, a small-claims action.

3. If you accept the return:  
   a. Arrange (and pay for) the return postage or collect the curtains yourself.  
   b. Refund the customer in full — including the original delivery charge — within 14 days of receiving the curtains back (or of receiving proof they’ve been sent).  
   c. Use the same payment method the customer used, unless they agree otherwise.

4. If you would prefer to repair or remake the curtains, you can offer that, but the customer can refuse and insist on a refund when the goods are not as described and it is still within 30 days of delivery.

5. Keep records of the order, your description, the customer’s complaint, and the refund or other resolution. This helps if the issue is escalated.

6. If no agreement is reached, signpost the customer to an ADR scheme you belong to (or must by your platform’s rules). Courts expect you to offer ADR before litigation.

More detail

GOV.UK guidance:  
Accepting returns and giving refunds – https://www.gov.uk/accepting-returns-and-giving-refunds  
Online and distance selling for businesses – https://www.gov.uk/online-and-distance-selling-for-businesses

If you are unsure at any stage, contact a business advice service (for example, the Citizens Advice consumer helpline) or your trade association for guidance.

prompt: I need to check if another business's VAT number is valid. How do I go about doing this and what information will I get once I've checked it?
generated_response:
You can check whether a UK VAT number is valid online and get basic details about the business.

1) Go to ‘Check a UK VAT number’ on GOV-UK (search that phrase or start from gov.uk and follow Business › VAT).  
2) Enter the 9-digit VAT number (or the number plus the GB prefix if you have it) and submit.  
3) The service will confirm whether the number is:
   • valid or invalid  
   • currently registered or has been cancelled  
4) If it is valid, you will also see:  
   • the registered business name  
   • the business address (usually its principal place of business)  
   • the date the information was last updated

You can print or save the results as evidence for your records.

If the check shows the number is invalid:

• Make sure you typed the digits correctly.  
• Ask the supplier to confirm the number.  
• If it still comes back invalid, do not treat the supply as zero-rated or reclaim input VAT until the issue is resolved—contact HMRC for advice if needed.

EU VAT numbers: use the EU’s VIES checker (linked from the same GOV-UK page) to confirm VAT numbers issued by member states.

Link: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/check-a-uk-vat-number

prompt: I run a small business in Northern Ireland and I'm exporting goods to the EU. How do I know if I need to register for Intrastat and what are the thresholds for registration?
generated_response:
Intrastat still applies to goods that move between Northern Ireland and EU member states.

Do you need to register?

• Dispatches (goods you send from Northern Ireland to the EU): register if the total value of those goods is more than £250,000 (excluding VAT, freight and insurance) in the current calendar year or was above that level in the previous year.

• Arrivals (goods you bring in from the EU to Northern Ireland): the separate threshold is £500,000.  

If your business only exports and your dispatches stay below £250,000 a year, you do not have to register.

What to do if you go over the dispatches threshold

1. Keep a running total of the value of all goods you dispatch from 1 January each year.  
2. As soon as the cumulative total goes over £250,000, you must register for Intrastat.  
3. Register by contacting HMRC’s Intrastat team (03000 594 815 or intrastatenquiries@hmrc.gov.uk) and they will set up your account on the Secure Data Exchange Service (SDES).  
4. From the month after you exceed the threshold, submit a monthly Intrastat Supplementary Declaration by the 21st of the following month.  
5. Continue submitting declarations for the rest of that calendar year, even if later months are below the threshold. Re-assess each January.

If you are close to the threshold and unsure, call the HMRC Intrastat helpline for confirmation.

More detail: GOV.UK guidance on Intrastat (uk-intrastat-guidance)

prompt: I want to know if I can use trade mark decisions to decide whether to object to a trade mark. What steps should I take to evaluate the relevance of these decisions to my situation?
generated_response:
Yes. Earlier UK Intellectual Property Office (IPO) trade mark decisions can help you judge whether it is worth opposing a new application, but you need to check how closely the facts match yours.

Follow these steps.

1. List the key facts of your own situation  
   • Your existing mark (how it looks, sounds and what it means)  
   • The mark you are thinking of opposing  
   • The goods or services each mark covers (Nice classes)  
   • Anything that affects distinctiveness or reputation (how long you have used your mark, market share, advertising, etc.)

2. Search the IPO decisions database  
   • Go to ‘Trade mark decisions’ on GOV.UK  
     https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/trade-mark-decisions  
   • Use keywords or class numbers to find opposition decisions that involve similar marks or the same types of goods/services.  
   • Download the full decision PDFs and note the decision number and date.

3. Read each decision in order: facts, legal grounds, outcome  
   • Section 5(1) – identical marks/goods  
   • Section 5(2) – similar marks/goods and likelihood of confusion  
   • Section 5(3) – marks with a reputation  
   • Section 5(4) – passing off  
   Make a short table of which grounds were argued and why the Hearing Officer accepted or rejected them.

4. Compare the decision facts with yours  
   • Are the marks visually, phonetically or conceptually as close as yours?  
   • Are the goods/services identical, overlapping or clearly different?  
   • Did the successful party show evidence of use or reputation similar to yours?  
   • Were any differences (for example, a small logo element) enough to avoid confusion?

5. Check if the decision was appealed  
   • If so, read the appeal judgment to see whether the reasoning changed.  
   • Appeals are listed with the original decision on the same GOV.UK page.

6. Note any consistent themes or thresholds  
   • For example, Hearing Officers often say that everyday retail consumers pay less attention to low-cost goods, so a small similarity can cause confusion.  
   • Look for wording such as “average consumer”, “distinctive element” and “dominant component” – these show what tipped the balance.

7. Weigh the likelihood of success against cost and time  
   • An opposition fee starts at £200 and can rise if the case becomes complex.  
   • If the IPO thinks your chances are low, you may have to pay the other side’s costs.  
   Use what you learned from past decisions to judge whether your facts meet or exceed cases that won.

8. If in doubt, get professional advice  
   Decisions are fact-specific and trade mark law can be technical. An IP attorney can give an opinion letter based on the decisions you have found and any newer case law.

If you decide to go ahead, follow the IPO guidance on ‘Oppose a trade mark application’:  
https://www.gov.uk/oppose-trade-mark-application

prompt: I want to start a business partnership with my friend. What steps do I need to follow to set up the partnership and register it with HMRC?
generated_response:
Here’s what you and your friend need to do to set up an ordinary business partnership and get it registered with HMRC.

1. Agree the basics  
   • Decide what the partnership will do, its trading name and a business address.  
   • Draw up a written partnership agreement covering profit share, decision-making, how partners can leave, etc. (optional but strongly recommended).

2. Pick a ‘nominated partner’  
   One partner must be named as the person who deals with HMRC. The other partner(s) still have individual tax duties.

3. Register the partnership with HMRC  
   • The nominated partner registers the partnership for Self Assessment using form SA400 (online or by post).  
   • Do this by 5 October in the second tax year after the business starts trading (for example, by 5 October 2025 if you start trading any time in 2023-24).  
   • HMRC will issue a Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) for the partnership and send a notice telling you when the first Partnership Tax Return is due.

4. Register each partner individually  
   • Every partner, including the nominated one, must register for Self Assessment as a sole trader using form SA401 (online or by post) unless they already file a tax return.  
   • HMRC will give each partner their own UTR.

5. Set up a business bank account and records  
   Keep separate accounts for the partnership. Record all income and expenses so you can complete the yearly Partnership Tax Return (SA800) and each partner’s personal return.

6. Check other tax and legal duties  
   • VAT – you must register if taxable turnover is over £85,000 in any 12-month period, or you can register voluntarily.  
   • PAYE – register as an employer if you’ll have staff (including paying yourselves a salary through payroll).  
   • Licences, insurance and sector-specific rules – check if you need professional licences, public liability insurance, etc., especially for premises in Scotland.

7. File and pay on time each year  
   • Partnership Tax Return (SA800): 31 January online / 31 October paper after the tax year ends.  
   • Each partner’s personal return and tax: same deadlines.  
   • Pay any Class 2 and Class 4 National Insurance and Income Tax due.

Where to get more detail  
GOV.UK guide: Set up a business partnership  
https://www.gov.uk/set-up-business-partnership

If anything is unclear, call HMRC’s Newly Self-Employed helpline or speak to a professional adviser before you start trading.

prompt: I'm a small business owner in Wales and I want to end my commercial lease early. What steps should I take to ensure I'm following the correct procedure?
generated_response:
Leaving a commercial premises early can be costly if you do not follow the lease and the law. Use the steps below to stay on the right side of your agreement and limit penalties.

1. Check your lease for a break clause  
   • Look for a clause that lets you end the lease on a set date (‘break date’) if you give the right amount of written notice.  
   • Note any conditions, such as being up to date with rent and leaving the unit in good repair.

2. Work out alternative options if there is no break clause  
   • Assignment – you find another business to take over the lease. You must get the landlord’s written consent first.  
   • Sub-letting – you rent the space to someone else for the remaining term (allowed only if the lease says so and the landlord agrees).  
   • Lease surrender – you and the landlord both agree in writing to end the lease. The landlord may ask for a lump-sum payment.

3. Take professional advice early  
   • Speak to a commercial property solicitor or a qualified surveyor.  
   • They can explain your rights under the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954 (which also applies in Wales) and check any compensation or notice rules.

4. Serve notice correctly  
   • Follow the notice period, address and delivery method set out in the lease.  
   • Keep proof of delivery (recorded post or courier with signature).

5. Settle all money owed  
   • Pay rent, service charges and any interest up to the lease-end date.  
   • Budget for business rates until the landlord becomes responsible again (usually the day after your lease ends).

6. Bring the premises back to the required condition (‘dilapidations’)  
   • Repair, redecorate and remove fittings if the lease says so.  
   • Ask your surveyor to negotiate any schedule of dilapidations the landlord serves.

7. Hand back keys and get written confirmation  
   • Agree a handover date and ask the landlord to confirm in writing that they have accepted the surrender or break.  
   • Request the return of any rent deposit that is due.

8. Keep records  
   • Store copies of the lease, correspondence, notices, payment receipts and the landlord’s acceptance for at least six years.

If you are unsure at any stage, get legal advice before you give notice. Breaking the lease incorrectly can leave you liable for the remaining rent and costs.

General business premises guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: What happens if I miss the deadline for filing my confirmation statement?
generated_response:
Missing the deadline can have serious consequences, so act quickly.

What can happen  
• There is no automatic late-filing fee, but it is a criminal offence not to file a confirmation statement on time.  
• Companies House can prosecute the company and its directors. Fines can reach up to £5,000 for each offence.  
• If the statement stays overdue, Companies House may start strike-off action. If the company is struck off, it stops existing and any remaining assets pass to the Crown.  
• The longer it is late, the harder it is to restore the company and the higher any court fines are likely to be.

How to put it right  
1. File the outstanding confirmation statement as soon as possible. You can do this online through Companies House WebFiling or the new Companies House Service (CHS). The online fee is £13.  
2. If more than one statement is overdue, file them in date order (oldest first).  
3. Pay any fines the court has already imposed, if applicable.  
4. If you have received a strike-off notice, contact Companies House immediately to explain that you are filing and to ask for the action to be paused.  
5. Keep a note of your next review period end date and set reminders so you do not miss it again.

Need more help?  
Visit GOV.UK’s business section (https://www.gov.uk/browse/business) or call the Companies House helpline for advice on filing online or dealing with prosecution or strike-off notices.

prompt: I need to get a copy of a patent document to prove ownership for a court case. How do I request a certified copy and what is the process?
generated_response:
You can order a court-ready, certified copy of any UK patent document from the Intellectual Property Office (IPO).

1. Gather the details  
   • UK patent (or application) number  
   • Title of the invention  
   • Name of the proprietor/applicant  
   • The specific document you need certified (for example “granted patent specification” or “assignment filed on 12 March 2019”).

2. Choose the type of copy  
   • Standard certified copy – normally issued within 5–7 working days.  
   • Same-day copy – costs more; the IPO must receive your request and payment before 15:00 on a working day.

3. Make the request  
   Online (quickest)  
   a) Go to GOV.UK → Intellectual Property Office → “Request certified documents”.  
      Direct link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business/intellectual-property (follow the “Get a certified copy” link).  
   b) Complete the online form, upload any supporting information and pay by card.

   By email or post  
   a) Download Patents Form 23 (“Request for certified office copy”) from the same GOV.UK page.  
   b) Fill it in and send it to certificates@ipo.gov.uk, or post it to:  
      Intellectual Property Office, Concept House, Cardiff Road, Newport NP10 8QQ.  
   c) The IPO will contact you for card payment, or you can enclose a cheque made payable to ‘Intellectual Property Office’.

4. Pay the fee  
   • Standard service: £20 per document.  
   • Same-day service: £50 per document.  
   (Fees can change, so check the form or webpage on the day you apply.)

5. Receive and check the copy  
   • Paper copies arrive by post with an embossed IPO seal and signature.  
   • Electronic copies (PDF) carry a digital stamp and are normally accepted by UK courts—confirm with the court if in doubt.  
   • Check that every required page bears the certification stamp before your hearing.

If you need help completing the form or have a deadline, phone the IPO information centre on 0300 300 2000 (Monday–Friday, office hours).

prompt: I want to complain about my insolvency practitioner, but I'm not sure where to start. What steps should I take to make sure my complaint is handled properly?
generated_response:
You complain through the Insolvency Practitioner Complaints Gateway run by the Insolvency Service.  

1) Put your concerns in writing to the insolvency practitioner (or their firm) first.  
   • Explain what you think they did wrong and what outcome you want.  
   • Keep copies of letters, emails, notices and any reply.  

2) Give them a reasonable time (usually up to 8 weeks) to respond.  
   • If they resolve the matter, no further action is needed.  
   • If you are still unhappy or get no reply, move to step 3.  

3) Send your complaint to the Insolvency Practitioner Complaints Gateway.  
   • Use the online form at gov.uk/complain-about-insolvency-practitioner, or  
   • Email the completed form to ip.complaints@insolvency.gov.uk, or  
   • Post it to IP Complaints, Insolvency Service, PO Box 1035, Ipswich IP1 9FY, or  
   • Phone 0300 678 0015 to ask for a paper form.  

4) Provide:  
   • The practitioner’s name and firm.  
   • The case type (for example bankruptcy, IVA, liquidation).  
   • Dates, facts, and copies of any evidence.  
   • Proof you first complained to the practitioner.  

5) The Gateway checks your complaint and passes it to the practitioner’s professional body, who will investigate and tell you the outcome. They can discipline the practitioner but cannot award you compensation.  

If you need advice while preparing your complaint, ring the Insolvency Service Enquiry Line on 0300 678 0015 or speak to a free debt-advice charity.

prompt: How can I find out if a company has changed its name or address recently?
generated_response:
You can check a company’s official details for free on the Companies House website.

1. Go to GOV.UK and search “Companies House – find and update company information” or use this direct link:
   https://www.gov.uk/get-information-about-a-company  

2. Enter the company name (or its registration number if you have it).

3. Select the correct company from the results list.

4. On the company page you can see:
   • the current registered office address  
   • any previous names (listed under “Previous company names”)  
   • recent filings that show when the address or name was changed (look in “Filing history” for forms called “AD01 – Change of registered office address” or “NM01 – Change of name”).

5. If you need proof, you can:
   • download the relevant filing documents for free, or  
   • order a formal certificate of name change or incorporation (there is a small fee).

6. To keep track of future changes, select “Follow this company” on the same page and enter your email address. Companies House will email you whenever new documents are filed.

If the business is not a limited company (for example, a sole trader or partnership), it will not appear on Companies House. In that case, you’ll need to ask the business directly or check trade directories, professional registers or the sector regulator.

prompt: I want to close my limited company in Scotland. What steps do I need to follow to strike it off the Companies Register?
generated_response:
To strike your Scottish limited company off the Companies Register (often called a “voluntary dissolution”):

1. Check you qualify  
   • The company must not have traded, sold stock, or changed its name in the last 3 months.  
   • It must have no ongoing legal action, no agreements with creditors (for example a CVA) and not be about to become insolvent.  

2. Tie up tax and payroll  
   • Tell HMRC you are closing the company. Send final Corporation Tax and PAYE returns, pay any tax due, and cancel VAT or PAYE schemes.  
   • Pay any outstanding debts, wages, and close business bank accounts.  

3. Deal with assets first  
   • Transfer or distribute any remaining cash, equipment or property before you apply. Anything left after dissolution passes to the Crown (bona vacantia).  

4. Complete form DS01  
   • Download form DS01 from GOV.UK or file it online.  
   • A majority of directors must sign.  
   • Send it to Companies House with the £10 fee (cheque or card). Use the Edinburgh address for Scottish companies if posting.  

5. Inform interested parties within 7 days  
   • Send a copy of the filed DS01 to shareholders, employees, creditors, pension managers, and any directors who did not sign.  

6. Gazette notices and waiting period  
   • Companies House publishes a notice in the Edinburgh Gazette.  
   • If no objections are received after 2 months, a second notice confirms the company is dissolved.  

7. Keep records  
   • Hold on to company financial and tax records for at least 7 years after dissolution.  

If the company has debts it cannot pay, or you are unsure whether it meets the conditions, seek professional advice first—an insolvency procedure may be safer than striking off.

Full guidance and the DS01 form: https://www.gov.uk/strike-off-your-company-from-companies-register

prompt: I'm a nurse in Wales and I need to complain about an insolvency practitioner. How do I find out which professional body they belong to and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
If you believe an insolvency practitioner (IP) has acted unprofessionally, you must complain to the body that licences (authorises) them.

1. Find out who licences the practitioner  
   • Phone the Insolvency Service Enquiry Line on 0300 678 0015 (Monday–Friday).  
   • Give the practitioner’s full name and, if possible, their firm’s name or the case they are handling.  
   • Ask which “authorising body” licences them. (Most IPs are licensed by ICAEW, the Insolvency Practitioners Association, ICAS, Chartered Accountants Ireland, or the Insolvency Service itself.)  
   • You can also look the practitioner up online using the GOV.UK “Find an insolvency practitioner” tool: https://www.gov.uk/find-an-insolvency-practitioner

2. Gather your evidence  
   • Copies of letters, emails, court papers, statements of fees, or anything else that shows what went wrong.  
   • A short, clear timeline of events.

3. Complain in writing to the authorising body  
   • Each body has its own complaint form or email address.  
   • State:  
     – the practitioner’s name and firm  
     – the case reference (if you have one)  
     – what happened, with dates  
     – what outcome you want (for example, an explanation, apology, fee review).  
   • Keep copies of everything you send.

4. If the practitioner is licensed directly by the Insolvency Service (listed as ‘Secretary of State’), send your complaint to:  
   email: ip.complaints@insolvency.gov.uk  
   post: IP Complaints, Insolvency Service, 3rd Floor Cannon House, 18 Priory Queensway, Birmingham B4 6FD

5. Wait for a reply  
   • The body should confirm they have your complaint within a few days and tell you roughly how long an investigation may take.  
   • They may ask for more documents or clarification.

6. If you are unhappy with the authorising body’s final decision  
   • You can ask the Insolvency Service’s Complaints Gateway to review how the complaint was handled: https://www.gov.uk/complain-about-insolvency-practitioner

Tips  
• Send documents by recorded delivery or use email with a delivery receipt.  
• If you need help putting the complaint together, you can contact Citizens Advice Cymru on 0800 702 2020.

prompt: I want to copy a photograph for a school project. How do I check if I need a licence and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
You may be able to copy the photograph without a licence if the use is covered by a copyright exception for education. Follow these checks in order.

1. Identify the photograph  
   • Note the title, photographer (if known) and where you found it.  
   • Look for any statement about copyright or a licence (for example “© Jane Smith 2022”, “All rights reserved”, “Creative Commons CC-BY”).

2. Is it still in copyright?  
   • Photographs are normally protected for 70 years after the photographer dies.  
   • If the photographer died more than 70 years ago, the image is out of copyright and you may copy it freely.  
   • If you cannot find the photographer’s details, assume it is still in copyright.

3. Check for an existing licence  
   • Some images are released under open licences such as Creative Commons.  
   • If the photograph has a licence, read the terms and follow any conditions (for example, crediting the creator).

4. Decide whether you need extra permission  
   • For a private school project shown only to your teacher or class, you can usually rely on the “illustration for instruction” exception in UK copyright law.  
   • You must:  
     – use only what is necessary for the task (one photograph is normally fine);  
     – give a clear acknowledgement (for example “Photo by Jane Smith, used for educational purposes”).  
   • If the project will be put on a public website, in a brochure or on social media, the education exception no longer covers you and you should get permission or a licence from the copyright owner.

5. If permission is needed  
   • Contact the photographer, photo agency or organisation that owns the rights.  
   • Explain how you plan to use the image, ask for written permission, and keep a copy of the reply.

6. Keep a record  
   • Save any licence terms, emails or letters granting permission.  
   • Note the source and acknowledgement used in your project.

Further guidance  
GOV.UK: Using someone else’s work: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/using-copyright-material

If you are unsure after these checks, ask your teacher or the school librarian, or seek advice before copying the photograph.

prompt: I'm a teacher in Wales and I need to know how to apply for a Start Up Loan for my business. What steps should I follow?
generated_response:
You apply for a Start Up Loan through the government-backed Start Up Loans Company. It is the same process for Wales, England, Scotland and Northern Ireland.

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • 18 or over and living in the UK  
   • starting a new business or have traded for less than 3 years  
   • able to pass credit checks and afford the repayments  
   • your business is based in the UK and legal

2. Gather the documents you will need  
   • photo ID and proof of address  
   • a simple business plan and cash–flow forecast (free templates are on the site)  
   • basic details about any other borrowing

3. Start the online application  
   • Go to GOV.UK and search “Start Up Loan” or use: https://www.gov.uk/start-up-loans  
   • Click ‘Apply’ and fill in the short eligibility checker.

4. Create an account and submit your details  
   • answer questions about you and your business idea  
   • upload your business plan and forecast (you can save and return later).

5. Work with a business adviser  
   • once your form is in, an adviser phones or emails you  
   • they review your plan, suggest changes if needed, and help decide how much to borrow (between £500 and £25,000 per person).

6. Receive a decision  
   • if approved, you sign the loan agreement online  
   • the money is paid into your bank account, usually within a few days.

7. After you get the loan  
   • repay monthly over 1 to 5 years at a fixed 6% interest  
   • you receive up to 12 months of free mentoring.

If you find the online form difficult, you can:

• phone Start Up Loans on 0344 264 2600  
• ask Business Wales for local face-to-face help (03000 6 03000).

If you are unsure about any part of the process, call the helpline above before you send in your application.

prompt: I need to request CCTV footage from a shop where I was present, but I'm not sure how to proceed. What steps should I follow to ensure my request is handled correctly?
generated_response:
To get a copy of CCTV footage that shows you, make a “subject access request” (SAR) to the organisation that operates the camera (the data controller). Follow these steps:

1. Act quickly  
   Most shops keep footage for 30 days or less, so send your request as soon as you can.

2. Find the right contact  
   • Ask in-store for the manager or head office details, or look for a privacy notice near the cameras.  
   • You need the postal or email address of the data protection officer, customer services team, or store manager.

3. Write your request  
   • Say you are making a subject access request under data-protection law for CCTV images that show you.  
   • Give the exact date, approximate time, and location (for example, “15 February 2026, between 14:10 and 14:25, aisle 3, High Street branch”).  
   • Describe yourself or what you were wearing to help them locate you.  
   • State whether you want a copy of the footage or just to view it.  
   • Attach proof of identity (for example a photo ID and a recent utility bill showing your address).

4. Send the request and keep a copy  
   • Email is fine if the shop accepts it; otherwise post it recorded delivery.

5. Wait for the response  
   • The shop normally has one calendar month to reply.  
   • They can extend by up to two months for complex requests, but must tell you why.  
   • They may blur other people’s faces for privacy. They should not charge a fee unless the request is “manifestly unfounded or excessive”.

6. If they refuse or do not respond  
   • Ask for a written explanation.  
   • If you think they are wrong, complain to them first.  
   • If you’re still not satisfied, you can complain to the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO).

Extra tips  
• Be polite and precise—vague requests can be refused.  
• Keep all correspondence and proof of posting.  
• If the footage is needed for legal proceedings, tell the shop; they may agree to preserve it for longer.

Further information  
General information about justice and data rights: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

prompt: I received a fine from a magistrates' court and am having trouble paying it. What steps should I take to get my fine reviewed?
generated_response:
You can ask for a magistrates’ court fine to be reviewed (varied) if you can’t keep up with the payments. Act quickly so extra enforcement fees are not added.

1. Find the reference number and contact details on the fine notice or any enforcement letter.  
   • If it was passed to His Majesty’s Courts and Tribunals Service (HMCTS) Collection and Compliance Centre, use the phone number on the letter.  
   • If not, contact the fines officer at the court that imposed the fine.

2. Ask for a “variation of payment terms” because your financial circumstances have changed. Tell them:  
   • how much you can afford each week or month, and  
   • whether you need more time, smaller instalments or (in exceptional cases) a reduction.

3. Complete form MC100 (Application to vary a fine). You can:  
   • download it from GOV.UK or request a copy from the court,  
   • list all your income, benefits, essential spending and any other debts, and  
   • attach evidence such as payslips or benefit letters.

4. Send the form back by the deadline the fines officer gives you (keep a copy). Keep paying what you can in the meantime to show goodwill.

5. The fines officer will write to confirm the new arrangement or tell you to attend a means hearing before magistrates. Go to the hearing if invited and take proof of your finances.

6. If you still cannot reach an affordable plan, get free debt advice straight away—ask the court staff for local services or search “free debt advice Wales” on GOV.UK.

The justice section on GOV.UK explains court fines and what to do if you cannot pay: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

prompt: I received a jury summons and need to respond. What are the steps to reply and what happens if I don't respond?
generated_response:
You must reply to a Scottish jury citation within 7 days of receiving it.

1) Read the citation carefully. It tells you the court, the start date of the jury panel and the last day you must reply.
2) Decide whether you can serve.  
   • If you are able to attend, confirm this.  
   • If you need to be excused or defer (for example because of illness, caring duties or a pre-booked holiday), state the reason and enclose evidence such as a doctor’s note or travel confirmation.
3) Fill in the ‘Reply to jury citation’ form that came with the letter, or respond online if the citation gives a web address for doing so.
4) Sign the form and return it in the prepaid envelope provided (or submit the online form). Keep a copy of what you send.
5) Wait for a confirmation letter or email. It will tell you whether you’ve been excused, deferred to a later date, or need to attend. Follow any instructions about phoning the court’s jury line the evening before the trial starts.

If you do not respond:
• It is a criminal offence under the Criminal Procedure (Scotland) Act 1995.  
• The court can fine you up to £1,000 and may issue a warrant to make you attend.  
• Ignoring the citation does not make it go away—contact the court straight away if you think your reply is late.

If you have lost the reply form or need help, phone the court listed on the citation or visit the jury service page on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/jury-service

prompt: How do I find out who is entitled to inherit if someone dies without a will and what steps do I need to take to apply for probate?
generated_response:
When someone dies without a will, the estate is shared out under the intestacy rules. You need to:

1. Check who inherits  
   • Use the GOV.UK tool ‘Who inherits if someone dies without a will’ (https://www.gov.uk/inherits-someone-dies-without-will).  
   • Answer the short questions about the person’s marital status, children and other close relatives.  
   • The tool tells you who is entitled and in what share (for example: spouse or civil partner first, then children, then parents, siblings, etc.).

2. Work out if you can apply for probate (called a ‘grant of letters of administration’ when there is no will)  
   • You can usually apply if you are the closest entitled relative: spouse/civil partner, child, grandchild, parent, and so on in that order.  
   • If several people are equally entitled (e.g. children), you can apply together or one of you can apply with the others’ agreement.

3. Value the estate  
   • List all assets (property, bank accounts, investments, personal possessions) and any debts.  
   • Use the GOV.UK guidance on valuing an estate (https://www.gov.uk/valuing-estate-of-someone-who-died).  
   • If the estate is over the Inheritance Tax threshold or complicated, you may need to report it to HMRC first.

4. Complete the probate application  
   Option A – Online  
     a) Create an online account and fill in the probate application (https://www.gov.uk/applying-for-probate).  
     b) Upload the death certificate, estate valuation summary and any Inheritance Tax forms.  
   Option B – By post  
     a) Fill in form PA1A (‘Application for a grant of letters of administration’).  
     b) Send it with the original death certificate and valuation documents to the Probate Registry.  
   • Pay the probate fee (£273 if the estate is worth more than £5,000; no fee below that).  

5. Send any requested originals  
   • The Probate Registry may ask for original financial statements or property deeds.  
   • Keep copies for your records.

6. Receive the grant  
   • Average processing time is 8–12 weeks but can be longer if documents are missing or tax is due.  
   • Once you have the grant, you can collect assets, pay debts, and distribute the estate strictly according to the intestacy shares identified in step 1.

If you are unsure about your entitlement, the estate is abroad or disputed, or Inheritance Tax is involved, consider getting legal advice or speaking to the Probate helpline (details on the probate page above).

prompt: I need to pay a court fee for a civil case in England. How do I know how much I need to pay and where to send the payment?
generated_response:
Court fees depend on the type of civil case and (for money claims) the amount you are claiming. Follow these steps:

1. Identify the claim type  
   • Money claim, possession, injunction, insolvency, judicial review, etc.  
   • For money claims, note the exact value you are claiming.

2. Look up the fee in the Civil and Family Court fees guide (form EX50) on GOV.UK.  
   – Go to GOV.UK and search “EX50 court fees” or start at: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice  
   – Use the tables to find the correct fee for your claim and stage (issuing the claim, allocation, hearing, application, etc.).

3. Check if you can get ‘Help with Fees’ (fee remission) before you pay.  
   – If your income or savings are low you may qualify.  
   – Apply online or with form EX160; the guide explains the thresholds.

4. Pay the fee to the court that is handling your case.  
   • Online services (for example Money Claim Online or the County Court Money Claims Centre) take card payments during the application.  
   • If you are posting or handing in forms, include a cheque or postal order made payable to ‘HMCTS’ or ‘HM Courts & Tribunals Service’.  
   • Some courts can take card payments by phone—ring the court office first.  
   • Always quote your case or claim number so the payment is matched correctly.

5. Keep proof of payment (receipt, online confirmation, or a copy of the cheque) in case the court asks for it later.

If you are unsure which fee applies, call the court office dealing with your case or the HMCTS court and tribunal fees helpline. It is better to check before you send payment, as applications can be rejected if the fee is wrong.

prompt: Can I bring someone with me to court to help me, and what are their limitations?
generated_response:
Yes. If you are representing yourself (‘a litigant in person’) you can usually take someone with you, often called a ‘McKenzie friend’.

What they can do  
• Sit with you in the courtroom or hearing room.  
• Take notes.  
• Quietly give you advice on your case, papers and what to say.  
• Help you organise documents.

What they cannot do without the judge’s permission  
• Speak to the court, question witnesses or make legal submissions.  
• Sign documents on your behalf.  
• Act as your official representative or call themselves your lawyer.

When permission may be refused  
A judge can refuse or limit their help if, for example, the person:  
• Disrupts the hearing.  
• Has a conflict of interest with your case.  
• Tries to act as an unqualified legal adviser for payment.  
• Presents a risk to the other party (for instance, in cases involving domestic abuse).

Practical steps  
1. Choose someone you trust who can keep information confidential.  
2. Arrive early and tell the court usher you have a McKenzie friend.  
3. When your case is called, briefly ask the judge for permission for the person to sit with you and assist.  
4. Bring photo ID for them in case court security asks.  
5. Make sure they understand the limits above and follow any directions the judge gives.

If you need more details, see the “Going to court without a lawyer” guidance under Courts and Tribunals on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

prompt: I want to suggest a new Community Payback project in my local area. What steps should I follow to submit a nomination and what information do I need to provide?
generated_response:
You can put forward a Community Payback project yourself. It costs nothing to apply, but you will normally need to supply the materials (for example paint, plants or rubbish sacks).

1. Check the idea fits  
   • The work must benefit the local community (for example litter-picks, repainting a community hall, clearing churchyards or improving footpaths).  
   • It cannot be for a private business or replace paid workers.  
   • It must be safe for supervised groups to do and be possible within daylight hours.

2. Gather the details you will be asked for  
   • Your name, phone number and email or postal address.  
   • The organisation you represent (if any).  
   • Exact address or map location of the site.  
   • A short description of the work needed and why it helps the community.  
   • Rough size of the job (how many days or people you think it might take).  
   • Who owns the land or building and confirmation that you have their permission.  
   • What materials or tools are already on site and what you can supply.  
   • Any obvious health and safety issues (busy roads, water, heights, asbestos, etc.).  
   • When you would like the work carried out (weekdays, weekends, or both).

3. Send in your nomination  
   Online (quickest):  
   • Go to GOV.UK > Justice > Community Payback and select ‘Suggest a Community Payback project’.  
   • Fill in the online form and click ‘Submit’. It is sent straight to your local Probation Service team.  

   By phone or email:  
   • If you prefer, phone or email your regional Probation Service office—contact details are on the same GOV.UK page—and give the same information over the phone or in an email.

4. After you apply  
   • A probation officer will review the idea for suitability and safety.  
   • They may visit the site or ask for more detail.  
   • If accepted, they will agree dates with you and confirm what materials need to be on site.  
   • You will have a named contact to keep you updated.

If you need help completing the form, call the number on the Community Payback nomination page and ask the team to talk you through it.

Relevant link  
Community Payback – suggest a project: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

prompt: I need to understand who can apply for probate and inherit if someone dies without a will. What are the steps to determine who is entitled to the inheritance and how do I apply for probate?
generated_response:
When someone in England dies without leaving a will this is called dying ‘intestate’. Two things need working out:  

A) who is legally entitled to the estate (the intestacy rules), and  
B) who can apply for probate (called ‘letters of administration’ when there is no will).

A) Work out who inherits  
The estate passes in the order below. If there is anyone alive in a higher category, people further down do not inherit.

1. Spouse or civil partner  
   • If there are no children: they inherit the whole estate.  
   • If there are children: they get   
     – all personal belongings  
     – the first £270,000 of the estate, and  
     – half of anything above that.  
   The other half above £270,000 is shared equally between the children.

2. Children (or their descendants if a child has died).  
3. Parents.  
4. Full (same-mother-and-father) siblings or their descendants.  
5. Half-siblings or their descendants.  
6. Grandparents.  
7. Full uncles and aunts or their descendants.  
8. Half uncles and aunts or their descendants.  
If none of these relatives exist, the estate goes to the Crown (Bona Vacantia).

B) Who can apply for probate (‘letters of administration’)  
Up to four people from the highest-ranking group that inherits can apply. So:  
• If there is a surviving spouse or civil partner, they have first right.  
• If not, all children aged 18 or over can apply (they can agree that one or two of them act).  
You cannot apply if you’re under 18, bankrupt or lack mental capacity.

C) Steps to get letters of administration  

1. Register the death and get several certified copies of the death certificate.  
2. Value the estate: list assets (property, money, possessions) and debts.  
3. Report the value to HMRC and deal with any Inheritance Tax (IHT).  
   • Complete IHT205 (most estates) or IHT400 (larger/complex estates).  
   • Pay any IHT due (or arrange the instalment option) before applying for probate.  
4. Complete the probate application (form PA1A) or use the online service. You’ll need:  
   • death certificate number  
   • estate valuation figures  
   • names and addresses of everyone entitled to inherit.  
5. Pay the probate fee (£273 if the estate is over £5,000; no fee below that, plus £1.50 per extra sealed copy).  
6. Send any requested documents (original death certificate, IHT confirmation, property deeds) to the Probate Registry.  
7. Wait for the grant of letters of administration (currently several weeks).  
8. As administrator you must:  
   • collect assets and close accounts,  
   • pay debts, taxes and expenses, and  
   • distribute the remaining estate according to the intestacy rules.  
Keep full records — you may have to provide an estate account to the beneficiaries.

Useful GOV.UK links  
Intestacy rules and who inherits: https://www.gov.uk/wills-probate-inheritance  
Apply for probate/letters of administration: https://www.gov.uk/applying-for-probate  

If anything is unclear (for example, a blended family or overseas assets) it is sensible to get specialist probate advice before applying.

prompt: What should I do if I don't know about my single justice procedure case?
generated_response:
If you have only just discovered that you were named in a Single Justice Procedure (SJP) case, act quickly.

1. Find out the case details  
   • Phone the Magistrates’ Court named on any paperwork you have, or the HMCTS Single Justice Service on 0300 123 9252 if you do not know which court dealt with it.  
   • Ask for: the offence, date of the hearing, the outcome (conviction, fine, points, etc.) and any reference numbers.

2. Check whether the case has been decided  
   • If the hearing is still pending, you can still submit your plea online or by post – follow the instructions under ‘Respond to a single justice procedure notice’ on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/single-justice-procedure-notice  
   • If you have already been convicted and you did not know about the case, move to step 3.

3. If you were convicted without knowing  
   • Make a statutory declaration within 21 days of finding out about the conviction. This is free and, if accepted, cancels the conviction and any penalties so the case can be heard again with your input.  
     How: phone or email your local Magistrates’ Court, ask for a statutory declaration appointment, and take proof of when you first found out (for example the letter or a note of the phone call).  
   • If more than 21 days have passed, you can still ask the court to reopen the case under section 142 of the Magistrates’ Courts Act 1980. Write to the court explaining that you never received the SJP notice and want the case reheard.

4. Deal with any enforcement action  
   • Tell bailiffs or enforcement staff that you have made (or will make) a statutory declaration or application to reopen the case and ask them to pause action.  
   • Keep copies of all letters, emails and proof of posting.

5. Next steps after the case is reopened  
   • You will receive a new date to enter a plea. Respond by the deadline.  
   • If you intend to plead not guilty, you can request a traditional court hearing.  
   • If you plead guilty, you can still send mitigation explaining your circumstances.

If you are unsure at any stage, phone the court for guidance or get free legal advice. GOV.UK justice information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

prompt: I was recently convicted in the Crown Court and want to appeal my sentence. What steps do I need to take to apply for permission to appeal and what happens if I get it?
generated_response:
