4. Upload supporting documents (for example, proof you’ll return home, evidence of funds, and details of where you’ll stay in Wales).
5. Track your application online. Processing usually takes 3 weeks, but allow more time before you travel.
6. Once approved, your passport is returned with a visa vignette. You can then travel to Wales.

If you are a British or Irish citizen you do not need a visa or an ETA.  

Next step: use the “Check if you need a UK visa” tool on GOV.UK to confirm what applies to your passport and follow the instructions it gives. Link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/visas-immigration

prompt: If I don't have a share code, how can I check a job applicant's right to work in the UK?
generated_response:
You have two other ways to confirm someone’s right to work without a share code:

1. Manual document check  
   a. Ask the applicant to show you an original document from the Home Office list (for example a UK or Irish passport, a current Biometric Residence Permit, a full UK birth certificate plus their National Insurance number).  
   b. Check that the photo, dates and any work restrictions match the person in front of you.  
   c. Make an unaltered copy (or clear scan) of the document.  
   d. Record the date you made the check and keep the copy securely for the length of the person’s employment plus two years.  
   If the document is on ‘List A’ you will have a continuous statutory excuse; if it is on ‘List B’ you must repeat the check when the permission expires.

2. Home Office Employer Checking Service (ECS)  
   Use this free online service if the applicant:  
   • has an outstanding visa application, administrative review or appeal  
   • has a digital status but cannot provide a share code (for example, technical issues)  
   • showed you an Application Registration Card (ARC) stating ‘work allowed’  
   • has their documents with the Home Office  
   Submit the ECS request form with the person’s details. You’ll usually receive a Positive Verification Notice within five working days. Keep that notice as proof; it gives you a six-month statutory excuse, after which you must re-check.

If the applicant says they only have an online immigration status, you should ask them to generate a share code; if they refuse, you cannot rely on a manual check and should not employ them unless the ECS gives you a Positive Verification Notice.

Full guidance and the ECS link are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/visas-immigration

prompt: If I'm bringing more than €10,000 in cash to the UK, what steps do I need to take to declare it at the airport?
generated_response:
You must declare cash worth £10,000 or more (or the same amount in another currency) when you enter or leave Great Britain. €10,000 is below the threshold, but if the euro amount you are carrying is worth £10,000 or more on the day you travel you must make a declaration.

What to do

1. Check the value  
   • Use a reliable exchange rate to see whether your euros are worth £10,000 or more.  
   • Include all cash you carry: notes, coins, travellers’ cheques, banker’s drafts, bearer bonds or similar.

2. If the value is £10,000 or more, submit an online ‘cash declaration’ form 72 hours to 1 hour before you arrive in the UK:  
   https://www.gov.uk/bringing-cash-into-uk  
   You’ll need:  
   • your passport details and contact address in the UK  
   • your travel route and flight number  
   • the amount and type of cash, where it came from and what it will be used for

3. Keep the email confirmation. It contains a reference you may be asked for at the border.

4. On arrival choose the red channel (‘goods to declare’) or use the red-point phone. Tell the Border Force officer you have made an online cash declaration and show the confirmation and the cash if asked.

If you could not submit the form in time

• Pick up form BOR9011 at the airport and hand it to a Border Force officer in the red channel, or use the red-point phone to request one.  
• The officer will stamp a copy for your records.

Keep your stamped or emailed declaration for at least 12 months in case HMRC asks for evidence later.

If you are still unsure, use the link above or call the HMRC helpline listed on that page before you travel.

prompt: I'm planning to move to the UK from Ukraine and need to know the exact steps to apply for a visa. What should I do first?
generated_response:
To give you the right step-by-step guide, can you tell me whether you have:

a) a close family member who is already living in the UK, or  
b) a named individual or organisation in the UK willing to sponsor you?

The application steps differ depending on which of those applies.

prompt: I'm planning to travel abroad and then return to the UK. How do I ensure my eVisa is correctly linked to my current passport and what steps should I take before my trip?
generated_response:
To avoid problems at the border, make sure the passport you will travel with is the one recorded on your eVisa record.

1. Sign in to the ‘UK Visas and Immigration (UKVI) account – view and prove your immigration status’ service.  
   • Check the passport number shown under ‘Travel documents’.  
   • If you have renewed or will soon renew your passport, go to ‘Update my details’ and add the new passport details. Do this as soon as you have the new passport and at least a week before you travel.

2. After updating, go back into the service and confirm the new passport now appears. This means your eVisa is linked.

3. While you are signed in, generate a share code and save or print the confirmation email. Airlines can use the share code if they need proof of your status before you board.

4. Take the same passport (and the share-code email or print-out) when you leave and when you return to the UK. At the UK border the passport is scanned and your eVisa should appear automatically.

5. Check your visa expiry date. If it will run out while you are away, apply to extend it before you travel.

6. Keep your UKVI account contact details up to date so the Home Office can reach you if needed.

If you cannot update your passport online or you lose your passport while abroad, contact UKVI as soon as possible for advice.

For the online services and contact details go to: https://www.gov.uk/browse/visas-immigration

prompt: What are the key differences between Attendance Allowance and Pension Age Disability Payment, especially for someone living in Wales?
generated_response:
In short, Pension Age Disability Payment (PADP) is a new Scottish benefit that will replace Attendance Allowance (AA) only for people who live in Scotland. If you live in Wales you can apply for Attendance Allowance, but PADP is not available to you unless you later move to Scotland.

Key points

1. Where each benefit applies  
   • Attendance Allowance – paid by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) in England, Wales and Northern Ireland.  
   • Pension Age Disability Payment – paid by Social Security Scotland and only for people whose main home is in Scotland.

2. Who can claim  
   • Both benefits are for people over State Pension age who have a disability or health condition that means they need help with personal care or supervision.  
   • In Wales you can only claim Attendance Allowance. To get PADP you must be habitually resident in Scotland.

3. How to apply  
   • Attendance Allowance – you complete form AA1A and post it to DWP (you can order a form by phone or download it to print).  
   • PADP – applications will be online, by phone or on paper through Social Security Scotland. Local-delivery staff can help complete the form.

4. Payment rates  
   • At launch PADP rates match Attendance Allowance (lower and higher rate), but the Scottish Government can change rates or rules in future.

5. Other rules  
   • Qualifying period: both generally require that you have needed help for at least 6 months.  
   • Mobility component: neither benefit has a mobility element (unlike Personal Independence Payment).  
   • Tax and means-testing: both are tax-free and do not depend on income or savings.

What this means if you live in Wales

• You should claim Attendance Allowance if you meet the conditions.  
• If you later move to Scotland, your claim will be transferred to PADP by Social Security Scotland—you will not need to make a fresh claim.  
• No action is needed regarding PADP while you remain in Wales.

How to claim Attendance Allowance

1. Order a claim form AA1A by calling 0800 731 0122 (textphone 0800 731 0317) or download it from GOV.UK.  
2. Fill in the form with detailed examples of the help you need day and night.  
3. Post it to the address on the form—posting date is treated as the claim date if you phoned for the form first.  
4. DWP may contact you or your GP for more information; no medical assessment is usually required.  
5. You will receive a decision letter and, if successful, payments every 4 weeks.

Further information: Benefits for disabled people and older people – https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: If I'm over State Pension age and living with my partner who hasn't reached State Pension age, can I still apply for Housing Benefit?
generated_response:
In most cases, no. Since 15 May 2019 a couple can only make a new Housing Benefit claim if both partners have reached State Pension age (or meet a narrow exception).

What this means for you

1. Because you are a “mixed-age” couple (one over State Pension age, one under), you cannot start a new Housing Benefit claim unless an exception applies.  
2. Instead, you normally need to claim Universal Credit for help with your rent until your partner also reaches State Pension age.  
3. The main exceptions are:  
   • you (or your partner) already got Housing Benefit as a couple before 15 May 2019 and the claim has carried on without a break, or  
   • you live in certain types of temporary or supported accommodation that remain outside Universal Credit.

What to do next

1. Check whether you were getting Housing Benefit together before 15 May 2019 or live in qualifying supported/temporary accommodation.  
2. If not, make a joint Universal Credit claim online or by phone instead of Housing Benefit.  
3. If you think an exception might apply, contact your local council’s benefits team and ask them to confirm before you submit any claim.  
4. If you need personalised advice, speak to a free welfare-rights service (for example Citizens Advice).

More information: GOV.UK benefits pages – https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: My partner and I are both working in the UK, and our child lives with us. We are not from the EU. How do we claim Child Benefit and what are the requirements?
generated_response:
Child Benefit is paid by HMRC to the person responsible for a child who lives with them in the UK. The fact that you are not from the EU is not a problem in itself, but your immigration status must allow you to claim public funds.

1. Check you are eligible  
   • You (or your partner) must be ‘habitually resident’ in the UK and not subject to immigration control that restricts access to public funds.  
     – If your visa or Biometric Residence Permit says ‘No Recourse to Public Funds (NRPF)’, you cannot claim unless you have since been granted permission that lifts that restriction (for example Indefinite Leave to Remain, settled status, or a visa that does not carry NRPF).  
   • The child must be under 16, or under 20 if they stay in approved education or training.  
   • Only one person can claim for each child, so you and your partner need to decide who will be the claimant.  

2. Get the claim form (CH2)  
   • Download it at gov.uk/child-benefit or ring the Child Benefit helpline (0300 200 3100) and ask for a paper form.  
   • If you register the birth in the UK you can ask the registrar for a claim form at the appointment.

3. Fill in the form  
   • Give details of yourself, your partner, the child and your immigration status.  
   • Tick the box if you want payments back-dated for up to 3 months from the date HMRC receives the form.  
   • Add supplementary pages if you are claiming for more than two children.

4. Send the form to HMRC  
   • Post it to the address on the form with the child’s original birth certificate (and adoption certificate if relevant).  
   • If HMRC needs to see proof of your immigration status they will write to you.

5. Wait for HMRC’s decision  
   • Payments are normally made every 4 weeks into your UK bank account.  
   • Keep the award notice: it can help you qualify for National Insurance credits that count towards your State Pension.

6. If either of you earns over £50,000 a year  
   • You may still claim, but you must register for Self Assessment and pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge, or opt out of being paid to avoid the charge. HMRC’s calculator will show what the charge would be.

7. Keep HMRC up to date  
   • Tell them within a month if your child leaves approved education/training, moves out, or you leave the UK permanently.  
   • Report any change in immigration status that adds an NRPF condition.

Where to get more help  
• Child Benefit helpline: 0300 200 3100 (Monday–Friday)  
• Guidance and the claim form: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit

If you are unsure whether your visa allows access to public funds, check the wording on your BRP or speak to an accredited immigration adviser before sending the claim.

prompt: What information do I need to provide to use the benefits calculator accurately?
generated_response:
To get reliable results from any of the benefits calculators listed on GOV.UK you’ll need details about your household, income and costs. Keep the following to hand:

1. Personal details  
   • Your date of birth, and your partner’s if you live together  
   • The ages of any children or other people who live with you  
   • Immigration status if you’re not a UK/Irish citizen

2. Earnings and other income  
   • Your latest payslip or self-employment figures (hours worked, gross pay, tax, National Insurance)  
   • Income for a partner or anyone else who contributes to household costs  
   • Any other money coming in – pensions, maintenance, student income, other benefits

3. Savings and capital  
   • Current balances of bank or building-society accounts  
   • Value of investments, premium bonds or second properties

4. Housing and council tax  
   • Weekly or monthly rent and any service charges, or your mortgage payment and outstanding mortgage balance  
   • Your council tax bill and whether you get any discounts or exemptions

5. Childcare costs  
   • Average weekly or monthly amount you pay to a registered provider

6. Disability or health details  
   • Whether anyone gets (or has applied for) Personal Independence Payment, Disability Living Allowance, Attendance Allowance or Armed Forces Independence Payment  
   • Any work-limiting health conditions

7. Student status  
   • Course details and student maintenance or loan amounts if anyone is in full-time education

Tips for accuracy  
• Use net figures where the calculator asks for them and gross figures where it asks for those.  
• Round to the nearest pound and use weekly or monthly amounts consistently.  
• If something changes soon (for example a new job or a baby), run the calculation twice: once for now and once for the future scenario.

You’ll find links to approved calculators on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

If you’re missing a piece of information you can estimate, but the result will only be as accurate as the figures you enter.

prompt: I'm over State Pension age and living with my partner who hasn't reached it yet. Can I still apply for Housing Benefit?
generated_response:
In most cases, no. Since 15 May 2019 a couple can only make a new claim for Housing Benefit if both of you have reached State Pension age.

What this means for you

• Because you and your partner are a “mixed-age couple” (one of you is pension-age, the other working-age), any new claim for help with rent usually has to be through Universal Credit, not Housing Benefit.

• The only exceptions are if you:
  – already get Housing Benefit as a couple and have had no break in your claim since before 15 May 2019 (you can stay on it while you remain eligible), or  
  – live in certain types of supported, sheltered or temporary accommodation that are still covered by Housing Benefit.

What to do next

1. Check whether you already have a protected Housing Benefit claim that started before 15 May 2019 and has carried on without a gap.  
2. If not, use the benefits calculator on GOV.UK or speak to an advice service to see how much Universal Credit housing element you could get.  
3. If you live in supported or temporary accommodation, ask your landlord or local council whether Housing Benefit still applies.  
4. If you’re unsure, contact your local council’s benefits team or an independent welfare rights adviser before making any new claim—moving to Universal Credit can end any right to go back to Housing Benefit.

Relevant GOV.UK pages  
• Housing Benefit: https://www.gov.uk/housing-benefit  
• Universal Credit: https://www.gov.uk/universal-credit

prompt: I'm applying for Universal Credit and want to know if my existing tax credits will stop automatically. What happens next and how long will it take to get my first Universal Credit payment?
generated_response:
Yes – once you send a claim for Universal Credit (UC), any tax credits you get now (Working Tax Credit or Child Tax Credit) stop automatically. You do not need to tell HMRC separately.

What happens after you claim Universal Credit
1. Day you submit the online claim: this becomes the first day of your UC assessment period and triggers HMRC to end your tax credits.
2. HMRC will calculate a final tax-credit award. You may get a final payment or, if you were overpaid, they’ll tell you how they’ll recover the debt (usually by deducting small amounts from future UC).
3. Universal Credit is assessed over a calendar month. At the end of that month, DWP work out what you’re entitled to.
4. Payment is made within 7 days after the assessment period ends, so you normally get your first UC payment about 5 weeks after you applied.
5. If you can’t manage during the wait, you can ask for a UC advance online or by phone and repay it from future UC.

Key points to remember
• Your tax credits stop even if your UC claim is later refused, so check you’re eligible before applying.  
• There is no 2-week “run-on” for tax credits (unlike some other legacy benefits).  
• Keep any letters from HMRC and DWP and check the figures – contact them straight away if something looks wrong.  
• If you need help budgeting for the 5-week wait, speak to your work coach or a free advice service.

More information: Benefits overview on GOV.UK – https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: If I'm self-employed and can't claim Statutory Maternity Pay, can I still get Maternity Allowance and how does it affect my other benefits?
generated_response:
Yes. If you are self-employed and do not qualify for Statutory Maternity Pay (SMP), you can usually claim Maternity Allowance (MA) instead.

1. Check you meet the MA work test  
   • You must have been employed or self-employed for at least 26 weeks in the 66 weeks before your baby is due.  
   • In any 13 of those weeks you must have earned (or had profits) of at least £30 a week.  
   • If you have paid Class 2 National Insurance contributions for the whole test period you meet the earnings part automatically.

2. Work out what you could get  
   • Standard MA is paid for up to 39 weeks at the lower of 90% of your average weekly earnings or the flat rate (currently £172.48 a week).  
   • If you have not paid enough Class 2 NICs you may still get the reduced rate of £27 a week.  
   • MA comes with automatic National Insurance credits that count towards your State Pension.

3. Claim  
   • Complete form MA1 and send it to Jobcentre Plus with your proof of earnings (for self-employed people this is usually your Small Earnings Exception certificate or evidence of Class 2 NICs).  
   • You can send the form by post or upload it via your online benefits account if you have one.  
   • The earliest you can claim is 26 weeks into pregnancy; payments start 11 weeks before the due date at the earliest.

Impact on other benefits

Universal Credit  
• MA counts as unearned income and is deducted in full from your monthly Universal Credit award.  
• You must report the start and end of MA in your online UC journal.

Tax Credits (if you are still on the legacy system)  
• MA is treated as income, but the first £100 a week is ignored.  
• Tell HMRC within 1 month of getting MA so your award is worked out correctly.

Housing Benefit and Council Tax Reduction (legacy schemes)  
• MA is treated as income and can reduce the amount you receive.  
• Inform your local council of the exact weekly amount.

‘New Style’ Jobseeker’s Allowance or Employment and Support Allowance  
• You cannot be paid MA at the same time as either of these contribution-based benefits.  

Other points  
• MA does not affect Child Benefit.  
• It gives you Class 1 NI credits each week, protecting your State Pension record.  
• If you get any means-tested benefit, always report your MA award promptly to avoid overpayments.

If the rules for your situation are complicated, speak to a benefits adviser (for example at Citizens Advice) or call the Maternity Allowance helpline on 0800 169 0283.

More information  
GOV.UK – Benefits and Maternity Allowance: https://www.gov.uk/maternity-allowance

prompt: If I stop receiving Child Benefit payments, can I still get the National Insurance credits for my child?
generated_response:
Yes – you can still get the National Insurance (NI) credits linked to Child Benefit even if you stop the payments, but you must keep your Child Benefit claim open.

How it works  
• NI credits for parents and carers of children under 12 are awarded automatically to the person who claims Child Benefit.  
• If your income is high and you do not want to receive the money (to avoid the High Income Child Benefit Charge), ask HMRC to “stop the payments” rather than ending the claim.  
• As long as the claim stays active, the credits continue to build up towards your State Pension.

What to do  
1. If you already claim Child Benefit:  
   • Contact the Child Benefit Office and ask them to stop the payments but keep the claim open.  
   • They will issue ‘nil-rate’ payments, which secure your NI credits without any money being paid.  

2. If you have cancelled your claim in the past:  
   • Make a new Child Benefit claim straight away – you can ask for payments not to be made.  
   • Credits can only be back-dated for up to three months, so act quickly.

3. Check your National Insurance record each year (through your personal tax account on GOV.UK) to make sure the credits are showing.

If you are unsure whether your claim is still active, call the Child Benefit helpline on 0300 200 3100.

More information:  
GOV.UK – Child Benefit and the high income charge: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-tax-charge

prompt: Can I still receive my State Pension if I'm living abroad?
generated_response:
Yes. Once you reach State Pension age you can claim your UK State Pension and have it paid to you abroad.

Key points

• Where it can be paid  
  – The State Pension can be paid into a UK bank, a bank in the country you live in, or certain international bank accounts.  
  – Payments are made every 4 weeks (or every 13 weeks if you ask).  

• Annual increases (‘uprating’)  
  – You keep getting the yearly State Pension increase only if you live in:  
    • the EU, Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, Switzerland or Gibraltar  
    • a country that has a social-security agreement with the UK which covers uprating (for example the USA or New Zealand).  
  – If you live in any other country your pension is frozen at the rate you first receive it, and it only goes up again if you later move back to the UK or to an uprating country.

• Before you move (or as soon as you decide to claim from abroad)  
  1. Contact the International Pension Centre (IPC) – they handle State Pension payments outside the UK.  
  2. Give them your overseas address, bank details and the date you want payments to start.  
  3. Check whether the country you are moving to taxes UK pensions and, if so, whether a double-taxation treaty applies.

• While you are abroad  
  – Tell the IPC straight away about any change of address, bank account or personal circumstances.  
  – From time to time you may be asked to complete a ‘life certificate’ to confirm you are still entitled to the pension—return it promptly to avoid payments being stopped.

If you need more detail or the IPC’s contact numbers, see ‘State Pension if you retire abroad’ on GOV.UK.

prompt: I'm a subcontractor in England and I'm considering working for a contractor. Do I need to register for the Construction Industry Scheme (CIS) and what happens if I don't?
generated_response:
Yes – if you’re paid as a self-employed subcontractor for construction work in the UK, you usually need to register for the Construction Industry Scheme (CIS).

Why register
• HMRC treats unregistered subcontractors as higher risk.  
• Contractors must deduct 30% from your payments (before materials) if you are not registered.  
• If you register, the deduction rate falls to 20%.  
• You still complete a Self Assessment tax return each year, but the amounts already deducted count towards the tax you owe.

Who must register
• Individuals, partnerships or companies that carry out construction work (building, repairs, demolition, site preparation, decorating and similar) for a contractor.  
• If you trade only as an employee on PAYE, CIS does not apply.

How to register
1. Make sure you have a Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) and are set up for Self Assessment.  
2. Go to GOV.UK and search “register as a CIS subcontractor” or call the CIS helpline if you prefer to register by phone.  
3. Provide your UTR, National Insurance number (or company registration details), and the business name you trade under.  
4. HMRC will confirm your registration to you and to any contractor who checks your details.

If you do not register
• Contractors must deduct 30% instead of 20% from your labour charges.  
• You may receive less cash flow and could struggle to cover business costs.  
• You still have to declare the income and may have to pay extra tax, interest or penalties if deductions do not cover what you owe.  
• Persistent non-compliance can trigger HMRC enquiries and financial penalties.

Next steps
• Gather your UTR and National Insurance number.  
• Register online or by phone before starting work with the contractor to avoid the 30% rate.  
• Keep records of all CIS statements from your contractor so you can offset the deductions on your tax return.

Further information: GOV.UK – Tax (https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax) and search “Construction Industry Scheme”.

prompt: I want to claim a National Insurance refund, but I'm not sure which class I fall into. How do I determine which class my refund is for?
generated_response:
To claim the right National Insurance (NI) refund you first need to know which class of NI you’ve paid. Check the points below against your own situation.

1. Look at your payslip, P60 or Self Assessment tax return  
   • If the deductions line says “NI” or “NIC” and you are an employee, you almost certainly pay Class 1.  
   • If you are self-employed and file a Self Assessment return, your bill shows Class 2 and possibly Class 4.  
   • If you make extra voluntary payments, they will be Class 3.

2. Match your work situation to the classes  
   • Class 1 – you work for an employer and NI is taken through PAYE.  
   • Class 1A or 1B – paid only by employers on benefits like a company car; individuals do not claim these back.  
   • Class 2 – flat weekly amount for self-employed people.  
   • Class 3 – voluntary payments you make to fill gaps in your record.  
   • Class 4 – profit-based NI for self-employed people, worked out in Self Assessment.

3. Typical reasons for refunds  
   • Class 1 – you had more than one job, changed jobs mid-year or earned above the annual upper limit.  
   • Class 2 – your profits were below the Small Profits Threshold or you paid twice (for example after amending a return).  
   • Class 3 – you paid for a year that later turned out to be ‘full’ already.  
   • Class 4 – you overpaid because your profits fell after submitting an amended return.

4. What to do next  
   a) Confirm which class(es) you paid using the documents in step 1.  
   b) If it’s Class 1: ask your employer first (they may correct it through payroll). If that is not possible, send form CA4139 or write to HMRC NI Contributions Office.  
   c) If it’s Class 2 or 4: amend your Self Assessment return or use form CA8480 for a direct refund.  
   d) If it’s Class 3: contact HMRC NI helpline and give the payment reference you used.

5. Get help if you’re unsure  
   • Call the HMRC National Insurance helpline on 0300 200 3500.  
   • Or see the guidance under ‘Tax’ on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

If after checking the documents you still cannot tell which class you paid, ring the helpline with your National Insurance number and they will confirm it for you before you apply for the refund.

prompt: How do I access HMRC services once I have an online account?
generated_response:
You use the same Government Gateway details each time.

1. Go to GOV.UK and choose ‘Sign in to your HMRC online account’ (or search: “HMRC sign in”).  
2. Enter your Government Gateway user ID and password.  
3. Type in the 6-digit security code sent by text, voice call or authenticator app (this is the 2-step verification).  

Once you are signed in

• You land on your Personal Tax Account “home” page. Click the tile for the service you need, for example  
  – Self Assessment tax return  
  – PAYE income tax and P60/P45 details  
  – National Insurance record  
  – Tax credits, Child Benefit or Tax-Free Childcare  
  – VAT, CIS or other business taxes (if you have them)  

• If the service you want is not listed, select ‘Add a tax, duty or scheme’ and follow the on-screen steps. You may need information such as your Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) or VAT number the first time you add a service.  

• Use the menu within each service to:  
  – check balances and deadlines  
  – submit returns or forms  
  – make a payment or set up a Direct Debit  
  – update personal details (address, bank account, authorised agent)  
  – read secure messages and notices from HMRC.  

Other ways to access

• HMRC app – sign in with the same Government Gateway details to quickly view your tax code, NI number, and make PAYE payments.  
• Telephone or webchat – you may be asked to verify your identity with information from your online account.  

If you forget your user ID or password, use the ‘recover user ID’ or ‘reset password’ links on the sign-in page and follow the prompts.  

Always sign out and close the browser or app when you have finished.  

For links to every HMRC service, start at:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I'm a landlord and need to know what I should do to prepare for Making Tax Digital for Income Tax. What are the key steps I need to follow?
generated_response:
Making Tax Digital (MTD) for Income Tax will replace the annual Self Assessment return with digital record-keeping and quarterly online updates.

Key steps to get ready:

1. Check when you have to join  
   • From April 2026 if your combined property and/or self-employment income is more than £50,000 a year.  
   • From April 2027 if it is £30,000–£50,000.  
   (If you earn below £30,000 you can join voluntarily for now.)

2. Review how you keep your records  
   • You will need to keep digital records of all rental income and allowable expenses.  
   • Spreadsheets are fine if they link to compatible “bridging” software, otherwise you will need MTD-ready accounting software.

3. Choose MTD-compatible software early  
   • HMRC lists suppliers that already meet the MTD standard.  
   • Make sure the package lets you send quarterly updates and the end-of-period statement (EOPS) direct to HMRC.  
   • Allow time to move data across and learn the system.

4. Separate each property business and trade  
   • If you also have self-employment income you must keep and submit digital records for each source separately.  
   • Jointly-owned property: each owner must keep their own digital records and submit their own updates.

5. Sign up for MTD for Income Tax  
   • You will sign in with your Government Gateway user ID and link your chosen software to HMRC.  
   • Do this after your software is set up – the software usually walks you through the sign-up.

6. Understand the new reporting cycle  
   • Four quarterly updates during the tax year (not payment demands – payment dates stay the same).  
   • An EOPS after the year-end to finalise figures and claim any further reliefs.  
   • A final “Final Declaration” to confirm all your income before 31 January following the tax year.

7. Keep paying and budgeting as normal  
   • Payment on account and balancing payment dates do not change.  
   • Continue to keep evidence (invoices, receipts) for at least 5 years in case HMRC asks.

If you use an agent, talk to them now so they can add you to compatible software and deal with sign-up on your behalf.

More detail and the list of MTD-compatible software is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/sign-up-your-business-for-making-tax-digital-for-income-tax

prompt: What are the options for paying my VAT bill and how do I get VAT repayments into my bank account?
generated_response:
Paying your VAT bill

You can pay electronically or, in limited cases, at a bank. HMRC must receive cleared funds by the deadline shown on your VAT return (usually 1 month + 7 days after the period ends).

Common electronic options:

1. Direct Debit  
   • Set it up in your VAT online account (‘Manage account’ → ‘Direct Debit’).  
   • Money leaves your account automatically 3 working days after the payment deadline.

2. Faster Payments (online or mobile banking)  
   • Use HMRC’s sort code 08-32-10 and the correct 9-digit VAT reference.  
   • Usually reaches HMRC the same or next day, including weekends.

3. CHAPS  
   • Same-day payment if you submit before your bank’s cut-off time.  
   • Use the same HMRC sort code and reference as Faster Payments.

4. Bacs  
   • Takes up to 3 working days to clear, so initiate it early.

5. Debit or corporate credit card (online only)  
   • Pay through your VAT online account.  
   • Card fees apply to corporate credit cards. Personal credit cards are not accepted.

6. Standing order (mainly for Annual Accounting or agreed Time to Pay plans).  
   • Set up with your bank using your VAT reference.

7. At a bank or building society (cash or cheque)  
   • You need an HMRC paying-in slip. This option is being phased out and is only available if HMRC still issues slips to you.  
   • Allow 3 working days for the payment to clear.

Getting VAT repayments paid straight into your bank

HMRC normally repays VAT by Bacs within 10 working days of receiving your return—if they have your bank details.

To add or change your repayment account:

1. Sign in to your VAT online account (Government Gateway).  
2. Select ‘View business details’.  
3. Choose ‘Add or change repayment details’.  
4. Enter the UK bank or building-society account name, sort code and account number that accept Bacs payments.  
5. Save the changes. The new details are usually active after 2–3 working days.

If you do not give bank details, HMRC will send a payable-order cheque, which takes longer and has to be paid in at your bank branch.

Need more help?

Full guidance is on GOV.UK – search ‘Pay your VAT bill’ and ‘VAT repayments’. Or call the VAT helpline if you hit a problem.

prompt: After valuing the estate, how do I report its value to the tax authorities and what are the deadlines for paying Inheritance Tax?
generated_response:
After you have worked out the estate’s value there are two jobs:

A) tell HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) what the estate is worth  
B) pay any Inheritance Tax (IHT) that is due

The exact form you use depends on whether any IHT is owed.

1. Check if the estate is an ‘excepted estate’  
   • Most estates where no IHT is due (because they are under the £325,000 ‘nil-rate band’ or everything passes to a spouse or civil partner or to charity) are classed as excepted.  
   • For deaths on or after 1 January 2022 you do not need to fill in a separate IHT form for an excepted estate in England or Wales. You simply give the estate figures in the online probate application or on PA1P (paper probate form).

2. If the estate is NOT excepted (IHT is due or the estate does not meet the excepted rules)  
   a) Phone HMRC’s Inheritance Tax helpline on 0300 123 1072 and ask for an IHT reference number. Do this at least three weeks before you pay.  
   b) Complete form IHT400 and any schedules that apply (for example assets held in trust or business property).  
   c) Send the IHT400 pack to HMRC, normally within 12 months of the person’s death.  
   d) Pay at least the tax on cash and listed-share assets at the same time (see step 4 below).

3. How to pay the tax  
   • Direct Payment Scheme: ask the deceased’s bank or building society to pay HMRC direct using form IHT423.  
   • From your own or estate funds by online banking, cheque or CHAPS, quoting the IHT reference.  
   • You can claim the money back from the estate once funds are released.

4. Deadlines  
   • Tax payment deadline: the end of the sixth month after the person’s death. Example: death in January → pay by 31 July. Interest is charged on anything unpaid after that date.  
   • Reporting deadline (IHT400): 12 months from the end of the month of death. Late filing penalties apply.

5. Paying by instalments for property or some unlisted shares  
   • You can pay the IHT on certain assets (most often a house) in up to 10 yearly instalments.  
   • The first instalment is still due by the six-month deadline; interest is charged on the outstanding balance.

6. Submit the probate application  
   • HMRC will send you a receipt (form IHT421) after they process your IHT400 and confirm payment.  
   • Attach that receipt to your probate application so the Probate Registry can issue the grant.

If you are not sure whether the estate is excepted or need help filling in IHT400, call the HMRC helpline or speak to a probate adviser.

Further guidance is on GOV.UK:  
Inheritance Tax – how to value, report and pay: https://www.gov.uk/inheritance-tax  
General tax forms and contact details: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I received a Simple Assessment letter from HMRC and need to pay my tax bill. How do I know when I need to pay and what options do I have for making the payment?
generated_response:
Your Simple Assessment letter tells you two key things:

• the exact amount you owe  
• the payment deadline

When to pay  
1. Look at page 1 of the letter. The deadline is either:  
   • 31 January after the end of the tax year, or  
   • 3 months from the date on the letter – HMRC uses the later of the two.  
2. If you miss the deadline you’ll be charged interest and may get late-payment penalties, so put the date in your diary.

Ways to pay  
Pick one of the methods below and use the 14-digit payment reference that starts with ‘XA’ (it’s on the letter).

Fastest (same or next working day)  
1. Online or telephone banking – use Faster Payments, CHAPS or Bacs to HMRC Cumbernauld:  
   • Sort code 08-32-10  
   • Account number 12001039  
   • Account name: HMRC Cumbernauld  
2. Debit or corporate credit card – pay online through GOV.UK Pay (credit cards incur a fee).

Takes 3–5 working days  
3. Direct Debit – set this up in your Personal Tax Account; allow five working days the first time.  
4. Cheque by post – payable to ‘HM Revenue and Customs only’, write your XA reference on the back.

In person  
5. At your bank or building society with the payslip from the letter (cash or cheque).  

If you can’t pay on time  
Contact HMRC’s Payment Support Service on 0300 200 3820 before the deadline to ask for a Time to Pay arrangement.

More detail: gov.uk/simple-assessment

prompt: If someone I owe money to applies for my bankruptcy, what will happen and how can I prepare for the process?
generated_response:
If a creditor (someone you owe at least £5,000) asks the court to make you bankrupt, the process is called a creditor’s bankruptcy petition.  

What normally happens

1. Warning notice  
   • The creditor usually serves you with a statutory demand first.  
   • If the debt is not paid or set aside within 21 days, they can present a bankruptcy petition to the court.  

2. Court petition and hearing  
   • You will receive a copy of the petition and a hearing date.  
   • At the hearing a judge decides whether to make a bankruptcy order.  
   • If the order is made, you are officially bankrupt from that date.  

3. Official Receiver takes control  
   • The Official Receiver (OR) is appointed immediately.  
   • You must give the OR full details of your income, assets, debts and spending within 21 days.  
   • Bank accounts are usually frozen and any surplus money or valuable assets may be sold to repay creditors.  

4. Restrictions and impact  
   • Bankruptcy normally lasts 12 months, but some restrictions (for example on getting credit over £500) apply straight away.  
   • Your credit file is marked and your details are published in the Insolvency Register.  
   • Certain jobs and tenancies can be affected, so check your employment contract and lease.  

How to prepare

1. Get debt advice as soon as you receive a statutory demand or petition. An adviser can check if the debt is correct and whether you have any grounds to challenge it or ask for time to pay.  

2. Gather information:  
   • Recent bank statements, wage slips, benefit letters, tenancy or mortgage details.  
   • A list of all debts, assets, pensions and insurance policies.  
   • Proof of essential monthly costs (rent, utilities, travel, childcare).  

3. Decide if you want to oppose or accept the petition. You may be able to:  
   • Negotiate a payment plan or settlement with the creditor.  
   • Apply for an Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) instead.  
   • Ask the court for an adjournment if you need more time to organise one of these options.  

4. Protect day-to-day banking:  
   • Open a basic bank account with a bank you do not currently owe money to.  
   • Move your salary or benefits into that account before your existing accounts are frozen.  

5. Check what you can keep:  
   • Everyday household items, tools you need for work and a reasonable car are usually exempt, but luxury items are not.  
   • Gather any proof you have of an item’s necessity or low resale value.  

6. Notify anyone who might be affected:  
   • Your employer, if your role is regulated.  
   • Your landlord or mortgage lender, if bankruptcy could breach your agreement.  

7. Keep communicating with the Official Receiver. Late or incomplete information can prolong the process or cause extra restrictions.  

If you are unsure about any stage, or think the petition is incorrect, contact a free debt adviser or a solicitor experienced in insolvency. Further guidance is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/bankruptcy

prompt: I'm interested in starting a postgraduate course. Where can I find information about these courses and how do I apply for them?
generated_response:
Most postgraduate courses are organised and advertised by the universities themselves, but there are a few reliable places to start and a common application process.

Where to find courses  
1. Use the UCAS Postgraduate course search to browse taught master’s, research degrees and postgraduate teacher-training courses across the UK.  
2. Check each university’s own website for full course pages, detailed entry requirements, term dates and fees.  
3. For research degrees (MPhil, PhD) look at university doctoral college pages and the FindAPhD or jobs.ac.uk listings for funded projects.  
4. GOV.UK’s education and learning section lists links to postgraduate study and student finance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education  

How to apply  
1. Note the application route shown on the course page.  
   • Most universities ask you to apply directly through their online portal.  
   • Around 30 universities use the UCAS Postgraduate system instead (mainly for PGCE teacher training and some master’s courses).  
2. Gather the documents you will need:  
   • degree transcript and certificate  
   • personal statement or research proposal  
   • two academic or professional references  
   • updated CV  
   • proof of English language level if required  
3. Submit your application before the course deadline. Taught master’s courses often work on a rolling basis, but competitive programmes and PGCEs set fixed dates.  
4. If you receive an offer, accept it online and follow the university’s instructions to meet any conditions (for example, final degree result or references).  
5. Arrange funding early:  
   • apply for a Postgraduate Master’s Loan or Doctoral Loan on GOV.UK if you are eligible  
   • explore university scholarships, research council studentships or employer sponsorship.  
6. Once your place and funding are confirmed, the university will send enrolment details and practical information about accommodation, registration and induction.

If you are unsure which application route a particular course uses, email the university’s admissions office—they will point you to the correct form and deadline.

prompt: Can my child get free school transport if they have a mobility problem?
generated_response:
Yes – children who cannot reasonably walk to school because of a mobility or medical condition are normally entitled to free home-to-school transport.

What counts  
• Your child’s mobility issue makes it unsafe or impractical for them to walk the usual statutory distance (2 miles for under-8s, 3 miles for 8 plus), even if you accompany them.  
• They attend the nearest suitable school for their age and needs.  
• The council accepts medical or specialist evidence that the condition affects their ability to travel.

How to apply  
1. Contact the education or school transport team at your local council and ask for a “home-to-school transport” application form for children with additional support needs.  
2. Provide the evidence they ask for – this could be a doctor’s letter, hospital report, educational psychologist report or your child’s coordinated support plan (if they have one).  
3. The council will assess the information, decide if your child is eligible and tell you the outcome in writing.  
4. If approved, the council will arrange suitable transport (for example a taxi, minibus or an escort to accompany your child) at no cost to you.  
5. If refused, you can ask for the decision to be reviewed. The refusal letter will explain how to appeal.

Tips  
• Apply as early as possible — councils often need several weeks to organise transport before a new term.  
• If your child’s condition is temporary (for example after surgery), some councils can agree short-term transport; make that clear on the form.  
• Keep copies of all forms and medical evidence you send.

Where to find more information  
Check your council’s website or phone its education department. Guidance on school transport is also on GOV.UK under Education and learning: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

If you are unsure about the evidence required or the appeal process, speak to your child’s school or a local advice service.

prompt: My child is 10 years old and attends a school 2 miles away. Do we qualify for free school transport?
generated_response:
Probably not, unless you meet one of the special-case rules.

Standard distance rule  
• In Scotland, councils must give free home-to-school transport if the child is:  
  – under 8 and the school is more than 2 miles away, or  
  – 8 or over and the school is more than 3 miles away (measured by the shortest safe walking route).

Your child is 10, the school is 2 miles away, so the standard distance rule is not met.

Exceptions that can still give you free transport  
1. Low-income entitlement – if your child gets free school meals or you receive the maximum Working Tax Credit, the council must provide transport if the school is 2 – 6 miles away.  
2. Unsafe walking route – if the only walking route is judged unsafe and no reasonable alternative exists.  
3. Additional support needs or disability – transport can be provided regardless of distance.  
4. Council discretion – some councils choose to be more generous than the legal minimum.

What to do next  
1. Check whether you qualify under any of the exceptions above.  
2. If you think you do, apply through your council’s school transport or education section (forms are usually online).  
3. If you do not qualify but believe the route is unsafe, ask the council for a formal route safety assessment.  
4. If your application is refused, you can use the council’s appeals process.

More information  
GOV.UK: Free school transport – https://www.gov.uk/free-school-transport

If you are still unsure, contact your local council’s education or school transport team for guidance.

prompt: How can I make a one-off repayment towards my student loan, and can I do this without signing in?
generated_response:
You have a few ways to make a single (lump-sum) repayment to the Student Loans Company (SLC).

1. Online without signing in  
   • Find your Student Loan customer reference number (the 11-digit number on letters from SLC).  
   • Go to the repayment page on GOV.UK, choose ‘Make a repayment’ and select the option to pay without signing in.  
   • Enter your reference number, date of birth and the amount you want to pay.  
   • Pay by debit card (Visa, Mastercard or Maestro). You’ll get an email confirmation.

2. Bank transfer  
   • Use online or telephone banking to send the money to:  
     Account name: Student Loans Company  
     Sort code: 08-32-10  
     Account number: 12001039  
   • Put your 11-digit customer reference number as the payment reference so the money goes to the right loan.  
   • Keep the bank transfer receipt as proof.

3. By phone  
   • Call SLC repayments on 0300 100 0611 (Monday to Friday, office hours).  
   • Have your customer reference number and debit card ready. They’ll process the payment while you’re on the line.

4. Cheque  
   • Make the cheque payable to ‘Student Loans Company’.  
   • Write your customer reference number, full name and address on the back.  
   • Post it to:  
     Student Loans Company  
     PO Box 555  
     Darlington  
     DL1 9YZ

Things to check first  
• Your loan type and remaining balance—early repayments are voluntary, so make sure the lump sum fits your budget.  
• If you pay in full, ask SLC for written confirmation that your balance is cleared.

Full guidance is on GOV.UK under Education and Learning → Student finance → Repaying your student loan: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

prompt: I'm looking to apply for a secondary school place for my child, but I'm not very tech-savvy. How can I apply without using online forms?
generated_response:
You do not have to use an online form to apply for a secondary school place. You can apply in writing or over the phone through your local council’s education admissions team.

1. Find your council’s education admissions contact details.
   • Go to GOV.UK and enter your postcode in “Find your local council”, or look on any recent council letter.  
2. Phone the admissions team and explain that you want a paper (or phone) application because you are not comfortable applying online.  
3. Ask them to:  
   a) post you a paper application pack, or  
   b) take your details over the phone, or  
   c) book an appointment so you can fill in the form at the council office or a local library.  
4. When you receive the form, fill in:  
   • your child’s details (name, date of birth, current school)  
   • your preferred secondary schools (list them in order)  
   • any supporting reasons (for example, sibling already at the school, additional support needs).  
5. Include any documents the form asks for, such as proof of address. Do not send originals unless the council specifically asks for them.  
6. Return the completed form by the deadline. Use the pre-paid envelope if one is provided, or hand it in at the council office and get a receipt.  
7. Keep a copy of everything you send and note the date you sent it.  
8. Wait for the council’s decision letter. They will normally send this by post on the national offer day for your area.

If you need help filling in the form, you can:

• Ask the council for a face-to-face appointment or a phone callback.  
• Visit your local library or community hub—staff can often help with forms and photocopying.  
• Contact your child’s current school; many schools have a family support worker who can guide you through the process.

Important: Application deadlines vary between councils (usually in October – December for entry the following August). Apply as early as you can.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

prompt: As a teacher in Scotland, I need to guide a student who started their course before August 2016 on how to apply for student finance. What are the key steps they need to take and what forms are required?
generated_response:
Students who began a higher-education course before 1 August 2016 have to use paper forms each year because the online system does not cover them.  

Key steps

1. Get the right form  
   • Full-time courses: download form PR1 for the current academic year – look for the version that says “continuing students who started their course before 1 August 2016”.  
   • Part-time courses: download form PTL1 (and PTLC if they also need a Tuition Fee Loan).  
   All are on the Student Finance forms page: https://www.gov.uk/student-finance-forms  

2. Read the accompanying notes booklet so the student knows which sections to complete and what evidence to send.

3. Gather evidence  
   • National Insurance number.  
   • Valid UK passport details (or original identity evidence if they have never supplied this before).  
   • Bank account details for payments.  
   • Household-income evidence (for income-assessed support), for example parents’ or partner’s P60 or Self Assessment statement.

4. Complete the form in black ink, sign it, and make sure anyone providing income details signs their section.

5. Photocopy everything for the student’s records, then post the original form and any required evidence to the address on the back page. Recorded delivery is recommended.

6. Wait for the assessment letter (normally 4–6 weeks). Because there is no online account for pre-2016 students, updates come by post or by calling Student Finance.

7. Report any changes (course, university, personal details) using the CO1 “change of circumstances” form from the same GOV.UK page.

Deadlines  
• Aim to submit the form at least nine weeks before the course starts to have funding in place on time.  
• Late applications are accepted up to nine months after the start of the academic year, but payments will be delayed.

If anything is unclear, the safest next step is to check the Student Finance forms page or ring the Student Finance England helpline on 0300 100 0607.

prompt: As a teacher, I need to know how to support a student who cannot afford school uniforms. What options are available?
generated_response:
Help is available from several places. The parent (or carer) will usually need to apply, but you can guide them and supply any proof the school must give.

1. Local council uniform grant  
   • Some councils give a one-off grant for school clothing (often £30 – £150).  
   • Ask the parent to search “[council name] school uniform grant” or look under Benefits on the council website.  
   • If a form needs the school to confirm attendance, help complete it quickly.

2. Pupil Premium or free-school-meals funding  
   • If the pupil is eligible for free school meals (or has been in the last 6 years) the school gets extra Pupil Premium money.  
   • The headteacher can use part of this to buy uniform or vouchers. Remind senior staff that the funding is flexible.

3. School hardship or welfare fund  
   • Many schools and academy trusts keep a small hardship pot for emergencies.  
   • Tell the parent how to apply—usually a short, confidential form to the head or business manager.

4. 16–19 Bursary (for sixth-form students)  
   • Up to £1,200 a year if the student is in care, on certain benefits or facing serious hardship.  
   • The bursary can cover essential clothing, including uniform for lower-school duties.

5. Second-hand uniform schemes  
   • Check if the PTA or school office runs termly “pre-loved” sales.  
   • Local community groups, church halls and charity shops may also stock the school’s uniform items.  
   • Make sure pupils know these options are normal and discreet.

6. Local charities and community funds  
   • Organisations such as the Salvation Army, Barnardo’s or small local trusts sometimes give small grants or supply items directly.  
   • A simple supporting letter on school headed paper often helps the application.

Tips for you as a teacher  
• Keep the request private: direct the family to a named staff member who deals with finance or welfare.  
• Offer to print any online forms and help fill them in if digital access is a problem.  
• Follow up after a week to check the family has made progress and to see if the school can bridge any gap meanwhile (for example, spare items from lost property).

Official information  
Parents can read the GOV.UK page “Help with school clothing costs” via the Education section: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

If you ever need clarification or the parent’s council does not list a scheme, call the council’s education or benefits team for the latest position.

prompt: I found some illegal posters on my street. How do I report this to the right authority?
generated_response:
Fly-posting (putting up adverts or posters without permission) is usually dealt with by your local council.

1. Find your local council  
   • Go to GOV.UK, choose ‘Housing and local services’, then ‘Your local council’.  
   • Enter your postcode to get the council’s contact page.

2. Report the posters  
   • Look for an online form or section called ‘graffiti and fly-posting’, ‘street cleansing’ or similar.  
   • Give the exact location (street name, nearest house number or landmark).  
   • Describe the posters and say how many there are.  
   • Attach a photo if the form allows—this helps the council find them quickly.

3. If you cannot use the online form  
   • Phone the council’s environmental or street care team.  
   • Explain you are reporting fly-posting and give the same details.

4. When to involve the police  
   • If the posters are threatening, racist or otherwise illegal content, call 101 (non-emergency) and report it to the police as well as the council.

Keep a note of any reference number the council gives you so you can follow up if the posters are not removed.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: Can I appeal a lawful development certificate decision if I already received an enforcement notice?
generated_response:
No. If an enforcement notice has already been served for the same development, you cannot appeal the council’s decision (or non-decision) on a certificate of lawfulness. Planning law stops you running the certificate and enforcement appeal routes in parallel.

What you can do instead

1. Appeal the enforcement notice itself.  
   • In Scotland you normally have 28 days from the date the notice was served to lodge an appeal with the Planning and Environmental Appeals Division (DPEA).  
   • One of the permitted grounds is that the development is in fact lawful, so you can still present any evidence you had planned to use for the certificate.

2. Check the notice for mistakes – for example the wrong site plan, incorrect dates, or quoting the wrong planning breach. Errors can be another ground of appeal.

3. If your deadline has already passed, speak to the planning authority straight away. They may agree to withdraw or vary the notice if you provide convincing evidence, but they do not have to.

4. If you think planning permission could be granted, you can apply for retrospective planning permission. This does not stop the enforcement process, but the council can choose to withdraw the notice if permission is later granted.

Need help?

• See the enforcement notice appeal guidance on the Scottish Government/DPEA website.  
• If you are unsure about deadlines or evidence, get professional planning advice as soon as possible.

General planning and housing guidance can be found at: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I'm 65 and live in Scotland. I'm on a low income and have high energy costs. How do I apply for the Warm Home Discount Scheme and what happens if I don't get it for winter 2024 to 2025?
generated_response:
Warm Home Discount for Scotland (winter 2024 to 2025)

1. Check if you will get it automatically  
   • If you get the Guarantee Credit part of Pension Credit you are in the ‘core group’. You should receive a letter in autumn telling you the £150 discount will be taken off your electricity bill by 31 March 2025. You do not need to apply.  
   • If you are not on Guarantee Credit, move to step 2.

2. Apply through your electricity supplier (‘broader group’)  
   • From around October 2024, go to your supplier’s website or phone their Warm Home Discount team.  
   • Ask for a Warm Home Discount application form (some suppliers take details over the phone or online).  
   • Have ready: your account number, National Insurance number, and evidence of the benefits or low-income criteria the supplier lists.  
   • Apply as soon as the scheme opens – suppliers have a fixed number of discounts and close applications once the pot is used up.  
   • Your supplier will confirm in writing if you will get the £150. The money is applied to your electricity account (or sent as a voucher if you use pre-payment meters) by 31 March 2025.

3. If you think you should have had it but do not get it  
   • First, ask your supplier to review the decision – mistakes do happen.  
   • If you are in the core group and have had no letter by mid-December, call the Warm Home Discount helpline (number will be on GOV.UK once the 2024-25 scheme opens).

If you still do not receive the discount

• You simply will not get the £150 off your bill for that winter, but other help is available:  
  – Winter Fuel Payment: all households with someone born before 23 September 1958 should get £250-£600 automatically.  
  – Winter Heating Payment (Scotland): paid if you are on certain low-income benefits.  
  – Supplier hardship funds or fuel vouchers: ask your energy company or a local advice agency.  
  – Home Energy Scotland (0808 808 2282): free advice and grants to cut bills, including help with insulation or heating upgrades.  
  – Check you are claiming everything you are entitled to (for example Pension Credit). Online calculators or Citizens Advice can help.

Key link  
Warm Home Discount Scheme overview: https://www.gov.uk/the-warm-home-discount-scheme

prompt: What should I do if my tenant asks for an inspection of the property?
generated_response:
If a tenant (contract-holder in Wales) asks you to inspect the property, treat it as a routine request and follow the normal access rules.

1. Reply within a few days and ask what they want you to look at (e.g. damp, broken appliance, general condition).  
2. Offer two or three dates and times, making sure they are reasonable for the tenant (evenings or weekends if the tenant works).  
3. Give at least 24 hours’ written notice of the visit. A short email or letter is fine – state:  
   • the date and time you will arrive  
   • who will attend (for example you, a managing agent or a tradesperson)  
   • the purpose of the visit (“inspection to check reported issues and condition of the property”).  
4. Wait for the tenant to confirm. You cannot enter without their agreement unless there is an emergency.  
5. On the day, arrive on time, show identification if asked and limit the inspection to what was agreed.  
6. Take notes and photos (with the tenant’s permission) so you have a clear record.  
7. After the visit, send the tenant a brief summary of what you found, any repairs you will arrange and likely timescales.  
8. Keep copies of the notice, the tenant’s consent and your inspection report in case of future disputes.

Remember  
• Landlords must keep the property safe and in good repair under Renting Homes (Wales) Act 2016 and general housing law.  
• Regular inspections (for example every 6–12 months) are allowed but always need the tenant’s consent and proper notice.  
• If the tenant reports a serious hazard (e.g. gas leak, no heating in winter), treat it as urgent and arrange repairs as soon as possible.

For general guidance on landlord responsibilities see GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: Can I recycle mobile phones and computers in Scotland? How do I do it?
generated_response:
Yes. Mobile phones, computers and other electrical items can all be recycled in Scotland. The main options are your local council recycling service or a retailer take-back scheme.

1. Clear your data  
   • Back up anything you need.  
   • Remove SIM or memory cards.  
   • Do a full factory reset or use secure-wipe software for computers.

2. Use your council’s recycling facilities  
   • Most household waste recycling centres (HWRCs) have a separate bay for electrical and electronic equipment (often labelled “WEEE”).  
   • Some councils also collect small electricals at the kerbside—check by entering your postcode at https://www.gov.uk/recycling-collections.  
   • Take the device to the centre or leave it out on the right collection day. Staff can tell you exactly where to put it.

3. Ask the retailer to take it back  
   • When you buy a new phone, computer or similar item in store or online, the seller must let you return an old device of roughly the same type for free under UK “take-back” rules.  
   • You usually have up to 28 days after your purchase to hand the old item over or post it back (check the retailer’s instructions).

4. Consider reuse before recycling  
   • Many charities and community projects accept working phones and computers.  
   • Some manufacturers and mobile networks run trade-in schemes that refurbish or responsibly recycle the device.

5. Don’t put electronics in your household waste bin  
   • Batteries, metals and plastics need separate treatment and can cause fires in collection vehicles.

If you’re unsure which option your council offers, enter your postcode on GOV.UK or phone the council’s waste services team for exact locations, opening hours and any booking requirements.

prompt: What happens if my dog attacks another person or animal?
generated_response:
If your dog injures someone, makes them fear being injured, or attacks another animal, the law treats the dog as ‘dangerously out of control’. The rules apply in Scotland whether it happens in public or on private property (including your own home or garden).

1. Make the situation safe  
   • Call the dog away or secure it on a lead.  
   • Offer help to anyone hurt and swap contact details.  
   • If another animal is injured, speak to the owner and offer to pay vet costs.

2. Expect police or council action  
   • Anyone can report the incident to Police Scotland or the local council’s animal control team.  
   • An officer can seize the dog if they think it’s a risk.  
   • The council can serve a Dog Control Notice (DCN) ordering you to: keep the dog on a lead or muzzled, arrange training, fit it with an identifying collar, or keep it securely fenced at home.

3. Possible criminal penalties  
   • Injuring a person: up to 5 years in prison and/or an unlimited fine.  
   • Making someone fear injury (no actual bite): up to 3 years’ prison and/or a fine.  
   • Attacking an assistance dog: up to 3 years’ prison and/or a fine.  
   • Attacking livestock: fines up to £40,000 and/or 12 months in prison under the Dogs (Protection of Livestock) (Amendment) (Scotland) Act 2021.  
   • The court can also order the dog to be destroyed or impose strict control conditions.

4. Civil (private) claims  
   • You may have to pay compensation for medical bills, loss of earnings, or vet costs.  
   • Check whether your pet, home or public-liability insurance covers the incident and report it straight away.

5. What to do next  
   • Take your dog to a vet to rule out pain-related aggression.  
   • Book a qualified behaviourist or training classes if required by a DCN.  
   • Keep records of all training and safety measures—you may need to show proof to the council or court.  
   • If you receive legal papers or a DCN, read them carefully and meet every deadline. Get legal advice if you are unsure.

Further guidance  
GOV.UK: Controlling your dog in public – https://www.gov.uk/control-dog-public

prompt: If I'm a tenant and want to get insulation through the Great British Insulation Scheme, what steps should I take and what do I need to ensure?
generated_response:
You can still benefit from the Great British Insulation Scheme (GBIS) as a tenant, but you will need your landlord’s co-operation.  

1. Check likely eligibility  
   • The property must usually have an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) rating of D, E, F or G.  
   • In Wales the council tax band must be A to E (A to D in England).  
   • If anyone in the household gets a means-tested benefit you may qualify under the “low-income” group even if the council tax band is higher.  
   • You can look up the EPC for free on the EPC register or ask your landlord for a copy.

2. Speak to your landlord or letting agent early  
   • Explain that the work can cut energy bills and may be fully or partly funded.  
   • Get their permission in writing – installers and energy suppliers will not proceed without it.  
   • Make sure they are willing to pay any contribution that might be required if the grant does not cover the full cost.

3. Gather basic information  
   • Property address, EPC number and council tax band.  
   • Proof of tenancy (for example, your tenancy agreement).  
   • Proof of qualifying benefits, if relevant.

4. Apply for the scheme  
   • Use the Great British Insulation Scheme page on GOV.UK to be matched with an approved installer or contact your energy supplier if they are taking applications.  
   • The installer will arrange a free survey to confirm what insulation (loft, cavity wall, solid-wall, room-in-roof, etc.) is suitable and how much funding is available.

5. Let the landlord review and sign the paperwork  
   • They will need to sign the customer consent form and any landlord contribution agreement.  
   • Check that the installer is TrustMark-registered and working to PAS 2030:2019 standards (a scheme requirement).

6. Installation and follow-up  
   • Agree an installation date that suits you and your landlord.  
   • Keep copies of all documents and the updated EPC once the work is finished.  
   • Report any snagging to the installer straight away.

Key things to ensure  
• Written landlord consent before anything starts.  
• No upfront payments until funding is confirmed.  
• The installer is TrustMark-registered – this protects you if something goes wrong.  
• The property’s EPC is updated after the work; this can improve rental prospects and helps prove the improvement.  
• You stay within the terms of your tenancy (for example, allowing reasonable access for surveys and installation).

If your landlord is reluctant, remind them of their legal duty to reach EPC band C by 2028 (proposed) and the fact that GBIS can reduce their costs.

Further information and the online application tool are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/apply-great-british-insulation-scheme

prompt: I'm running a limited company and I think I've overpaid Corporation Tax. How do I get a refund and what happens if I don't include my bank details on my Company Tax Return?
generated_response:
If your company has paid too much Corporation Tax, HMRC will repay the extra once you ask for it.

1. Check your latest Company Tax Return (CT600) and payment records to confirm the amount you overpaid.  
2. Ask for the refund:  
   • If you have not filed the return yet, complete the ‘Repayments and set-offs’ section of the CT600 and tick ‘yes’ to a repayment.  
   • If the return has already been filed, submit an amended CT600 (you can do this online within 12 months of the filing deadline) and request the repayment there.  
   • If you are outside the amendment window, write to HMRC’s Corporation Tax Services with your company name, UTR, accounting period and the amount you believe is due back.  
3. Include your bank details (account name, sort code, account number and, if relevant, building-society roll number) in the CT600 or in your letter.  
4. Keep a copy of everything you send and note the date. HMRC normally repays within a few weeks, but it can take longer at busy times. You can track progress through your company’s online tax account or by calling the Corporation Tax helpline.

What happens if you leave out your bank details?

• HMRC cannot make a BACS transfer without them.  
• They may send a payable-order cheque to your company’s registered office, or they may hold the credit and write asking for bank details. Both options usually take longer than a direct bank transfer.  
• If you owe other taxes, HMRC may set the credit off against those instead of issuing a refund.

If you have already filed your return without bank details, phone the Corporation Tax helpline or write to HMRC with the account information so they can process the repayment by BACS.

Further guidance: GOV.UK – Corporation Tax: refunds and set-offs (https://www.gov.uk/browse/business).

prompt: How do I find out if I need a licence to import live animals or animal products to Great Britain?
generated_response:
Follow these steps to check whether you need an import licence, certificate or other permission for live animals or animal products coming into Great Britain (England, Scotland and Wales).

1. Work out the basics  
   • Exactly what you plan to import (species, product type and whether it is for commercial use, research, breeding, pet movement, food, etc.).  
   • The country the consignment will come from.  
   • The route it will enter Great Britain (port or airport).

2. Check the official guidance  
   • Go to ‘Import or move live animals and animal products to Great Britain’ on GOV.UK:  
     https://www.gov.uk/guidance/import-or-move-live-animals-and-biological-material-to-great-britain  
   • Open the link for your commodity group (for example ‘livestock’, ‘pets’, ‘meat and meat products’, ‘dairy’, ‘fish’, ‘animal by-products’).  

3. Read the Import Information Note (IIN) for your commodity  
   • Each IIN sets out:  
     – whether an import licence is needed;  
     – the health certificate or commercial document required;  
     – any border control post (BCP) and pre-notification rules (IPAFFS);  
     – special conditions such as quarantine or testing.  

4. Confirm any licence requirement  
   • If the IIN says a licence is required, it tells you which form to use (for example an APHA ‘IMP’ licence) and how to apply.  
   • If the IIN says ‘no licence’, you still must follow any certificate and notification rules it lists.  

5. Get help if you are unsure  
   • If you cannot find an IIN that matches your goods, or the wording is unclear, contact the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA) Centre for International Trade:  
     – Email: citimports@apha.gov.uk  
     – Telephone: 03000 200 301 (option 1)  
   • They can confirm whether a licence is needed and guide you through the application.  

6. Keep evidence  
   • Save copies of the IIN, your licence (if issued), health certificates and IPAFFS notifications. Border officials may ask to see them when the consignment arrives.

If in any doubt, check the guidance again or contact APHA before the goods are shipped. It is the safest way to avoid delays, extra costs or the consignment being refused at the border.

prompt: As a subcontractor in the construction industry, do I have to register for the Construction Industry Scheme (CIS)?
generated_response:
You do not have to register for the Construction Industry Scheme (CIS), but it is almost always in your interest to do so.

Why register  
• If you register, contractors deduct tax at 20% from your payments.  
• If you do not register, contractors must deduct 30%.  
• You still sort out your final tax and National Insurance through Self Assessment, but registering means less money is held back up-front.

Who can register  
• Sole traders, partnerships and limited companies that are paid for construction work in the UK.  
• If you trade through a company and also pay other subcontractors, the company may have to register as both a contractor and a subcontractor.

How to register  
1. Make sure you have a Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) and a Government Gateway user ID.  
2. Go to GOV.UK and search for “Register as a CIS subcontractor” (under Business > Construction Industry Scheme).  
3. Sign in, complete the online form and submit.  
4. HMRC will send confirmation and explain how your contractor can verify you at the 20% rate.

If you can’t use the online service, call the CIS helpline and ask for a paper form.

Next steps  
• Tell each contractor your legal name, UTR and National Insurance or company number so they can verify you.  
• Keep records of all CIS statements your contractors issue showing the deductions they’ve made.  
• Include these deductions on your Self Assessment tax return to make sure you don’t pay too much tax.

More information: GOV.UK – Business and self-employed (https://www.gov.uk/browse/business).

prompt: I'm running a home-based nursing business and need to know if I need special permission or insurance. What should I check first?
generated_response:
Running nursing services from home involves both health-care rules and general business rules. Start with the legal and safety checks listed below.

1. Regulated activity  
   • If you will provide nursing or personal care in people’s homes, you may need to register as a ‘domiciliary support service’ with Care Inspectorate Wales (CIW). Contact CIW first to confirm whether your exact service must be registered.  
   • You must also hold a current Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) registration.

2. Professional indemnity (mandatory)  
   • By law, every practising nurse must have adequate professional indemnity insurance. Check whether your trade-union or professional body policy is enough for running a business; if not, arrange your own cover.

3. Other insurance to consider  
   • Medical malpractice or clinical negligence cover (often packaged with professional indemnity).  
   • Public liability insurance in case a client, visitor or member of the public is injured or their property is damaged.
