If you do not want to delay, you can tell your employer that you intend to serve as summoned and that only the court can change the dates.

Further guidance:
Jury service – delaying or being excused: https://www.gov.uk/jury-service/delaying-or-being-excused-from-jury-service  
Time off work for jury service: https://www.gov.uk/time-off-work-jury-service

prompt: How can I get help with travel if I have a medical condition that affects my mobility?
generated_response:
If your medical condition makes it hard to get around, several schemes can cut the cost of travel or make it easier.

Blue Badge – parking
• Lets you park closer to where you need to go (for example on-street disabled bays and many pay-and-display bays for free).  
• Apply through your local council in Scotland. You will need medical evidence and a passport-style photo.

National Entitlement Card – free bus travel in Scotland
• If you meet the disability rules (which cover many mobility problems) you can get free travel on almost all local and long-distance buses.  
• Apply at your council service point or online through your council website. Proof of age, address and your medical condition is needed.

Disabled Persons Railcard
• Gives one-third off most rail fares for you and the same discount for one adult travelling with you.  
• Apply online at disabledpersons-railcard.co.uk or by post. You will be asked for evidence of your disability (for example Child Disability Payment or medical letter).

Motability Scheme
• If you receive the higher-rate mobility component of Child Disability Payment (Scotland) or Disability Living Allowance, you can lease a car, powered wheelchair or scooter through Motability instead of taking the cash benefit.  
• Visit motability.co.uk or speak to a participating dealer.

Help with NHS appointment travel
• If your doctor says you cannot use public transport for medical reasons, ask about patient transport services. The hospital or your GP practice can arrange this free service.

College or university travel support
• Disabled Students’ Allowance (DSA) can pay extra travel costs if the journey you have to make is because of your disability. Speak to your student support or SAAS.

Local taxi or community transport schemes
• Some Scottish councils run Taxicard or community minibus services for disabled residents. Check your council’s website or call their customer service line.

If you are not sure which scheme you qualify for, contact:

• Your local council’s blue badge or concessionary travel team.  
• The free Disability Information Scotland helpline: 0300 323 9961.  
• Citizens Advice Bureau for a benefits check and help with forms.

Official links
Blue Badge: https://www.gov.uk/apply-blue-badge  
Disabled Persons Railcard: https://www.disabledpersons-railcard.co.uk  
Driving and mobility help: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

If any application asks for medical evidence you do not have, ask your GP or specialist for a simple supporting letter.

prompt: I'm 65 and considering delaying my pension to get a higher amount. What are the steps I need to take and what factors should I consider?
generated_response:
Deferring (delaying) your State Pension is simple – you normally just don’t claim it when you reach State Pension age – but it’s worth weighing up the pros and cons first.

1. Check your State Pension age and forecast  
   • Use the ‘Check your State Pension forecast’ service on GOV.UK to see the date you can first claim and the weekly amount due at that point.  
   • Confirm you have or will have the full National Insurance record for the maximum rate.

2. Decide how long you might defer  
   • Under the new State Pension rules (for people reaching State Pension age on or after 6 April 2016) your pension rises by about 1% for every 9 weeks you delay – roughly 5.8% for each full year.  
   • You must defer for at least 9 weeks to get any increase.  
   • You can’t take the rise as a lump sum under the new rules – it is added to your regular payments when you eventually claim.

3. Work out the break-even point  
   • Compare the extra you’ll get each week with the income you’ll miss while you are not claiming.  
   • As a rough guide, deferring for one year adds about £11 a week to a full pension (2024/25 figures). It typically takes 16–17 years to earn back the year you skipped, so consider your health and life expectancy.

4. Check how deferring affects tax and benefits  
   • State Pension counts as taxable income, so the increase could push you into a higher tax band later.  
   • While you are deferring, you will not receive that pension income. This may improve or reduce your eligibility for means-tested benefits (for example, Pension Credit, Housing Benefit or Council Tax Reduction).  
   • Once you start drawing the higher pension, it could tip you over the savings or income thresholds for some benefits.

5. Make sure you have money to live on while you wait  
   • Confirm you can cover day-to-day costs from earnings, savings or other pensions during the deferral period.  
   • If you have a workplace or personal pension, ask the provider about your options – you are free to take those while deferring the State Pension, but charges, investment performance and annuity rates may change over time.

6. How to defer in practice  
   • If you have not yet reached State Pension age: simply do nothing when you get the invitation to claim – the pension will automatically defer.  
   • If you have already started receiving it (only possible if you reached State Pension age earlier): call the Pension Service and ask to pause payments. You can only do this once and must stop them for at least 9 weeks.  
   • Keep records of the date you deferred; when you decide to claim, contact the Pension Service and request your pension. The extra amount is then added automatically.

7. Get advice if unsure  
   • Use a free, impartial service such as Pension Wise (for defined contribution pensions) or speak to an independent financial adviser to check the whole-of-retirement picture.  
   • If you receive any means-tested benefit, ask the provider or a welfare rights adviser how stopping your pension might affect your entitlement.

Key GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/working (search within ‘Pensions and retirement planning’ for ‘Deferring your State Pension’ and ‘Check your State Pension forecast’).

If you are not certain deferring is right for you, consider seeking regulated financial advice or contacting the Pension Service before making a final decision.

prompt: I have limited savings and receive benefits. Can I get help with paying court fees?
generated_response:
Yes. If your case is in a Scottish civil court you may not have to pay the fee (this is called a ‘fee exemption’) or you may get a reduction.

You can usually get a full exemption if at least one of these applies:
• You get Income Support, income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance, income-related Employment and Support Allowance, Pension Credit (Guarantee Credit) or Universal Credit and your net monthly earnings are below the current limit.  
• You receive Working Tax Credit and Child Tax Credit and your annual household income shown on the award notice is below the limit.  
• You are receiving civil legal aid or Advice and Assistance for the case.  

If you are not on one of those benefits, you may still qualify if:
• Your gross annual income and your savings (‘capital’) are both under the set thresholds.  
• You can show paying the fee would cause hardship.

How to apply
1. Ask the court counter for the “Application for exemption from paying court fees” form (sometimes called Form FEE2) or download it from the Scottish Courts and Tribunals Service website.  
2. Fill in the form and attach evidence:  
   – a recent benefit award letter or online statement, OR  
   – proof of income (recent payslips, tax credits notice) and details of your savings.  
3. Hand the form and evidence to the sheriff clerk (or Court of Session fees office) when you lodge your claim or before the fee is due.  
4. The clerk will tell you—usually the same day—whether the fee is waived or reduced.  
5. If you are refused and think this is wrong, ask the sheriff clerk to review the decision or speak to a free advice agency.

If your case is in an English or Welsh court, use the “Help with Fees” scheme instead (see GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/get-help-with-court-fees).

Unsure which rules apply or what evidence to provide?  
Contact the court where your case will be heard or a local advice centre (e.g. Citizens Advice Scotland) before you pay anything.

prompt: What happens if my child is receiving DLA for children and we move from Scotland to England or Wales?
generated_response:
Moving from Scotland to England or Wales affects your child’s disability benefit in one of two ways, depending on which benefit they’re getting now.

1. Your child is still on DLA for children (paid by DWP)
• DLA is a Great-Britain benefit, so it can continue after you move.  
• Phone the Disability Living Allowance helpline (0800 121 4600) as soon as you know your new address and the date you’re moving.  
• Payments carry on as normal unless your child’s care or mobility needs change – in that case, tell DWP so the award can be looked at again.

2. Your child has already been transferred to Child Disability Payment (CDP) from Social Security Scotland
• CDP is only payable while you live in Scotland. It can keep being paid for up to 13 weeks after you leave.  
• Tell Social Security Scotland about the move – if you don’t, payments may stop sooner and you could be overpaid.  
• Before the 13-week period ends, make a new claim for DLA for children with DWP. If you claim within those 13 weeks, DLA can usually start from the day after CDP ends, so there’s no gap. Use form DLA1A Child (download or request by calling 0800 121 4600).  

Other things to check
• Update any linked benefits (for example Carer’s Allowance, Universal Credit, free school meals) with your new address and your child’s ongoing award.  
• If you rely on a Motability vehicle, contact Motability to confirm the change in benefit and address.  

Where to get help
• Disability Living Allowance (children) helpline: 0800 121 4600  
• Social Security Scotland helpline (if on CDP): 0800 182 2222  
• GOV.UK guidance on DLA for children: https://www.gov.uk/disability-living-allowance-children  

If you’re unsure whether your child is on DLA or CDP, check the latest award letter or phone the relevant helpline before you move.

prompt: What are the maximum weight limits for parcels sent through the British Forces Post Office (BFPO) and how do they vary depending on where I post from?
generated_response:
Parcels for BFPO addresses all travel through the UK postal network first, but different weight limits apply depending on the service you use and (for some destinations) local operational rules.  

1. Posting at a UK Post Office (friends, family or businesses in the UK)  
   • Royal Mail services: up to 2 kg per parcel.  
     – Use First Class, Special Delivery or Signed For depending on how quickly or securely you need it to arrive.  
   • Parcelforce Worldwide BFPO service: normally up to 30 kg, with a maximum length of 1.5 m.  
     – Some operational locations cut this down to 11 kg or 20 kg for safety and air-transport reasons. The Post Office counter system will flag the lower limit once you give the BFPO number.  

2. Posting from an overseas BFPO location (serving personnel or MOD civilians)  
   • Standard BFPO mail to the UK: up to 2 kg free of charge.  
   • Parcelforce/BFPO parcel service: up to 30 kg unless a local notice sets a lower limit (common in forward operating bases and ships with limited stowage). Check the local BFPO information desk or orders before sending.  

3. Size limits (apply everywhere)  
   • The longest side must not exceed 1.5 m.  
   • Length + girth combined must not exceed 3 m.  

Always check the specific BFPO number before you post. The BFPO location list on GOV.UK shows any reduced weight limits for that address and whether dangerous-goods or seasonal embargoes are in force.  

More information: https://www.gov.uk/bfpo

prompt: I received a refusal for my UK visa and want to appeal. How do I know if I am eligible to appeal and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
Not every visa refusal can be appealed. Start by reading the first page of your refusal letter—under “Right of appeal” it will say either that you can appeal or that you must use another route (usually an administrative review).

If the letter says you may appeal, follow these steps.

1. Note your deadline  
   • 14 days from the date you received the decision if you are in the UK.  
   • 28 days from the date you received the decision if you are outside the UK.  
   Missing the deadline almost always means the tribunal will refuse to accept your case unless there are exceptional reasons.

2. Decide on the type of appeal  
   • “Papers only” (fee £80) – the judge decides from documents.  
   • “With a hearing” (fee £140) – you and any witnesses can speak to the judge.

3. Gather your grounds and evidence  
   • State clearly why the decision is wrong (for example, how it breaches immigration rules or your human-rights/family-life rights).  
   • Collect supporting documents: passports, financial evidence, relationship evidence, correspondence, expert reports, etc. Label and date everything.

4. Submit the appeal to the First-tier Tribunal (Immigration and Asylum Chamber)  
   • Online is quickest: use the “Appeal a visa or immigration decision” service on GOV.UK.  
   • If you cannot apply online, post form IAFT-1 (outside UK) or IAFT-5 (inside UK) to the tribunal with the fee or fee-exemption request.  
   • Send a copy of the appeal to the Home Office address on your refusal letter.

5. After you lodge the appeal  
   • The tribunal will send a timetable telling you when to submit your evidence bundle.  
   • The Home Office will produce its own bundle explaining why it refused.  
   • If you chose a hearing, you will get a date, time and location (in person or by video).

6. Prepare for the hearing (if applicable)  
   • Arrange any witnesses and translate documents if needed.  
   • Take all originals plus copies of your evidence and your appeal bundle.  
   • At the hearing you, your representative (if you have one) and the Home Office Presenting Officer can speak.

7. Get the decision  
   • The judge usually sends a written determination within a few weeks.  
   • If you lose, you may be able to ask for permission to appeal to the Upper Tribunal, but only on a legal error.

If your refusal letter says you have no right of appeal  
• Check whether you can request an administrative review instead (usually 14 days).  
• You can also consider making a fresh application that fixes the reasons for refusal, or, in limited situations, applying for judicial review.

Free or low-cost advice  
Immigration appeals are complex; getting qualified legal advice improves your chances. Look for an adviser registered with the OISC or a solicitor with an immigration and asylum accreditation.

Useful links  
Appeal a visa or immigration decision: https://www.gov.uk/immigration-asylum-tribunal  
Visas and immigration overview: https://www.gov.uk/browse/visas-immigration

prompt: What should I do if I think my employer is discriminating against me because of my disability?
generated_response:
Disability discrimination at work is illegal. You can take these steps to deal with it:

1. Write down what has happened  
   • Dates, times, places and what was said or done.  
   • Keep emails, messages or photos that show the unfair treatment.

2. Talk to someone you trust at work  
   • If you feel safe, speak to your line manager or the HR (personnel) team.  
   • Explain why you believe the behaviour is discrimination and what outcome you want (for example, the behaviour to stop, reasonable adjustments, an apology).

3. Get free, confidential advice  
   • Call the Acas helpline on 0300 123 1100. They will explain your rights and the options you have.  
   • You can also speak to Citizens Advice or a trade union rep if you have one.

4. Raise a formal grievance if the problem is not fixed  
   • Ask for your employer’s grievance procedure and put your complaint in writing.  
   • Keep a copy of everything you send and receive.

5. Consider Acas Early Conciliation  
   • Before you can make an employment tribunal claim, you must contact Acas for Early Conciliation.  
   • Acas will try to help you and your employer reach an agreement without going to tribunal.

6. Employment tribunal (last resort)  
   • You normally have 3 months minus 1 day from the last act of discrimination to start the tribunal process.  
   • Tribunals can order compensation and other remedies if they find discrimination.

7. Look after yourself  
   • Talk to friends, family or a support group about how you feel.  
   • Your GP can help if the situation is affecting your health.

More information and links to your rights at work are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

If you are unsure at any stage, call Acas or Citizens Advice—they can guide you step by step.

prompt: What steps should I take if I want to start a petition to influence government decisions?
generated_response:
You can ask the UK Government or Parliament to act on an issue by starting an online petition.  

1. Check who is responsible  
   • Use GOV.UK to see whether the UK Government, UK Parliament or the Scottish Parliament controls the matter.  
   • If it is devolved to Scotland (for example, education or most health services), use the Scottish Parliament petitions site instead.

2. Create an account  
   • UK-wide issues: go to https://petition.parliament.uk and click ‘Start a petition’.  
   • Scottish issues: go to https://petitions.parliament.scot.  
   • Set up a free account with your name, address and email.

3. Draft the petition  
   • Give it a short, clear title.  
   • Explain the background (what’s happening and why it matters).  
   • State exactly what you want the Government or Parliament to do.  
   • Keep to one issue only, use plain language and avoid anything that could be defamatory or offensive (the site rules set this out).

4. Submit for moderation  
   • When you press ‘Submit’, staff check it meets the rules.  
   • They may suggest small changes.  
   • You’ll get an email when it is accepted or rejected (usually within a week).

5. Find 5 supporters  
   • An accepted petition stays “hidden” until five other UK-based supporters confirm their names and emails.  
   • Send them the confirmation link you receive.

6. Collect signatures  
   • Once the fifth supporter signs, the petition is published and open for six months.  
   • Share the petition link by email or social media—only online signatures on the official site count.

7. What happens next  
   • 10,000 signatures: the UK Government issues a written response.  
   • 100,000 signatures: the House of Commons Petitions Committee will consider it for a parliamentary debate.  
   • You will be told of any response, debate or further action.

8. Keep supporters informed  
   • The petitions website emails signatories whenever there is news (government response, debate date, outcome).

If you are unsure which body is responsible, start by reading the subject guides on GOV.UK or phone your local MP/MSP’s office for advice.

prompt: I need to understand the legal requirements for employing someone in England. What are the key points I should know about contracts of employment and working hours?
generated_response:
Below is a summary of the main legal duties most small employers in England need to meet. 

1. Provide a written statement (the basic contract)
   • You must give every employee a written statement of employment particulars on or before their first day of work.  
   • It must set out at least: job title or brief description, start date, place of work, pay rate and when it’s paid, hours and days of work, holiday entitlement, length of any probation, notice periods, and any benefits or training you provide.  
   • Although a contract can be verbal, the written statement is a legal requirement and forms the core terms of the employment contract.  

2. Confirm any additional terms in writing within two months
   • Things like pension arrangements, disciplinary and grievance rules, and any collective agreements can follow within 2 months of the start date.  

3. Make sure the contract respects statutory rights
   • Employees are always entitled to the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage, statutory holiday (5.6 weeks a year pro-rata), statutory sick pay if eligible, and protection from unfair dismissal after the qualifying period.  
   • You cannot contract out of these rights, even with the employee’s agreement.  

4. Working hours – follow the Working Time Regulations
   • Adult workers (18 +) must not be required to work more than an average of 48 hours a week over a 17-week reference period unless they sign a voluntary opt-out.  
   • They are entitled to:  
     – At least 11 consecutive hours’ rest in every 24 hours.  
     – A 20-minute unpaid rest break if the working day is longer than 6 hours.  
     – At least one day off each week (or two days off in 14).  
   • Night workers must not average more than 8 hours in any 24-hour period and you must offer them free health assessments.  
   • Young workers (16-17) have stricter limits: 8 hours a day, 40 hours a week, no night work after 10 pm (some sector exceptions), and longer rest breaks.  

5. Keep accurate records
   • Keep pay, hours-worked and holiday records for at least two years to show compliance with minimum wage and working-time rules.  

6. Review and update contracts when things change
   • Any change to the terms (for example pay, hours or workplace) should be discussed with the employee and confirmed in writing.  

7. Give employees a staff handbook or policies
   • Disciplinary, grievance, sickness absence and health-and-safety procedures do not have to be in the contract itself, but employees must know where to find them.  

8. Check right to work and arrange PAYE
   • Before the first day, check proof of right to work in the UK.  
   • Register as an employer with HMRC, run PAYE and provide payslips.  

Full guidance and handy templates are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

If you are unsure about any point, consider taking professional HR advice or contacting Acas for free employer support (0300 123 1100).

prompt: I want to open a small business near a protected area in Scotland. What steps do I need to follow to ensure compliance with the regulations?
generated_response:
Opening a business next to a protected area is possible, but you must show that the activity will not harm the site or any protected species. Follow these steps:

1. Identify the designation  
   • Use NatureScot’s online map (‘SiteLink’) to confirm whether the land is an SSSI, National Park, National Scenic Area, Special Area of Conservation (SAC), Special Protection Area (SPA) or within a National Nature Reserve.  
   • Note any protected species records (for example bats, otters, nesting birds).

2. Speak to the local planning authority early  
   • Ask for pre-application advice. Tell them the exact location and type of business.  
   • They will confirm if you need full planning permission, an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) or a Habitats Regulations Appraisal (HRA).

3. Take expert ecological advice  
   • Hire a qualified ecologist to survey the site and prepare any required reports (protected species survey, ecological impact assessment, mitigation plan).  
   • If the survey finds a European-protected species, you may also need a species licence from NatureScot.

4. Consult the regulators  
   • NatureScot: advice on impacts to designated sites and species, and on any licences you may need.  
   • SEPA: permits for discharges, surface-water drainage, waste, noise, or air emissions.  
   • Local authority environmental health: checks on noise, odour, food safety, licensing, and trading standards.

5. Prepare mitigation and management measures  
   • Show how you will avoid or minimise disturbance (for example, restricted working hours, sound barriers, low-impact lighting, waste-water controls).  
   • Include a plan for ongoing monitoring once the business is running.

6. Submit your planning application  
   • Attach all supporting documents: plans, ecological surveys, mitigation measures, drainage strategy, and any other specialist reports.  
   • Pay the fee and keep copies of everything you send.

7. Secure any other business licences  
   • Depending on what you are doing, you might need a food hygiene registration, alcohol licence, waste carrier registration, or signage consent.  
   • Apply well in advance—some licences can take several weeks.

8. Wait for the decision and comply with conditions  
   • The planning authority may set conditions (for example, seasonal restrictions or habitat creation).  
   • You must follow these conditions—breaching them can lead to enforcement action or fines.

9. Keep good records and monitor impacts  
   • Store all permits and licences on site.  
   • Carry out any monitoring required by your planning consent or SEPA licence and keep reports up to date.

If you are unsure at any stage, the safest next step is to ask the local planning department or NatureScot for written advice before you spend money on detailed plans.

Further reading  
Environment and countryside guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/environment-countryside

prompt: If I'm stopped by customs and my items are seized, can I get them back and how do I challenge the decision?
generated_response:
Yes. In many cases you can ask for your goods back or challenge the seizure, but you must act quickly and use the correct procedure.

1. Read the seizure notice  
   Border Force or HMRC should give you a ‘Seizure Information Notice’ (form BOR 156). Keep it safe – it shows the date of seizure and why your goods were taken.

2. Decide which route you want to use  
   a) Challenge the legality (‘condemnation proceedings’)  
      • Write to the address on the notice within 1 month of the seizure date, saying you want to contest it.  
      • The case goes to court (the sheriff court in Scotland). The court decides whether the seizure was lawful.  
   b) Ask for the goods back (‘restoration’)  
      • Write to Border Force or HMRC within 45 days asking for restoration.  
      • Explain why the goods should be returned and include any evidence (for example receipts, proof the items are not prohibited).  
      • If they refuse, you can request an internal review, then appeal to an independent tribunal.

   You can use both routes if you wish, but you must meet each time limit.

3. Pay any duty, VAT or fees if required  
   Even if restoration is agreed, you usually have to pay any outstanding import duty, VAT, storage or handling charges before the goods are released.

4. Know what cannot be returned  
   Prohibited items (for example illegal drugs, offensive weapons, counterfeit goods, endangered species products) will not be restored.

5. Get help if you need it  
   • A solicitor or advice centre can guide you through condemnation proceedings.  
   • For restoration appeals you do not have to use a lawyer, but it can help if the case is complex.

Full guidance is in Notice 12A: ‘What you can do if things are seized by HMRC or Border Force’ at:  
https://www.gov.uk/guidance/notice-12a-what-you-can-do-if-things-are-seized-by-hmrc-or-border-force

If you no longer have the seizure notice, contact Border Force or HMRC as soon as possible, quoting the date and place of seizure, to get the reference number and instructions.

prompt: My marriage ended in England, but I've lost my final order or decree absolute. Can I still apply for a copy, and what steps do I need to follow?
generated_response:
Yes. Even if you have mislaid the original, you can order an official replacement of your final order (formerly called a decree absolute).

How to get a copy

1. Gather the details you know  
   • Your full name (and your ex-partner’s, including any previous names)  
   • The date of the divorce or final order  
   • The name of the court that dealt with the case  
   • The case number, if you still have it (it makes the search cheaper).

2. Decide how to apply  
   a) Online: use the “Get a copy of a decree absolute or final order” service on GOV.UK.  
   b) By post: download form D440 from the same page, fill it in and post it with the fee.

3. Pay the fee  
   • £10 if you can give the court name and case number.  
   • £45 if you do not have the case number (the extra covers a search of the records).  
   You pay by debit/credit card online, or by cheque/postal order if posting the form.

4. Wait for the copy  
   • HM Courts & Tribunals Service will post it to you. Allow at least 2–3 weeks, longer if a search is needed.  
   • If the court needs more information they will contact you.

If you do not know which court handled the divorce

1. Apply online or by post using as much information as you can (both parties’ names and dates).  
2. HMCTS will carry out a wider search; the £45 fee covers this.  
3. If nothing is found you’ll be told what further steps to take.

If your divorce was granted using the new online divorce service (any application started April 2018 onwards), you can also email contactdivorcecase@justice.gov.uk with your names and, if possible, the reference number. They will email back a PDF copy at the same fee.

Useful link  
Get a copy of a decree absolute or final order: https://www.gov.uk/browse/births-deaths-marriages

prompt: What is the process for getting a Disabled Facilities Grant and what kind of evidence do I need to provide?
generated_response:
You apply to your local council for a Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG). The council checks two things:

• that the work is necessary and suitable for your disability  
• that the cost is reasonable and you (or the person you’re applying for) meet the financial rules

Follow these steps.

1. Contact the housing adaptations team at your local authority and say you want to apply for a Disabled Facilities Grant.  
2. The council will arrange for an occupational therapist (OT) or trusted assessor to visit and decide what adaptations are “necessary and appropriate” (for example, a stair-lift, level-access shower, widened doors).  
3. If the OT agrees the work is needed, the council will give you an application pack. You usually need to supply:  
   • the OT’s recommendations (the council adds this)  
   • proof of identity and address for the disabled person  
   • proof of ownership or the landlord’s written permission if you rent  
   • at least two written quotes from contractors for the proposed work  
4. The council carries out a financial assessment (means test) of the disabled person and any spouse/partner. You’ll be asked for:  
   • recent payslips or benefit award letters  
   • bank or savings statements  
   • details of any other income or capital  
   Children’s applications are not means-tested.  
5. Return the signed application form, supporting documents and quotes. The council must give you a written decision as soon as reasonably practical (in Wales, the target is within six months).  
6. If approved, the council either:  
   • pays the contractor directly after work is signed off, or  
   • reimburses you once you submit invoices and compliance certificates.  
   Do not start the work until you get written approval, or you may lose the grant.  

Typical evidence checklist.

• Occupational therapist report (provided after the assessment)  
• Proof of identity (passport, driving licence or birth certificate)  
• Proof of address (utility bill, council tax letter)  
• Proof of ownership or landlord consent letter  
• Two (sometimes three) itemised builders’ quotes  
• Financial documents for the means test (payslips, benefits, bank statements)  
• Planning permission/building regulations approval, if required (the council will tell you)  

If you’re unsure which team handles DFGs in your area, start on GOV.UK and search “Disabled Facilities Grant” with your postcode, or phone your council switchboard.

prompt: What support can I receive while waiting for the outcome of my immigration appeal?
generated_response:
Help available while you wait for an immigration or asylum appeal depends on your status and personal circumstances.

1. Home Office support (asylum cases only)
   • If your appeal is about an asylum claim and you have no income, you can apply for ‘asylum support’.  
     – Section 95 support: weekly cash card (£47.39 per person) and accommodation if you are still an asylum seeker.  
     – Section 4 support: prepaid card (£45/week) and accommodation if your original claim has been refused but your appeal is outstanding.  
   • Apply on form ASF1 or phone the Asylum Support Application line: 0808 801 0503.

2. Local authority help
   • If you have dependent children, ask the children’s services team at your council for support under section 17 of the Children Act 1989 (England and Wales).  
   • If you have serious care or mobility needs, adult social services may provide accommodation or care under the Care Act 2014.  
   • Support is means-tested and you normally need to show you are destitute and have ‘no recourse to public funds’.

3. Legal Aid
   • Most immigration and asylum appeals qualify for legal aid (subject to a merits and means test).  
   • Use the Legal Aid finder or call Civil Legal Advice on 0345 345 4 345 to locate a solicitor.

4. Healthcare
   • Asylum seekers and anyone with an outstanding asylum appeal are entitled to free NHS care.  
   • If you are appealing a different immigration decision, urgent or immediately necessary treatment is still free, but you may be charged for routine care.

5. Permission to work
   • You normally lose the right to work when your previous visa expires.  
   • Asylum appellants can ask the Home Office for permission to work if their claim has been outstanding for 12 months through no fault of their own. Only jobs on the shortage occupation list are allowed.

6. Charities and community support
   • Refugee Council, Red Cross, Migrant Help and local refugee charities can offer small hardship grants, food parcels, travel vouchers and advocacy.  
   • Food banks (find via Trussell Trust) do not ask about immigration status.

7. What you cannot usually claim
   • Mainstream benefits such as Universal Credit, Pension Credit, Housing Benefit or social housing are generally off-limits if you have ‘no recourse to public funds’.

Next steps
A. Work out which category you fall into (asylum or other immigration appeal).  
B. If you think you qualify for asylum support, complete form ASF1 or ask Migrant Help (0808 801 0503) to do it with you.  
C. If you have children or care needs, contact your local council’s social services team and ask for a “section 17” or “Care Act” assessment.  
D. Contact a legal aid solicitor to take on your appeal and, if needed, request emergency support from the court or Home Office.

If you are unsure which route applies, speak to an accredited immigration adviser or solicitor as soon as possible, or check the visas and immigration pages on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/visas-immigration

prompt: I'm looking for an internship in Wales and I've heard about the Graduate Talent Pool. Is it still open and how can I apply if it's not?
generated_response:
Graduate Talent Pool has closed and is no longer accepting new internship listings or applications.  

You can still find paid internships and early-career roles in Wales through other services:

1. Search ‘internship’ or ‘graduate’ on Find a job (https://www.gov.uk/find-a-job). You can filter by location, employer and contract type.  
2. Check Civil Service Jobs (https://www.civilservicejobs.service.gov.uk) for UK-wide internships, including schemes based in Wales.  
3. Visit Careers Wales (https://careerswales.gov.wales) and use its job search plus the “Graduate” and “Work Experience” sections.  
4. Ask your university careers service (even as an alumnus) for access to their vacancy boards and any Wales-specific internship programmes.  
5. Look at GO Wales: Work Experience, a Welsh Government scheme that helps graduates under 25 into short, paid placements—details are on the Careers Wales site.  
6. Set up job alerts on LinkedIn and major job boards using keywords like “intern”, “placement year” and “graduate trainee” together with “Wales” or a specific town or sector.  

If you need more support, contact Careers Wales on 0800 028 4844 or use their webchat—they can review your CV and point you to current internship schemes in your area.

prompt: I'm having trouble with a consumer issue and need advice. Which helpline should I call in Wales?
generated_response:
Call the Citizens Advice consumer helpline.

• English-language line (Wales): 0808 223 1133  
• Welsh-language line: 0808 223 1144  
Both numbers are free from mobiles and landlines, Monday to Friday, 9 am – 5 pm.

Have to hand:
1. A brief summary of the problem and what you’ve done so far (dates, who you spoke to, any paperwork).  
2. Copies or photos of receipts, contracts, emails or letters.  
3. Notes of what outcome you want (refund, repair, replacement, compensation).

The adviser will explain your rights, suggest next steps and—if needed—pass the case to Trading Standards.

More about consumer rights: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

prompt: I'm trying to find new customers for my retail business in Scotland. How can I use great.gov.uk to find overseas buyers and export opportunities?
generated_response:
great.gov.uk is the Department for Business and Trade’s free platform for UK firms that want to sell overseas.  
Here is how to use it to reach new customers and spot export opportunities.

1. Create a free business profile  
   • Go to great.gov.uk and choose ‘Find a buyer’.  
   • Add a short company description, products or services, and high-quality images.  
   • List the countries you can supply to and any industry certifications.  
   Buyers registered on the platform search these profiles, so the more detail you add the better.

2. Search and apply for live export opportunities  
   • Under ‘Export opportunities’ you can filter by sector, country, value and closing date.  
   • Each listing shows what the overseas buyer wants, deadlines and how to submit an expression of interest.  
   • If the opportunity suits you, complete the online form; your profile is sent straight to the buyer.

3. Sign up for email alerts  
   • Use the ‘Get updates’ option to receive new opportunities that match your sector or target markets.  
   • You can change or pause alerts at any time through your account settings.

4. Use the market guides and webinars  
   • The ‘Markets’ section has country overviews, sector demand, tariffs and cultural tips.  
   • Free webinars and virtual trade missions are listed under ‘Events’. These help you meet in-market specialists and buyers.

5. Get 1-to-1 export support in Scotland  
   • From your great.gov.uk dashboard choose ‘Get export support’ and request a call-back.  
   • A local International Trade Adviser can:  
     – review your export plan and pricing  
     – introduce you to overseas posts and trade shows  
     – explain grants such as the Internationalisation Fund (if eligible).

6. Promote your profile outside the platform  
   • Share the unique URL of your great.gov.uk profile on emails, LinkedIn and marketing material so buyers can see verified government information about your business.

If you run into problems or need help refining your offer, call the Department for Business and Trade export helpline on 0300 303 8955 (Monday to Friday).  

More general business guidance is also on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: I need to contact HMRC because I disagree with a tax decision they made. What steps should I take to ensure my concerns are properly addressed?
generated_response:
Follow these steps to challenge an HMRC tax decision and make sure your case is handled properly.

1) Read the decision letter carefully  
   • Note the date on the letter – most appeals or reviews must be requested within 30 days.  
   • Identify exactly what HMRC has decided (for example an assessment, penalty or tax credit award).

2) Check whether you have a formal right of appeal  
   • Most income tax, VAT, PAYE, Self Assessment and penalty decisions can be appealed.  
   • If you are unsure, phone the helpline number on the letter and ask whether the decision is appealable.

3) Gather your evidence  
   • Collect any records, calculations or correspondence that support why you think the decision is wrong.  
   • Keep copies; you may need them later for a tribunal.

4) Ask HMRC for a review or send a written appeal  
   • Use the appeal form or follow the instructions on the decision letter.  
   • State clearly: the decision you are appealing, the reasons you disagree, and the outcome you want.  
   • Sign and date the letter or form and keep a copy.  
   • Send it to the address shown on the decision letter within 30 days (or explain why you are late).

5) Await HMRC’s internal review outcome  
   • HMRC will confirm they have your appeal and usually reply within 45 days.  
   • If they agree with you, they will amend the decision.  
   • If they still disagree, they will send you a ‘review conclusion letter’ explaining your next options.

6) Consider taking the case to the independent tax tribunal  
   • You have a further 30 days from the review conclusion letter to apply to the First-tier Tax Tribunal.  
   • The tribunal is separate from HMRC and will look at the evidence from both sides before making a decision.

7) Use Alternative Dispute Resolution (optional)  
   • For complex cases you can ask HMRC for mediation using Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) before or during a tribunal appeal.

8) Make a service complaint if needed  
   • If you feel HMRC staff have treated you unfairly or caused unreasonable delays, use HMRC’s complaints procedure.  
   • If still unhappy after HMRC’s final response, you can ask the Adjudicator or your MP to refer the complaint to the Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman.

Further information and contact details are on GOV.UK under Tax: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

If you are unsure at any stage, consider getting independent tax advice or contacting a professional body such as Citizens Advice.

prompt: I'm separating from my partner and we have children. What steps should I take to ensure we both provide financial support for our children?
generated_response:
You have three main options for arranging child maintenance (financial support) after you separate. Start with the simplest and move on only if you cannot agree.

1. Work out what the children need  
   • List their regular costs – food, clothing, school travel, clubs, housing, utility bills, etc.  
   • Gather details of both parents’ income and how many nights the children stay with each of you.

2. Try a family-based arrangement first  
   • Use the Child Maintenance Calculator to get a rough weekly figure: https://www.gov.uk/calculate-child-maintenance  
   • Discuss the amount and how it will be paid (for example, a standing order each month).  
   • Put the agreement in writing, date it and both sign. Review it each year or if circumstances change.

3. Consider family mediation if you cannot agree  
   • A mediator is an independent professional who helps you reach a voluntary agreement.  
   • Mediation is usually quicker and cheaper than going straight to a formal service or court.

4. Apply to the Child Maintenance Service (CMS) if needed  
   • Use CMS if a private agreement is impossible or payments stop.  
   • There is a £20 application fee (waived if you have experienced domestic abuse or are under 19).  
   • CMS will calculate the legal amount, tell the paying parent what to pay, and can collect and pass on payments if necessary (collection charges apply).  
   • Start the application online or by phone: https://www.gov.uk/make-child-maintenance-arrangement

5. Keep records and review  
   • Save bank statements or CMS statements showing payments made and received.  
   • Update any agreement if income, overnight care or the children’s needs change.

If you’re unsure which option suits you, call Child Maintenance Options on 0800 953 0191 for free, impartial guidance.

prompt: How can I complain about a service I received that didn't meet my expectations?
generated_response:
Start with the provider’s own complaints procedure first. If that does not sort things out, you can usually take the issue to an independent body.

1. Check the provider’s complaints policy  
   • Look on their website or ask for it in writing.  
   • Note the time-limits for raising a complaint and any information they want (e.g. account number, dates, evidence).

2. Put your complaint in writing  
   • Explain what went wrong, when it happened and how it has affected you.  
   • Say clearly what you want the provider to do (refund, apology, repair, compensation).  
   • Keep copies of emails, letters, receipts, photos or screenshots.

3. Give the provider time to respond  
   • Most organisations aim to reply within 8 weeks (it may be less if their policy says so).  
   • If they phone you, ask for the decision in writing as well.

4. Escalate within the organisation  
   • If the first reply is not satisfactory, ask for it to be reviewed by a senior manager or a second-stage complaints team.  
   • Make it clear why the first outcome did not resolve the problem.

5. Go to an independent body if you are still unhappy  
   The right body depends on the type of service:  
   • Public services in Wales (local council, NHS, housing association, Welsh Government bodies): contact the Public Services Ombudsman for Wales.  
   • Financial products and insurance: Financial Ombudsman Service.  
   • Energy, water or telecoms: their approved Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) scheme.  
   • Package holidays, airlines and other consumer services: look for an ADR scheme listed on GOV.UK or ask Citizens Advice which one covers that sector.  
   Most ombudsmen will only look at your case after you have completed the provider’s full complaints process.

6. Last resort – court or enforcement action  
   • For smaller consumer claims (up to £10,000) you can use the Money Claim Online service in England and Wales.  
   • Seek free advice first from Citizens Advice or a local advice centre.

Useful links  
GOV.UK complaints and justice services: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice  
Public Services Ombudsman for Wales: https://www.ombudsman.wales  

If you’re unsure which ombudsman or ADR scheme is right, contact Citizens Advice Wales on 0800 702 2020 or use their webchat.

prompt: I want to apply for a secondary school place for my child in Wales. What are the key steps I should follow and what information do I need to prepare?
generated_response:
Applying for a secondary school place in Wales is done through your local council. The process is broadly the same across Wales, but dates and forms can vary slightly, so always check your council’s admissions booklet first.

1. Check key dates  
   • Most councils open secondary applications in early September of Year 6 and close them in late October.  
   • Offer day is usually 1 March.  
   • Late applications are considered after on-time ones.

2. Read your council’s admissions booklet  
   • It explains catchment areas, oversubscription criteria and how each school allocates places (distance, siblings, faith, Welsh-medium, additional learning needs, looked-after status, etc.).  
   • Note any schools that require a supplementary form (for example, faith schools).

3. List your preferences  
   • You can normally list at least three schools.  
   • Rank them in genuine order of preference—listing only one does not improve your chances.  
   • Include a catchment school even if it is not your first choice, in case higher preferences are full.

4. Complete the online (or paper) application  
   Information you’ll usually need:  
   • Child’s full name, date of birth and current school  
   • Home address (and how long you’ve lived there)  
   • Parent/guardian contact details  
   • Proof of address – often a recent council tax, utility or benefit statement  
   • Child’s birth certificate or passport number (some councils ask for this)  
   • Details of siblings already at the preferred school, if relevant  
   • Any special circumstances (e.g. looked-after child, medical or social need, additional learning needs)  
   • For faith schools: baptism certificate or minister’s reference, if required

5. Submit before the deadline and keep the confirmation email/receipt.

6. After you get the offer  
   • Accept or decline by the stated date.  
   • If unhappy with the outcome, you can join waiting lists and/or appeal—follow the instructions in the decision letter.  
   • If moving house, tell the admissions team immediately and send new proof of address.

7. Keep copies of everything you send.

If you’re unsure about catchment boundaries, oversubscription rules or what counts as proof of address, contact your council’s school admissions team. You can find their details via GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

prompt: I am a carer in Scotland and receive Carer's Allowance. What happens if I move to England or Wales?
generated_response:
Carer’s Allowance is a UK-wide benefit run by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP), so moving from Scotland to England or Wales does not normally stop the allowance itself. The main changes to think about are below.

1. Tell DWP as soon as you know your new address.  
   • Phone the Carer’s Allowance Unit on 0800 731 0297 (textphone 0800 731 0317) or use the change-of-circumstances form on GOV.UK.  
   • Your payments will carry on if you still look after the same person for at least 35 hours a week and meet the other rules.

2. Carer’s Allowance Supplement is only for people who are resident in Scotland on the set “qualifying dates” (usually in April and October).  
   • Once you live in England or Wales you will not get future Supplement payments.  
   • If you were still living in Scotland on the last qualifying date before you move, you will receive that final Supplement automatically.

3. Check any other devolved benefits or local help you receive (for example, the Scottish Council Tax Reduction scheme or Best Start grants). You may need to apply for the English or Welsh equivalents once you move.

4. If you also get Universal Credit, Pension Credit or another means-tested benefit, report the move to those offices as well. A change of rent, council tax or household can alter the amount you are paid.

5. Keep proof that you still meet the caring criteria (for example, a letter from the person you care for or their GP) in case DWP asks for an update.

If you have doubts about how the move affects your wider benefits or tax position, contact an advice charity (such as Citizens Advice) before relocating.

Find more information or report changes here: https://www.gov.uk/carers-allowance

prompt: I am in Northern Ireland and need to change the details on my Blue Badge. How is this process different from the rest of the UK?
generated_response:
In Northern Ireland the Blue Badge scheme is run centrally by the Department for Infrastructure’s Blue Badge Unit, not by local councils. Because of that, changing your details works a little differently from England, Scotland and Wales.

What to do in Northern Ireland  
1. Phone or email the Blue Badge Unit and tell them what needs changing (for example a new address, name or vehicle registration).  
   • Telephone: 0300 200 7818  
   • Email: blue.badge@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk  
2. They will send you a short amendment / replacement form or give you the link to complete it online via nidirect.  
3. Return the form with any proof they ask for (for example proof of your new address or a copy of your deed-poll if you have changed name).  
4. If the change means you need a new badge (for example a name change or your badge has been damaged), you pay a £10 replacement fee.  
5. The Blue Badge Unit issues the new badge and cancels the old one. Allow about 2 weeks for it to arrive.

How this differs from the rest of the UK  
• England and Wales – you contact your local council (not a central unit). The council can charge up to £10 for a replacement.  
• Scotland – you also go through your local council, but the replacement fee is up to £20.  
• Online GOV.UK “Change a Blue Badge” services only work for Great Britain. They will redirect you to your council; they will not process Northern Ireland badges.  

If you are unsure which form to use or what evidence to send, call the Blue Badge Unit first. For general information on Blue Badges, you can read the driving and transport guidance on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I need to find contact details for a lost pension. How do I use the online service to find this information?
generated_response:
You can use the free Pension Tracing Service to get the current contact details for a workplace or personal pension you have lost track of.

Online steps

1. Go to GOV-UK and search for ‘Find pension contact details’, or type this direct address into your browser: https://www.gov.uk/find-pension-contact-details

2. Select ‘Start now’.

3. The service will ask whether the pension was:
   • a workplace pension (through an employer)  
   • a personal or stakeholder pension you arranged yourself  
   • a civil service, NHS or other public-sector scheme  

   Choose the option that fits.

4. Enter any details you remember. The more you can give, the easier the search:
   • the name of the employer or pension scheme  
   • the employer’s old or new trading name, if it has changed  
   • the employer’s address or postcode  
   • approximate dates you worked there or paid in  

5. Review the results page. It will list the most likely pension schemes and give the latest:
   • scheme name  
   • postal address  
   • phone number and, if available, email or website  

6. Make a note of those details. Contact the scheme directly and ask for:
   • confirmation you have benefits with them  
   • the value of your pension and how to access it in future  

If you prefer to speak to someone

• Phone the Pension Tracing Service on 0800 731 0193 (Monday to Friday, 8 am – 6 pm).  
• They can run the same search and post or email the contact details to you.

What the service cannot do

• It does not tell you the value of your pension or hold your money.  
• You will still need to contact the scheme administrator to get statements or make any changes.

If the online search returns no match, try variations of the employer’s name, old addresses, or call the helpline for extra help.

prompt: As a teacher with a child under 16, can I get help with childcare costs and how do I apply for it?
generated_response:
Several government schemes can help with childcare costs for a child under 16. Which one suits you depends on your child’s age and your circumstances.  

Main options  

1. Tax-Free Childcare  
   • Available for children up to 11 (17 if disabled).  
   • For every £8 you pay into an online childcare account, the government adds £2 (up to £2,000 a year per child, £4,000 if disabled).  
   • You and any partner must each earn at least £167 a week on average and no more than £100,000 a year.  
   How to apply  
   a. Go to GOV.UK and search “Tax-Free Childcare” or start from https://www.gov.uk/browse/childcare-parenting.  
   b. Set up or sign in with a Government Gateway ID.  
   c. Open a childcare account, add money and pay your registered childcare provider from it.  
   If you prefer to speak to someone, call the Childcare Service helpline on 0300 123 4097.

2. Free childcare hours for 3- and 4-year-olds  
   • All families get 15 hours a week during term time.  
   • Working parents who meet the same earnings limits as Tax-Free Childcare can get up to 30 hours.  
   How to apply  
   a. Apply online through the same childcare service account as above.  
   b. Give your 11-digit code to your childcare provider or school nursery each term so they can claim the funding.

3. Universal Credit childcare (only if you claim Universal Credit)  
   • Pays back up to 85% of childcare costs, capped at £951 a month for one child (£1,630 for two).  
   • You claim the cost after you have paid the provider.  
   Apply by updating your Universal Credit journal or phoning the UC helpline.

4. Childcare Vouchers or Salary-sacrifice schemes  
   • Closed to new joiners since 2018, but if your school still runs a scheme and you are already in it you can keep using it while you stay with the same employer and scheme.

Which scheme is best?  
• You cannot use Tax-Free Childcare and Childcare Vouchers or UC childcare for the same costs, so compare which gives you the higher saving.  
• Free hours can be combined with either Tax-Free Childcare or Universal Credit support for any extra hours.

Quick next steps  

1. Check your eligibility using the childcare calculator on GOV.UK (find it under the link above).  
2. If Tax-Free Childcare or 30-hour free childcare looks right:  
   • Gather your National Insurance number, your child’s birth certificate details and, if you have one, your partner’s details.  
   • Apply online or phone 0300 123 4097 for help.  
3. Once approved, reconfirm your details online every three months to keep getting the support.

If anything is unclear, or you run into difficulties online, call the Childcare Service helpline. Staff can guide you through the application or complete parts of it with you over the phone.

prompt: Where can I find the nearest postcode finder tool to locate my area?
generated_response:
Use Royal Mail’s free online postcode finder:

1. Go to Royal Mail’s Postcode Finder: https://www.royalmail.com/find-a-postcode  
2. Type the first line of your address (for example ‘10 High Street’) and your town or city.  
3. Click ‘Find postcode’. The tool will show the full postcode and a list of matching addresses.  
4. Select the correct address to copy or note down the postcode.

If you do not have internet access, you can:
• Ring Royal Mail customer services on 0345 774 0740 and ask for a postcode lookup.  
• Ask at your local post office—staff can check the postcode database for you.

That is the quickest way to confirm the postcode for any UK address.

prompt: If I have a British passport, do I still need to apply for an ETA when visiting the UK?

I'm 68 and live in Scotland. I've been receiving Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for many years. What changes will happen to my benefit and how do I apply for the new Scottish Adult Disability Living Allowance?
generated_response:
In Scotland, Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for adults is being replaced by Adult Disability Payment (ADP). If you already get DLA:

1. You do not need to make a new claim. Your award will be moved automatically from DLA (paid by the Department for Work and Pensions) to Adult Disability Payment (paid by Social Security Scotland).  
2. DWP will write to tell you when your case is about to transfer. Social Security Scotland will then confirm the move. There will be no break in your payments and the amount you receive will stay the same at the point of transfer.  
3. You will not be asked to attend a face-to-face assessment just because of the transfer. Social Security Scotland may ask for more information by phone or post if something in your file is unclear.  
4. Keep your details up to date. Continue to tell DWP about any changes in your circumstances until you get the letter confirming you have moved to Adult Disability Payment. After that, report changes to Social Security Scotland instead.  
5. If your care or mobility needs increase before you are moved, you can still ask DWP for your DLA to be looked at again. Any award you have on the day you transfer will be matched in Adult Disability Payment.  

There is no separate application form for a “Scottish Adult Disability Living Allowance”; the new benefit is called Adult Disability Payment and the move is managed for you.

For general information on disability benefits, see GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

If you have questions about the transfer timetable or your own case, you can:

• Call the DWP Disability Service Centre on 0800 121 4600 (textphone 0800 121 4523).  
• Once you receive a letter from Social Security Scotland, contact their helpline on 0800 182 2222.

If you receive any letter you’re unsure about, contact one of those helplines or an accredited advice service (such as Citizens Advice Scotland) before taking action.

prompt: I'm a nurse in Wales and I work on a farm. Do I have the right to paid holiday and sick pay as an agricultural worker?
generated_response:
Yes. Agricultural workers in Wales have legal rights to both paid annual leave and agricultural sick pay, as well as the standard Statutory Sick Pay (SSP).

1. Paid holiday  
   • You must receive at least 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday a year (the legal minimum for all employees).  
   • The Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order also gives most agricultural workers extra entitlement after the first year with the same employer. Check your contract or payslips to see how many days you have built up.

2. Sick pay  
   • After 52 weeks’ continuous service with the same farm you are normally entitled to Agricultural Sick Pay (ASP). This is paid at your normal basic rate for up to 26 weeks in any 12-month period, as long as you follow your employer’s sickness reporting rules.  
   • If you have not yet worked a full year, you may still qualify for Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) if you earn at least £123 a week and have been off sick for 4 or more days in a row.

What to do next  
1) Ask your employer (the farm) for a written statement of your holiday balance and their sick-pay policy.  
2) Compare it with the Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order or seek advice if anything is missing.  
3) If there is a disagreement, raise it informally first, then use your employer’s grievance procedure.  
4) For free help you can call Acas (0300 123 1100) or speak to a trade union or advice centre.

Further information  
GOV.UK overview on employing people: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

If you are unsure about your exact entitlement, check the latest Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order on the Welsh Government website or contact Acas.

prompt: I'm expecting my first child and I live in Scotland. When should I apply for the Pregnancy and Baby Payment?
generated_response:
You can apply once you are 24 weeks pregnant and you have until the day your baby turns 6 months old.

To make sure you do not miss the deadline, Social Security Scotland suggests applying as soon as you reach 24 weeks.

How to apply
1. Online: use the Best Start Grant and Best Start Foods form on mygov.scot (or ask someone you trust to help you fill it in).  
2. Phone: call Social Security Scotland free on 0800 182 2222 and apply over the phone or ask for a paper form.  
3. Paper form: return the completed form by post in the prepaid envelope provided.

What you will need
• National Insurance number (and your partner’s if you have one).  
• Proof of pregnancy or birth (for example, a birth certificate or MATB1 form).  
• Details of the bank or credit union account you want the payment paid into.  
• Information about any benefits or tax credits you receive, as the payment is aimed at low-income families.

If you have adopted or taken over the care of a baby under one year old, you can apply up to 6 months after the child was placed with you.

If you are close to the 6-month deadline and are unsure whether you qualify, apply anyway or call Social Security Scotland for guidance. Missing the deadline usually means you lose the chance to claim.

For a general overview of benefits, you can also visit: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I need to know if I have to get an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) for my small shop that's under 50 square metres and not used for residential purposes. What are the rules around this?
generated_response:
You usually need an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) when you sell, rent out or build a commercial property.  
However, there is a specific exemption that often applies to very small shops.

1. No EPC is needed if the building is:  
   • a stand-alone (detached) commercial building, and  
   • has a total useful floor area of less than 50 square metres, and  
   • is not used as a dwelling.

2. If the shop shares a wall or roof with another building (for example, it is in a parade of shops or forms part of a larger structure), it is not “stand-alone”. In that case you must have a valid EPC if you intend to sell or let the premises.

3. An EPC is not required simply for continuing to trade; the duty only arises on sale, letting or construction.

4. If you later extend the shop so the usable floor area reaches 50 m² or more, the exemption falls away and an EPC will be needed at the next sale or letting.

Check the official guidance on GOV.UK if your situation is borderline or you are unsure:
https://www.gov.uk/energy-performance-certificate-commercial-property

If you remain uncertain, contact your local authority’s building control team or a qualified non-domestic energy assessor before marketing the property.

prompt: How can I apply for a disabled person's bus pass in England?
generated_response:
You apply through your local council. The process is mostly the same across England, but the form and evidence they ask for can differ.

1. Check you’re eligible. You usually qualify if you:
   • are blind or severely sight-impaired  
   • are profoundly or severely deaf  
   • are without speech  
   • have a disability that makes it hard to walk (e.g. you get the higher rate mobility component of DLA, PIP enhanced rate for mobility, or War Pensioners’ Mobility Supplement)  
   • do not have arms or can’t use them  
   • have a learning disability  
   • would be refused a driving licence on medical grounds (other than for alcohol or drugs)

2. Go to the GOV.UK page Apply for a disabled person’s bus pass and enter your postcode. It will link you to the right council webpage.  
   https://www.gov.uk/apply-for-disabled-bus-pass

3. Gather the documents your council asks for, typically:  
   • proof of disability (award letter, consultant letter, etc.)  
   • proof of age and identity (passport, birth certificate, driving licence)  
   • proof of address (utility bill, council tax bill, bank statement)

4. Apply:  
   • Online – upload scans or photos of your documents.  
   • By post – print the form, attach copies, and send it to the address given.  
   • In person – some councils let you apply at a library or customer service centre.

5. Keep the originals. The council may ask to see them later.

6. You’ll normally get a decision within a few weeks. If approved, the pass is posted to you or you collect it in person.

Renewals: most passes last up to 5 years. Follow your council’s renewal reminder and send updated proof if asked.

If you actually live in Scotland, you’ll need the separate National Entitlement Card instead—apply through your local Scottish council.

prompt: Is there a way to get a parking permit without going through my local council?
generated_response:
For parking on public roads or in council-run car parks, the only lawful way to get a resident or visitor permit is through the local council that manages the parking zone. No private company or national body can issue a permit that overrides council controls.

Other options, if you want to avoid applying for a council permit:

1. Use private parking. Some workplaces, rail stations, supermarkets, landlords and private car parks issue their own permits. These are valid only on their land, not on the street.
2. Rent a driveway or space from a private owner (for example, via a driveway-letting website). Again, this gives permission only for that private space.
3. Check whether nearby streets are un-restricted. If a street has no controlled parking zone (CPZ) signs, you can usually park there without a permit—but always read local signs to make sure.

If you need long-term on-street or council-car-park parking close to home, you will have to apply to your council.

More about local parking services: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I have a mental health condition and need to modify my home for better accessibility. What types of adaptations can I get funding for through a Disabled Facilities Grant?
generated_response:
A Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) does not apply in Scotland. Instead, Scottish councils fund essential adaptations through their “Scheme of Assistance”. The rules and paperwork are very similar to a DFG and the same kinds of work are normally covered.

Typical adaptations that can be funded when the need is linked to a mental-health condition include:

• Making it easier to get in and out of your home – ramps, handrails, shallow steps, widened doorways, automatic door openers.  
• Creating a calmer or safer living environment – improved lighting, sound-proofing, window restrictors, reinforced or self-closing doors, video/remote door entry systems, secure outdoor space.  
• Changes inside the home so you can reach and use every key room – stairlifts, through-floor lifts, widened internal doors, relocating a bedroom or living room to the ground floor.  
• Bathroom adaptations that reduce stress or risk – level-access shower, bath hoist, wash-dry WC, non-slip flooring, colour-contrasting fittings for way-finding.  
• Kitchen changes to help with daily living – lowered worktops, simple-control taps and hobs, automatic shut-off devices, brighter task lighting.  
• Heating, ventilation or sensory controls – easy-use thermostats, quiet extractor fans, blackout blinds, dimmable or circadian-lighting systems.  
• Assistive technology and telecare – smoke or flood alarms that alert a carer, fall sensors, “safe room” monitors.

What is unlikely to be funded: purely decorative work, general home repairs, or extensions that are not essential for disability-related access.

How to apply in Scotland

1. Contact your local council’s social work or housing adaptations team and ask for an occupational therapy (OT) assessment.  
2. The OT visits, discusses how your mental-health condition affects daily tasks and recommends adaptations.  
3. If adaptations are agreed, the council sends you a grant application pack and tells you what costs they can cover.  
   • If you own your home, the grant is usually means-tested.  
   • If you rent (private or social), the work may be funded in full.  
4. Get at least one quote (the council may have approved contractors) and submit it with the forms.  
5. Wait for the council’s written approval before any work starts—otherwise it may not be paid for.  
6. When the work is finished, the council pays the contractor or reimburses you, depending on local policy.

If you are uncertain what might help or the council turns down something you think is essential, ask the OT to review the recommendation or seek independent advice (for example from Citizens Advice Scotland).

More about disability benefits and support: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I have a terminal illness and need to make my home more accessible. What can a Disabled Facilities Grant cover?
generated_response:
A Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) helps pay for changes at home so you can move around safely and live as independently as possible.

What the grant can usually cover  
1. Installing ramps, widened doorways or a step-free entrance.  
2. Fitting a stairlift or through-floor lift.  
3. Converting or adapting a bathroom (for example, a level-access shower, specialist bath or lower basin).  
4. Adapting the kitchen – such as lowering worktops, sinks or cupboards.  
5. Improving or relocating heating controls, power points and light switches so they’re easier to reach.  
6. Adding grab rails, handrails or other fixed safety equipment.  
7. Making space for a wheelchair to turn or for medical equipment.  
8. Adapting or building a downstairs bedroom or bathroom if needed.  
9. Improving lighting, ventilation or heating where this is part of the adaptation.  
10. Professional fees linked to the works (surveyor, architect, planning permission).

Key points to know  
• If you are under 18, the grant is not means-tested.  
• Your local council must agree the works are necessary, suitable and reasonable for the property.  
• The council can arrange the work or let you choose your own contractor, but you must wait for written approval before starting.  
• Tell the council you have a terminal illness: they can often fast-track the assessment and prioritise urgent adaptations.

Next steps  
1. Contact the housing or adaptations team at your local council and ask for a Disabled Facilities Grant assessment.  
2. An occupational therapist will usually visit to discuss what you need and recommend specific adaptations.  
3. Get at least two quotes if the council asks you to arrange builders.  
4. Wait for the council’s written grant approval before work begins.  
5. Keep all paperwork and receipts until the council confirms the grant is settled.

If you need help with the process or want independent advice, speak to a local disabled persons’ organisation or Citizens Advice.

Full guidance: https://www.gov.uk/disabled-facilities-grants

prompt: I'm a student in Scotland and I need to apply for student finance. How do I apply online and what should I do if I can't use a computer?
generated_response:
Student funding for Scottish higher-education students is handled by the Student Awards Agency Scotland (SAAS).

Applying online  
1. Go to saas.gov.uk and select “Apply for funding”.  
2. Create an online account or sign in if you already have one.  
3. Fill in the application form. You will need:  
   • your course and university/college details  
   • National Insurance number  
   • household income figures if you are applying for income-assessed support  
   • bank account details for payments  
4. Upload or post any evidence SAAS asks for (for example, proof of identity or income).  
5. Submit the form. Apply before 30 June if you want your funding in place for the start of term; you can still apply later, up to nine months after your course starts.  
6. Check your SAAS account and email for updates or requests for more information.

If you cannot use a computer  
• Phone SAAS on 0300 555 0505 and ask for a paper application pack.  
• You can also get help completing the form at:  
  – your university or college’s student finance/registry office  
  – a local Citizens Advice Bureau  
  – most public libraries, where staff can help you get online or print the form.  
• If you have accessibility needs (for example, large-print or braille forms), tell SAAS when you call and they will provide a suitable format.

Keep copies of any forms and evidence you send.

For general education and funding links, you can also start at GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

prompt: I'm a teacher and I've noticed some students with disabilities aren't getting the support they need in class. What steps should I take to ensure they're treated fairly and have access to the resources they require?
generated_response:
All pupils who have a disability, special educational needs or both are entitled to “reasonable adjustments” and the right support.  
Here is a practical way to tackle the problem.

1) Record what you have noticed  
   • Keep notes of the barriers each pupil faces (for example, inaccessible worksheets, lack of assistive tech, support staff not available).  
   • Gather examples of work or behaviour that show the gap in access.

2) Speak to your school’s ALNCo/SENCo (Additional Learning Needs / Special Educational Needs Coordinator)  
   • Share your evidence and ask whether each pupil already has an Individual Development Plan (IDP) or an Education, Health and Care Plan (EHCP if they live in England).  
   • Check that agreed adjustments (e.g. enlarged print, a reader, seating changes) are written down and being delivered.

3) Involve the pupil and their parents or carers  
   • Explain what you have observed and invite their views.  
   • Ask what has worked previously and what still needs to change.  
   • Make sure their wishes are recorded in any plan.

4) Agree specific, reasonable adjustments  
   Examples include:  
   • providing resources in alternative formats  
   • assistive technology or software  
   • extra time or a quiet space for tasks  
   • in-class support from a teaching assistant  
   • changes to the classroom layout or timetable.  
   Write the adjustments into the pupil’s IDP and add review dates.

5) Check resources and training  
   • Confirm the school budget or ALN funding can cover equipment or hours for support staff.  
   • If training is needed (for example in using a communication aid), arrange it through the ALNCo or local authority inclusion service.

6) Monitor and review  
   • Track each pupil’s progress against their targets.  
   • Review the IDP at least once a term, sooner if the support is not working.  
   • Update the plan and adjustments as needed.

7) Escalate if support is still inadequate  
   • Raise the issue with your headteacher in writing.  
   • If unresolved, follow the school’s complaints policy and involve the governing body.  
   • The local authority has a duty under the Additional Learning Needs and Education Tribunal (Wales) Act 2018 to secure suitable provision; you can request their review.  
   • Independent disagreement resolution and appeal to the Education Tribunal for Wales are available if necessary.

Key duties to cite  
• Equality Act 2010 – schools must make reasonable adjustments so disabled pupils are not put at a substantial disadvantage.  
• Additional Learning Needs and Education Tribunal (Wales) Act 2018 – gives pupils with ALN the right to an IDP and suitable provision.

Further guidance  
Special educational needs and disability (SEND) – GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

If you need more detailed advice, contact your local authority’s inclusion service or a specialist charity such as SNAP Cymru.

prompt: I'm worried about an older person in a care home because they seem unhappy and aren't getting proper care. What should I do to report this and ensure they're protected?
generated_response:
If you think the person is in immediate danger or serious harm, call 999.

Otherwise, follow these steps.

1. Raise the issue with the care home manager as soon as you can. Explain clearly what you have seen or heard.  
   • Ask what action they will take and when.  
   • Make a note of the date, time and what was said.

2. Contact the local council’s adult safeguarding team if you are not satisfied or feel unable to speak to the home.  
   • Search “[name of council] adult safeguarding” online or phone the council’s main switchboard and ask for Safeguarding Adults.  
   • Give as much detail as you can (name of the care home, what happened, dates, people involved).
